```mermaid
    monitorBlock -.Feedback.-> planBlock
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

> **Define DevOps. List any four reasons why traditional software development models fail to meet modern delivery demands.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
DevOps is a cultural, philosophical, and technical movement that integrates software development (Dev) and IT operations (Ops) teams to enable continuous collaboration, automation, and rapid delivery of high-quality software throughout the SDLC.

**Four Reasons (0.375 Marks Each):**

1. **Siloed Teams:** Development and Operations work in isolation, causing a "wall of confusion" and blame culture.
2. **Manual Processes:** Deployment and infrastructure setup are manual, leading to inconsistencies and errors.
3. **Late Feedback:** Defects and customer feedback are detected late, making fixes exponentially costlier.
4. **Infrequent Releases:** High deployment risk forces teams to bundle changes into big-bang releases, increasing failure blast radius and debugging complexity.

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`

> **Explain the CALMS framework of DevOps in brief.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **CALMS framework**, proposed by Damon Edwards, encapsulates the five pillars of DevOps:

- **C — Culture (0.6 Marks):** Foster shared responsibility, psychological safety, and blameless learning between Dev and Ops.
- **A — Automation (0.6 Marks):** Automate builds, tests, deployments, and infrastructure provisioning using CI/CD and IaC.
- **L — Lean (0.6 Marks):** Apply Lean principles to eliminate waste, amplify learning, and deliver value continuously.
- **M — Measurement (0.6 Marks):** Track DORA metrics like deployment frequency, lead time, MTTR, and change failure rate.
- **S — Sharing (0.6 Marks):** Encourage knowledge sharing across teams via documentation, dashboards, and ChatOps.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme requires internal choice within the module. Both options below are equally weighted and cover Module 2 content.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

> **(a)** Define DevOps in detail. Explain the eight phases of the DevOps lifecycle with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the problems faced by traditional SDLC models and explain how DevOps addresses each of these problems. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
DevOps is a collaborative methodology that unifies software development and IT operations through cultural change, automation, and continuous feedback. It aims to deliver software with high velocity, reliability, and quality by breaking down the silos between teams who build software and teams who run it.

**Eight Phases of DevOps Lifecycle (5 Marks — 0.625 per phase):**

| Phase | Activity | Example Tool |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Plan | Define requirements, user stories, sprint goals | Jira, Azure Boards |
| 2. Code | Write and version-control source code | Git, GitHub |
| 3. Build | Compile code, package artifacts | Maven, Gradle |
| 4. Test | Run automated unit, integration, and security tests | Selenium, JUnit |
| 5. Release | Store and version artifacts | JFrog Artifactory |
| 6. Deploy | Push artifacts to production automatically | Jenkins, ArgoCD |
| 7. Operate | Provision and manage infrastructure | Terraform, Kubernetes |
| 8. Monitor | Observe systems and collect telemetry | Prometheus, Grafana |

These phases form an **infinity loop** — monitoring insights feed back into planning, creating a continuous improvement cycle.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Problems in Traditional SDLC (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Waterfall rigidity:** Sequential phases mean changes are costly; feedback arrives too late.
2. **Siloed teams:** Dev and Ops have conflicting goals, leading to blame culture.
3. **Manual deployments:** Human errors cause downtime; releases are infrequent and risky.
4. **Late defect detection:** Bugs found in production are 100x more expensive than those found during development.
5. **Poor visibility:** No shared metrics between Dev and Ops; problems are discovered reactively.
6. **Inefficient infrastructure:** Over-provisioned, rigid servers waste resources.

**How DevOps Solves These (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Agile + Continuous Delivery:** Replaces rigid Waterfall with iterative, feedback-driven cycles.
2. **Culture of shared responsibility:** Dev and Ops collaborate from day one; blameless post-mortems replace blame.
3. **CI/CD pipelines:** Automate build, test, and deployment for repeatability and speed.
4. **Shift-left testing:** Automated tests run on every commit, catching defects early.
5. **DORA metrics:** Deployment frequency, lead time, MTTR, and CFR provide shared visibility.
6. **Infrastructure as Code (IaC):** Terraform and Kubernetes enable elastic, reproducible infrastructure.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative]`

> **(a)** Explain the "Wall of Confusion" between Development and Operations teams. How does DevOps break down this wall? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the DORA metrics used to measure DevOps performance. Explain how each metric improves software delivery. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**The Wall of Confusion (3.5 Marks):**
The "Wall of Confusion" is a metaphorical barrier between Development and Operations teams that arises from conflicting objectives. Developers aim to ship features quickly, while Operations aims to maintain production stability. This conflict causes:

- Developers throwing code "over the wall" without operational considerations.
- Operations rejecting deployments as unstable or undocumented.
- Blame culture after incidents, with each team accusing the other.
- Slow, infrequent releases bundled with high-risk changes.
- Poor communication via ticketing systems rather than collaboration.

**How DevOps Breaks Down the Wall (3.5 Marks):**
DevOps replaces the wall with a **bridge of shared responsibility:**

- **Cultural shift:** Dev and Ops share goals, KPIs, and on-call responsibilities.
- **Shared tooling:** Common platforms like GitHub, Jenkins, and Kubernetes replace fragmented tools.
- **Continuous feedback:** Monitoring data flows back to developers, who fix issues proactively.
- **Blameless post-mortems:** Incidents are analyzed for systemic causes, not individual blame.
- **Automated pipelines:** Code flows smoothly from commit to production without manual handoffs.
- **"You build it, you run it":** Developers take ownership of their code in production, fostering empathy for operational realities.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**DORA Metrics — Overview (1 Mark):**
The DORA (DevOps Research and Assessment) metrics, developed by Google, are the four key indicators of DevOps performance.

**The Four Metrics (4 Marks — 1 Each):**

1. **Deployment Frequency (DF):**
   - **Definition:** Number of deployments to production per unit time.
   - **Improvement:** Higher DF indicates faster delivery. Elite performers deploy multiple times per day.

2. **Lead Time for Changes (LT):**
   - **Definition:** Time elapsed from code commit to successful production deployment.
   - **Improvement:** Shorter LT means quicker feedback incorporation. Elite performers achieve LT less than one hour.

3. **Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR):**
   - **Definition:** Average time required to restore service after a production incident.
   - **Improvement:** Lower MTTR indicates better resilience. Elite performers recover in less than one hour.

4. **Change Failure Rate (CFR):**
   - **Definition:** Percentage of deployments that cause a production failure.
   - **Improvement:** Lower CFR means higher quality releases. Elite performers maintain CFR between 0% and 15%.

**Impact on Software Delivery (2 Marks):**
Together, these metrics balance **speed** (DF, LT) and **stability** (MTTR, CFR). High-performing DevOps teams optimize all four, achieving both rapid delivery and high reliability — a combination impossible in traditional models.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls — Definition and Need for DevOps:**
>
> 1. **Defining DevOps only as a toolset (e.g., "DevOps is Jenkins + Docker"):** Examiners deduct up to **1.5 marks** because DevOps is fundamentally a *culture and philosophy*, not just tools.
>
> 2. **Conflating DevOps with Agile:** Many students write "DevOps is the same as Agile." This is factually wrong. Agile focuses on development; DevOps extends Agile to operations. Lose **1 mark** for this error.
>
> 3. **Missing the "Why":** When explaining the *need* for DevOps, students often list tools without explaining the **problems** DevOps solves. Always state the **problem → solution** mapping. Missing this loses **2 marks** in 14-mark questions.
>
> 4. **Skipping DORA Metrics:** In any question about measuring DevOps success, omitting the four DORA metrics is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.
>
> 5. **Incorrect Lifecycle Order:** Writing the DevOps lifecycle out of order (e.g., Deploy before Test) signals conceptual confusion. Memorize the order: **Plan → Code → Build → Test → Release → Deploy → Operate → Monitor**.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning continuous feedback:** Any explanation of DevOps without emphasizing the **continuous feedback loop** is incomplete. Examiners look for this keyword.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Definition and Need for DevOps**

- [x] **DevOps** = **Dev**elopment + **Ope**rations; a culture, not just tools.
- [x] Coined in **2009** by **Patrick Debois** at the first **DevOpsDays** in Ghent, Belgium.
- [x] DevOps **extends Agile** beyond development into deployment and operations.
- [x] The **Wall of Confusion** between Dev and Ops is the primary problem DevOps solves.
- [x] **Six core problems** of traditional SDLC: siloed teams, manual processes, late feedback, infrequent releases, inefficient infrastructure, resistance to change.
- [x] **CALMS Framework:** Culture, Automation, Lean, Measurement, Sharing.
- [x] **CAMS Framework:** Culture, Automation, Measurement, Sharing (simplified version).
- [x] **The Three Ways:** Flow (Systems Thinking), Feedback (Amplify Loops), Continuous Learning (Culture of Experimentation).
- [x] **DevOps Lifecycle (8 Phases):** Plan → Code → Build → Test → Release → Deploy → Operate → Monitor (infinity loop).
- [x] **DORA Metrics (4 Key Indicators):** Deployment Frequency, Lead Time for Changes, Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR), Change Failure Rate (CFR).
- [x] **Cycle Time Formula:** $CT_{total} = \sum T_{phase}$ across all eight lifecycle phases.
- [x] **Change Failure Rate Formula:** $CFR = \frac{Failures}{Total\ Deployments} \times 100\%$.
- [x] **Availability Formula:** $A = \frac{Uptime}{Uptime + Downtime} \times 100\%$.
- [x] DevOps is **not a job role** — it is a team-wide mindset and culture.
- [x] DevOps is **not a replacement for Agile** — it is a **complementary extension**.
- [x] **Business impact:** Amazon deploys code every 11.7 seconds on average — only possible via DevOps.
- [x] **Shift-Left Testing:** Catching defects early via automated testing on every commit is 100x cheaper than fixing in production.
- [x] **Infrastructure as Code (IaC):** Terraform, Ansible, CloudFormation enable reproducible, version-controlled infrastructure.
- [x] **Blameless Post-Mortems:** Learning from failures without assigning individual blame is a cornerstone of DevOps culture.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DevOps Lifecycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT742 - SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT WITH AGILE AND DEVOPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DevOps Lifecycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# DevOps Lifecycle — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **DevOps Lifecycle** is the **continuous, iterative, and automated end-to-end flow** of activities that an organization executes to deliver, deploy, monitor, and improve software. It unifies **Development (Dev)** and **Operations (Ops)** through a tightly integrated loop of eight (8) core phases — **Plan, Code, Build, Test, Release, Deploy, Operate, and Monitor** — overarched by **Continuous Feedback**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP-2020 aligned) syllabus for **PEITT742**, the DevOps Lifecycle is treated as the *operational backbone* of modern Agile engineering. It is **not a single tool** but a **cultural, procedural, and technological loop** that operationalizes the Agile Manifesto in production environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEITT742 — Module 2):**
> Students are expected to demonstrate **CO2 — *Apply DevOps principles to design continuous integration, delivery, and deployment pipelines***. The DevOps Lifecycle is the central artifact used to evaluate this outcome in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Analogy

Imagine a high-end **cloud kitchen** that serves thousands of customers daily. To keep food hot, consistent, and safe, the kitchen cannot work in "silos":

| Kitchen Stage | DevOps Lifecycle Phase | Analogy Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Recipe design & customer surveys | **Plan** | Deciding *what* to build and *why* |
| Chef cooks the dish | **Code** | Developer writes application code |
| Mise-en-place (preparing ingredients) | **Build** | Compiling & packaging the software |
| Taste-testing by QC chef | **Test** | Automated & manual validation |
| Plating & labeling | **Release** | Versioning and approval |
| Dispatch to customer | **Deploy** | Pushing to production |
| Running the restaurant | **Operate** | Keeping the system live |
| Customer reviews & feedback | **Monitor** | Observing metrics & logs |

The crucial insight: **the moment a customer review arrives, the head chef re-plans the menu**. This is the **closed feedback loop** that makes DevOps a *cycle*, not a *waterfall*.

---

## 1.3 Why the DevOps Lifecycle Matters in 2024-Scheme KTU Examinations

> [!TIP]
> The 14-mark ESE questions in PEITT742 frequently test whether a student can:
> 1. **Draw** the lifecycle as an **infinity symbol (∞)** or circular loop.
> 2. **Map** a tool (e.g., Jenkins, Git, Selenium, Ansible, Nagios) to a specific phase.
> 3. **Explain** the *continuous* sub-processes (CI, CT, CD, CM, CF) that surround the loop.

A precise answer is worth **14 marks** — a vague definition is rarely worth more than **3-4 marks**.


<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DevOps Lifecycle — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Phases — Analytical Breakdown

### **Phase 1: PLAN**
* **Objective:** Translate business vision into an executable, prioritized backlog.
* **Inputs:** Customer feedback, market research, compliance requirements.
* **Outputs:** Epics → User Stories → Sprint Backlog.
* **Typical Artifacts:** Product Roadmap, Sprint Goal, Definition of Done (DoD).
* **Standard Tools:** **Jira, Azure Boards, Trello, GitHub Projects**.

> [!NOTE]
> *Why this phase matters:* The KTU examiner expects you to know that **Plan** is where **Agile and DevOps intersect**. It is the *only* phase that is heavily human-driven; the other seven are increasingly automated.

### **Phase 2: CODE**
* **Objective:** Translate planned user stories into source code.
* **Practices:** Pair programming, trunk-based development, peer reviews.
* **Version Control Standard:** **Git** with branching models such as **GitFlow** or **GitHub Flow**.
* **Output Metric:** **Mean Time to First Commit (MTTFC)**.
* **Tools:** VS Code, IntelliJ, Git, Bitbucket.

### **Phase 3: BUILD**
* **Objective:** Convert source code into a runnable, packaged artifact (e.g., `.jar`, `.war`, Docker image, `.exe`).
* **Automation:** Build scripts committed to repository; triggered on every commit.
* **Key Tools:** **Maven, Gradle, npm, Webpack, Docker**.
* **Build Health Metrics:** **Build Success Rate (BSR)** and **Build Duration**.

### **Phase 4: TEST**
* **Objective:** Validate functional, performance, and security behavior.
* **Test Pyramid (Kent Beck):** Unit (largest) → Integration → System → Acceptance (smallest).
* **Automation Frameworks:** **Selenium, JUnit, TestNG, Postman, JMeter**.
* **Modern Concept:** **Shift-Left Testing** — test as early as the *code* phase.

### **Phase 5: RELEASE**
* **Objective:** Approve and version a build for production deployment.
* **Key Activities:** Change Advisory Board (CAB) sign-off, release notes, rollback plan.
* **Conceptual Distinction:**
  * **Continuous Delivery (CDe):** Release-ready builds, *manual* production push.
  * **Continuous Deployment (CDp):** *Automated* push to production.

### **Phase 6: DEPLOY**
* **Objective:** Provision infrastructure and roll out the artifact to production.
* **Paradigms:** **Blue-Green**, **Canary**, **Rolling** deployments.
* **Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC) Tools:** **Terraform, Ansible, Pulumi, AWS CloudFormation**.
* **Deployment Metric:** **Deployment Frequency (DF)** — a core **DORA** metric.

### **Phase 7: OPERATE**
* **Objective:** Ensure the running system meets **Service Level Objectives (SLOs)**.
* **Activities:** Capacity planning, patching, scaling, incident response.
* **Container Orchestration:** **Kubernetes, Docker Swarm, Amazon ECS**.
* **SRE Concept:** **Error Budgets** — allowed downtime over a period.

### **Phase 8: MONITOR**
* **Objective:** Observe, record, and surface system health and user experience.
* **Three Pillars of Observability:** **Logs, Metrics, Traces**.
* **Tools:** **Prometheus, Grafana, ELK Stack, Splunk, Nagios**.
* **Key SRE Metric:** **Mean Time To Detect (MTTD)** and **Mean Time To Resolve (MTTR)**.

---

## 2.2 The "Continuous" Sub-Processes Wrapping the Loop

| Continuous Process | Spans Across | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Continuous Integration (CI)** | Code → Build → Test | Merge code frequently; verify with automated builds/tests |
| **Continuous Testing (CT)** | Build → Test → Release | Run automated tests *at every stage* |
| **Continuous Delivery (CDe)** | Test → Release → Deploy (manual gate) | Keep code *always* deployable |
| **Continuous Deployment (CDp)** | Test → Release → Deploy (no gate) | *Auto-deploy* every passing build |
| **Continuous Monitoring (CM)** | Deploy → Operate → Monitor | Real-time visibility into production |
| **Continuous Feedback (CF)** | Monitor → Plan (closes the loop) | Feed learnings back into planning |
| **Continuous Security (DevSecOps)** | All phases | Embed security scanning into the pipeline |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Metric Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **measurable quantities** that appear in KTU 14-mark numerical/analytical questions on the DevOps Lifecycle.

| Symbol | Metric / Formula | Description | Standard Target (DORA) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $DF$ | Deployment Frequency | Deployments per unit time | Elite: *On-demand* (multiple per day) |
| $LT$ | Lead Time for Changes | Time from commit → production | Elite: $\lt 1$ hour |
| $CFR$ | Change Failure Rate | $\dfrac{\text{Failed Deployments}}{\text{Total Deployments}} \times 100\%$ | Elite: $0\% - 15\%$ |
| $MTTR$ | Mean Time To Recover | Avg. recovery time after failure | Elite: $\lt 1$ hour |
| $MTTD$ | Mean Time To Detect | Avg. time to detect an incident | Minimize |
| $BSR$ | Build Success Rate | $\dfrac{\text{Successful Builds}}{\text{Total Builds}} \times 100\%$ | Target $\ge 95\%$ |
| $T_{cycle}$ | Cycle Time | $\text{Plan}_{\text{end}} - \text{Plan}_{\text{start}}$ | Minimize |
| $SLO$ | Service Level Objective | $\dfrac{\text{Successful Requests}}{\text{Total Requests}} \times 100\%$ | e.g., $99.9\%$ ("three nines") |

> **Engineering Utility:** These metrics collectively form the **DORA Four Keys** (Accelerate, 2018), which is the industry's de-facto measure of DevOps maturity. KTU questions on the lifecycle often require students to *compute* one of these metrics given a scenario.

---

## 2.4 Why the Lifecycle is a *Cycle*, Not a *Pipeline*

> [!TIP]
> Many students lose marks by drawing a one-way arrow. The DevOps Lifecycle is a **closed loop (∞ symbol)** because **Monitor** continuously feeds intelligence into **Plan**. Without this return arrow, the diagram is *incomplete* and the answer is marked down by at least **2 marks** in a 14-mark question.


<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# DevOps Lifecycle — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Computing the **Change Failure Rate (CFR)**

**Problem Statement (typical 7-mark KTU sub-question):**
> *"A team deployed 60 builds in a sprint. Out of these, 9 caused production rollbacks. Calculate the Change Failure Rate and classify the team as Elite, High, Medium, or Low according to DORA benchmarks."*

### Step 1 — Identify the given values

The total number of deployments $N_{\text{total}}$ and the number of failed deployments $N_{\text{fail}}$ are extracted from the problem statement.

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 60, \quad N_{\text{fail}} = 9
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the CFR formula

The Change Failure Rate is defined as the ratio of failed deployments to total deployments, expressed as a percentage.

$$
CFR = \frac{N_{\text{fail}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values

$$
CFR = \frac{9}{60} \times 100\%
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the fraction

$$
\frac{9}{60} = \frac{3}{20} = 0.15
$$

Therefore:

$$
CFR = 0.15 \times 100\% = 15\%
$$

### Step 5 — Classify per DORA benchmark

> [!NOTE]
> **DORA Performance Classification (2018–2024, Google Cloud DevOps Research & Assessment):**
> * **Elite Performers:** $CFR \in [0\%, 15\%]$
> * **High Performers:** $CFR \in (15\%, 30\%]$
> * **Medium Performers:** $CFR \in (30\%, 45\%]$
> * **Low Performers:** $CFR > 45\%$

Since $CFR = 15\%$ falls on the **upper boundary of the Elite band**, the team is classified as an **Elite Performer**.

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{CFR = 15\% \quad \text{(Elite Performer)}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Computing the **Build Success Rate (BSR)**

**Problem Statement:**
> *"Over a 10-day sprint, Jenkins executed 200 builds. 12 builds failed due to compilation errors, 5 failed due to test failures, and the rest succeeded. Find the Build Success Rate and infer the pipeline's health."*

### Step 1 — Tally the total number of failed builds

Failures occur due to two distinct causes in this problem.

$$
N_{\text{compile\_fail}} = 12, \quad N_{\text{test\_fail}} = 5
$$

Total failures:

$$
N_{\text{fail}} = 12 + 5 = 17
$$

### Step 2 — Compute successful builds

Successful builds are the complement of failures within the total number of builds.

$$
N_{\text{success}} = N_{\text{total}} - N_{\text{fail}} = 200 - 17 = 183
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the BSR formula

$$
BSR = \frac{N_{\text{success}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and simplify

$$
BSR = \frac{183}{200} \times 100\% = 0.915 \times 100\% = 91.5\%
$$

### Step 5 — Infer pipeline health

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Industry standard $BSR \ge 95\%$ indicates a *healthy* CI pipeline. Since $BSR = 91.5\% \lt 95\%$, the pipeline is **moderately unhealthy** and warrants **root-cause analysis** of the 5 test failures and 12 compile errors.

$$
\boxed{BSR = 91.5\% \quad \text{(Pipeline requires improvement)}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Phase-to-Tool Mapping (Exam-Ready Table)

| Lifecycle Phase | Primary Activity | Industry-Standard Tool | Output Artifact | Continuous Process it Belongs To |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Plan** | Backlog grooming, sprint planning | **Jira / Azure Boards** | Sprint Backlog | — |
| **Code** | Implementation, version control | **Git + GitHub / GitLab** | Source code commits | — |
| **Build** | Compile, package, containerize | **Maven / Gradle / Docker** | `.jar` / `.war` / Docker image | **CI** |
| **Test** | Automated & manual testing | **Selenium / JUnit / Postman** | Test reports | **CI + CT** |
| **Release** | Versioning, approval gate | **JFrog Artifactory / GitHub Releases** | Release tag | **CDe** |
| **Deploy** | Infrastructure provisioning & rollout | **Ansible / Terraform / Kubernetes** | Running services | **CDp** |
| **Operate** | Scaling, patching, SRE tasks | **Kubernetes / AWS / Azure** | Live system | **CM** |
| **Monitor** | Logs, metrics, traces | **Prometheus / Grafana / ELK** | Dashboards, alerts | **CM + CF** |
| **Loop-back** | Feedback → Plan | **Jira (re-issues) / Retrospectives** | New user stories | **CF** |


<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# DevOps Lifecycle — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Complete DevOps Lifecycle (∞ Loop Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["PLAN"] --> B["CODE"]
    B --> C["BUILD"]
    C --> D["TEST"]
    D --> E["RELEASE"]
    E --> F["DEPLOY"]
    F --> G["OPERATE"]
    G --> H["MONITOR"]
    H -->|"Continuous Feedback"| A

    subgraph CI["CONTINUOUS INTEGRATION"]
        B
        C
        D
    end

    subgraph CD["CONTINUOUS DELIVERY DEPLOYMENT"]
        E
        F
    end

    subgraph CM["CONTINUOUS MONITORING"]
        G
        H
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to draw this in an exam (without Mermaid):**
> 1. Draw a large **infinity symbol (∞)** or a **circle of 8 boxes** with arrows.
> 2. Label each box in clockwise order: **Plan → Code → Build → Test → Release → Deploy → Operate → Monitor**.
> 3. Draw a **dashed feedback arrow** from **Monitor back to Plan** — this is the *single most important* arrow in the diagram.
> 4. Surround the figure with the labels: **CI, CT, CDe, CDp, CM, CF** as appropriate "rings" or "bands".

---

## 4.2 Phase-to-Tool Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P["PLAN PHASE"] --> P1["Jira"]
    P --> P2["Azure Boards"]
    P --> P3["Confluence"]

    C["CODE PHASE"] --> C1["Git"]
    C --> C2["VS Code"]
    C --> C3["GitHub"]

    B["BUILD PHASE"] --> B1["Maven"]
    B --> B2["Gradle"]
    B --> B3["Docker"]

    T["TEST PHASE"] --> T1["Selenium"]
    T --> T2["JUnit"]
    T --> T3["JMeter"]

    R["RELEASE PHASE"] --> R1["Artifactory"]
    R --> R2["GitHub Releases"]

    D["DEPLOY PHASE"] --> D1["Ansible"]
    D --> D2["Terraform"]
    D --> D3["Kubernetes"]

    O["OPERATE PHASE"] --> O1["AWS"]
    O --> O2["Azure"]

    M["MONITOR PHASE"] --> M1["Prometheus"]
    M --> M2["Grafana"]
    M --> M3["ELK Stack"]
```

---

## 4.3 Continuous Sub-Process Interaction Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Outer["CONTINUOUS SUB-PROCESSES"]
        CI["CI: Continuous Integration"]
        CT["CT: Continuous Testing"]
        CDE["CDe: Continuous Delivery"]
        CDP["CDp: Continuous Deployment"]
        CMO["CM: Continuous Monitoring"]
        CF["CF: Continuous Feedback"]
    end

    CI --> CT
    CT --> CDE
    CDE --> CDP
    CDP --> CMO
    CMO --> CF
    CF --> CI
```

> [!NOTE]
> The "Continuous" sub-processes themselves form a *meta-cycle* that wraps around the operational phases. In KTU 14-mark answers, mentioning this **meta-cycle** typically earns **2-3 extra marks** for demonstrating depth.

---

## 4.4 DORA Maturity Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["Compute CFR, LT, MTTR, DF"] --> Q1{"All four in Elite band?"}
    Q1 -->|"Yes"| A1["ELITE PERFORMER"]
    Q1 -->|"No"| Q2{"Three of four in Elite or High?"}
    Q2 -->|"Yes"| A2["HIGH PERFORMER"]
    Q2 -->|"No"| Q3{"Two of four in High or Medium?"}
    Q3 -->|"Yes"| A3["MEDIUM PERFORMER"]
    Q3 -->|"No"| A4["LOW PERFORMER"]
```


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# DevOps Lifecycle — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### **Q1. Define the DevOps Lifecycle and list its eight phases.**
*Model Answer (for 3 marks):*
The DevOps Lifecycle is a continuous, iterative end-to-end process that integrates development and operations to deliver software rapidly and reliably. The eight phases are:
**Plan → Code → Build → Test → Release → Deploy → Operate → Monitor**, with a continuous feedback loop from Monitor back to Plan.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

### **Q2. Differentiate between Continuous Delivery and Continuous Deployment.**
*Model Answer (for 3 marks):*

| Aspect | Continuous Delivery (CDe) | Continuous Deployment (CDp) |
|---|---|---|
| Production Push | **Manual** approval required | **Fully automated** |
| Goal | Code is *always* deployable | Code is *automatically* deployed |
| Risk | Lower (human gate) | Higher (requires mature testing) |
| Maturity Level | High | Elite |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — PEITT742 — Module 2]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the eight phases of the DevOps Lifecycle with suitable tools used in each phase. **(7 marks)**

*Model Answer:*
The eight phases form an ∞-shaped loop.

| Phase | Purpose | Tool(s) |
|---|---|---|
| **Plan** | Backlog, sprint goals | Jira, Azure Boards |
| **Code** | Implementation, version control | Git, GitHub, VS Code |
| **Build** | Compile & package | Maven, Gradle, Docker |
| **Test** | Validate quality | Selenium, JUnit, JMeter |
| **Release** | Version & approve | JFrog, GitHub Releases |
| **Deploy** | Push to production | Ansible, Terraform, Kubernetes |
| **Operate** | Run & scale | AWS, Azure, Kubernetes |
| **Monitor** | Observe health | Prometheus, Grafana, ELK |

The **Continuous Feedback** loop from Monitor back to Plan closes the cycle, ensuring learnings inform future planning.

**(b)** Discuss the role of **Continuous Integration (CI)** and **Continuous Deployment (CDp)** in the DevOps Lifecycle. Explain how Jenkins implements CI/CD. **(7 marks)**

*Model Answer:*
**CI** merges all developer code into a shared trunk multiple times a day. Each merge triggers an automated build and test cycle, surfacing integration bugs early. **CDp** extends this by automatically deploying every green build to production.

**Jenkins Implementation Steps:**
1. Developer pushes code to GitHub (webhook triggers Jenkins).
2. Jenkins pulls the code, runs `mvn clean install` (build).
3. Jenkins executes JUnit/Selenium tests.
4. On green, Jenkins builds a Docker image.
5. Jenkins invokes Ansible/Terraform to deploy to Kubernetes.
6. Jenkins archives artifacts in Artifactory and notifies via Slack/Email.

This end-to-end automation embodies the *CI/CD* portion of the DevOps Lifecycle.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the DevOps Lifecycle. Describe in detail the **Monitor** and **Operate** phases, including SRE concepts like SLO and Error Budgets. **(7 marks)**

*Model Answer:*
[Diagram: ∞ loop with all 8 phases and a feedback arrow from Monitor to Plan.]

The **Operate** phase ensures production systems meet **Service Level Objectives (SLOs)** — quantified reliability targets (e.g., $99.9\%$ availability). An **Error Budget** is the inverse: $1 - SLO$. If $SLO = 99.9\%$, the error budget over 30 days is:

$$
\text{Error Budget} = (1 - 0.999) \times 30 \times 24 \times 60 = 43.2 \text{ minutes}
$$

The **Monitor** phase uses the **three pillars of observability** — *Logs* (event records), *Metrics* (numerical time-series), and *Traces* (request paths) — to detect breaches of the error budget. Tools include **Prometheus** (metrics), **ELK** (logs), and **Jaeger** (traces). When monitoring reveals an issue, an **alert** fires, initiating the incident response loop.

**(b)** A team deploys 80 builds in a sprint. 8 caused production failures. The team wants to maintain an Elite DORA classification. Calculate the **Change Failure Rate (CFR)** and recommend improvements. **(7 marks)**

*Model Answer:*
**Step 1:** Apply the CFR formula:

$$
CFR = \frac{N_{\text{fail}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% = \frac{8}{80} \times 100\% = 10\%
$$

**Step 2:** $CFR = 10\%$ lies in the Elite band ($0\% - 15\%$). The team qualifies as **Elite**.

**Step 3:** Recommendations to *maintain* Elite status:
1. Strengthen **automated test coverage** (target $\ge 80\%$).
2. Adopt **trunk-based development** to reduce merge conflicts.
3. Implement **Blue-Green deployments** to enable instant rollback.
4. Use **Feature Flags** to decouple deployment from release.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns in KTU 14-Mark DevOps Lifecycle Answers:**
> 1. **Missing the feedback arrow** from Monitor → Plan. *Penalty: 2 marks.*
> 2. **Confusing Continuous Delivery with Continuous Deployment.** *Penalty: 1-2 marks.*
> 3. **Listing tools without explaining their phase-mapping.** *Penalty: 1-2 marks.*
> 4. **Drawing a one-way (waterfall) diagram** instead of an ∞ loop. *Penalty: 2-3 marks.*
> 5. **Ignoring the "Continuous" sub-processes** (CI, CT, CDe, CDp, CM, CF). *Penalty: 2 marks.*
> 6. **Not mentioning DORA metrics** in any monitoring-related sub-question. *Penalty: 1-2 marks.*
> 7. **Writing vague definitions** like "DevOps is about collaboration" without naming the eight phases. *Penalty: 2-3 marks.*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DevOps Lifecycle — Top 12 Things to Remember Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Eight phases:** Plan → Code → Build → Test → Release → Deploy → Operate → Monitor.
- [ ] **It is a closed loop (∞)** — never draw it as a waterfall.
- [ ] **Feedback arrow** from Monitor to Plan is **mandatory** in the diagram.
- [ ] **CI** = Code + Build + Test automation.
- [ ] **CDe** = Manual production gate; **CDp** = Fully automated.
- [ ] **DORA Four Metrics:** Deployment Frequency, Lead Time, CFR, MTTR.
- [ ] **Elite CFR band:** $0\% - 15\%$.
- [ ] **Three pillars of observability:** Logs, Metrics, Traces.
- [ ] **IaC tools:** Terraform, Ansible, Pulumi.
- [ ] **Container orchestration:** Kubernetes.
- [ ] **Shift-Left Testing** = test as early as the Code phase.
- [ ] **Error Budget** $= 1 - SLO$; consumed by downtime.


<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment (CI/CD): Jenkins, GitLab CI, Travis CI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT742 - SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT WITH AGILE AND DEVOPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment (CI/CD): Jenkins, GitLab CI, Travis CI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Continuous Integration (CI) — Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuous Integration (CI)** is a software development practice in which developers merge their code changes into a shared central repository frequently (preferably multiple times a day). Each merge triggers an **automated build and test sequence**, allowing teams to detect integration bugs early, improve software quality, and reduce the time taken to validate and release new software updates.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, CI is the **automation pillar of DevOps** that enforces *frequent integration* + *automated verification* + *immediate feedback* on every commit pushed to a version control system (typically Git).

### 1.2 Continuous Deployment (CD) — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuous Deployment (CD)** is the DevOps practice of automatically releasing every validated code change to a production-like (or production) environment, **without manual intervention**, after it successfully passes the automated test suite. It extends CI by ensuring that the software is *always* in a deployable, ship-ready state.

A closely related but distinct term is **Continuous Delivery**, where every successful build is *automatically packaged and made ready for release*, but the **actual production push requires a human click** of approval.

| Term | Automation Level | Manual Gate? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Continuous Integration | Build + Test only | Yes (manual deployment) |
| Continuous Delivery | Build + Test + Package + Stage | Yes (manual production push) |
| Continuous Deployment | Build + Test + Package + **Auto Production Push** | **No** |

### 1.3 Real-World Intuitive Analogy

**The Assembly Line Analogy (Automobile Factory):**
Imagine a car manufacturing plant. In the old "waterfall" days, an entire car was assembled, and only at the **end of the month** would quality control inspect it. If the brakes were faulty, the entire month's production had to be recalled.

**CI** is like placing a **quality sensor after every robotic arm**. After every small action (welding, painting, engine mount), an automated check fires. **CD** is the conveyor belt that, once the car passes all sensors, automatically rolls it onto the transport truck for the showroom — **no human dispatcher needed**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** *CI = "Commit and Check"*, *CD = "Code to Customer (automated)"*.

### 1.4 The Three CI/CD Tools — Quick Introduction

- **Jenkins** — An open-source, self-hosted **automation server** written in Java. The de-facto industry standard with ~1,800+ community plugins. Highly customizable but requires manual setup and maintenance.
- **GitLab CI** — A **tightly integrated** CI/CD engine built directly into the GitLab platform. Defined via a `.gitlab-ci.yml` file in the repo root. Offers seamless integration with GitLab's issue tracking, registry, and Kubernetes.
- **Travis CI** — A **hosted (cloud-based)** CI service, popular in the open-source community, configured via a `.travis.yml` file. Excellent for GitHub-hosted public repositories.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A typical 14-mark KTU question asks students to *compare* Jenkins, GitLab CI, and Travis CI across **architecture, configuration, hosting model, plugin ecosystem, scalability, and pipeline-as-code** support.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Canonical CI/CD Pipeline — Stage-by-Stage Breakdown

A robust CI/CD pipeline (regardless of the underlying tool) follows a predictable sequence. The following logical stages are universal:

1. **Source Stage (SCM Trigger):**
   - Developer pushes code (`git push`) to the central repository.
   - The CI server's **webhook listener** detects the change and triggers a pipeline run.
   - The build agent pulls the latest source tree.
2. **Build Stage:**
   - Source code is compiled (Java → bytecode), transpiled (TypeScript → JS), or assembled (Maven, Gradle, npm).
   - Dependencies are resolved and cached.
3. **Test Stage:**
   - **Unit Tests** — Validate individual functions/methods.
   - **Integration Tests** — Validate component interactions.
   - **Static Analysis** — SonarQube, ESLint, PMD scan the code for smells.
4. **Code Quality / Security Gate:**
   - Tools like OWASP Dependency-Check, Snyk, or Trivy scan for known vulnerabilities.
   - Build **fails the pipeline** if CVSS score exceeds threshold.
5. **Package Stage:**
   - The artifact (`.jar`, `.war`, Docker image, `.tar.gz`) is versioned (semantic versioning) and pushed to an artifact repository (Nexus, Artifactory, GitLab Container Registry, Docker Hub).
6. **Deploy Stage (Staging → Production):**
   - The artifact is deployed to a **staging environment** for smoke tests, UAT, and load tests.
   - On passing, it is promoted to **production** (auto in CD, manual click in Continuous Delivery).
7. **Monitor & Feedback Stage:**
   - Tools like Prometheus, Grafana, ELK stack monitor the deployed app.
   - Feedback loop closes back to the developer.

### 2.2 Jenkins — Deep Architecture Analysis

**Jenkins follows a Master-Agent (Controller-Node) architecture:**

- **Master (Controller):** The central process that:
  - Serves the web UI (default port **8080**).
  - Schedules jobs.
  - Dispatches builds to agents.
  - Stores configuration in `~/.jenkins` (or `JENKINS_HOME`).
- **Agent (Node/Slave):** A worker machine (physical, VM, Docker container, or Kubernetes pod) that **actually executes** the build steps. Multiple agents allow **parallel execution** and **OS-diverse builds** (e.g., Linux + Windows + macOS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Jenkins Pipeline-as-Code:** A `Jenkinsfile` is a text file (Groovy DSL) checked into the repository. It defines the pipeline declaratively (`pipeline { ... }`) or scripted (`node { ... }`). Using a Jenkinsfile ensures the build definition is **version-controlled**, **reviewable**, and **reproducible**.

**Core Jenkinsfile Stages (Declarative):**
- `pipeline { }` — Root block.
- `agent any` — Where the pipeline runs.
- `stages { stage('Name') { steps { ... } } }` — Sequential execution units.
- `post { }` — Actions after pipeline (e.g., `success`, `failure`, `always`).
- `triggers { }` — What starts the build (`pollSCM`, `cron`, `githubPush`).

### 2.3 GitLab CI — Deep Architecture Analysis

**GitLab CI is built on a Runner-based architecture:**

- **GitLab Server (SaaS or self-hosted):** Hosts the repository, UI, and pipeline orchestrator.
- **`.gitlab-ci.yml`:** YAML file in repo root defining the pipeline.
- **GitLab Runners:** Lightweight agents (Go binary) registered to the GitLab instance. They pick up jobs via polling. Runners can be:
  - **Shared Runners** — Available to all projects in the instance.
  - **Group Runners** — For a specific group.
  - **Project-Specific Runners** — Dedicated to one project.
- **Runner Executors:** The environment where the job actually runs — `shell`, `docker`, `docker-machine`, `kubernetes`, `ssh`, `virtualbox`, `parallels`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept — `stages` vs `jobs`:** In GitLab CI, `stages` define the *order* (run sequentially), while `jobs` within a stage run *in parallel* by default. A `needs:` keyword allows DAG-style job dependencies.

### 2.4 Travis CI — Deep Architecture Analysis

**Travis CI uses a virtualized build worker model:**

- **Travis CI Service:** Hosted on `travis-ci.com` (private repos) or `travis-ci.org` (open source). The user *does not manage infrastructure*.
- **`.travis.yml`:** YAML configuration file in the repo root.
- **Build Matrix:** Travis allows testing against multiple combinations of **language versions**, **operating systems** (`linux`, `osx`, `windows`), and **environment variables** simultaneously via the `matrix:` key.
- **Lifecycle Phases:** `before_install` → `install` → `before_script` → `script` → `before_deploy` → `deploy` → `after_success` / `after_failure` → `after_script`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Industry Note:** Travis CI's free tier for open-source has been **significantly restricted** since 2021. Most new KTU questions now favor Jenkins + GitLab CI for industry relevance.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

| Feature | Jenkins | GitLab CI | Travis CI |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Origin / Vendor** | Hudson fork (Kohsuke Kawaguchi, 2011) | GitLab Inc. (2015) | Travis CI GmbH (2011) |
| **Hosting Model** | Self-hosted (mandatory) | SaaS or Self-hosted | SaaS only |
| **Configuration File** | `Jenkinsfile` (Groovy DSL) | `.gitlab-ci.yml` (YAML) | `.travis.yml` (YAML) |
| **Architecture** | Master + Agents | Server + Runners | Hosted Workers |
| **Plugin Ecosystem** | ~1,800+ plugins | Built-in (no plugins) + Integrations | Limited (deploy providers) |
| **Pipeline as Code** | Excellent (declarative + scripted) | Excellent (YAML DAG) | Good (YAML) |
| **Container Native** | Via plugins / Jenkins X | First-class (Docker, K8s) | Limited |
| **Parallel Builds** | Yes (matrix, parallel stages) | Yes (parallel jobs, DAG) | Yes (build matrix) |
| **Best For** | Complex, customizable enterprise pipelines | GitLab-centric teams, K8s, DevSecOps | Open-source GitHub projects |
| **Learning Curve** | Steep | Moderate | Low |
| **Cost** | Free (OSS) | Free tier + Premium tiers | Free OSS / paid private |

### 2.6 Core Engineering Principles Behind CI/CD

- **Fail Fast:** Detect defects within *minutes* of introduction, not weeks.
- **Single Source of Truth:** Pipeline definition lives in the repo, not on a server.
- **Reproducibility:** Any commit, on any machine, can rebuild the same artifact (achieved via locked dependency versions, Docker, declarative pipelines).
- **Trunk-Based Development:** Encourages short-lived branches merged frequently to `main` to reduce merge hell.
- **Immutable Artifacts:** The exact same binary that passed tests in staging is promoted to production — never rebuilt.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Pipeline Construction & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Walkthrough 1 — Building a Jenkins Declarative Pipeline (Node.js App)

**Scenario:** A KTU student must construct a Jenkinsfile that:
- Triggers on every GitHub push.
- Runs on any available agent.
- Installs Node.js, runs `npm install`, executes tests, and publishes a coverage report.

**Step 1 — Define the pipeline skeleton and trigger.**

```groovy
pipeline {
    agent any
    triggers {
        githubPush()
    }
    options {
        timestamps()
        timeout(time: 30, unit: 'MINUTES')
    }
```

> **Logic:** `agent any` lets the master dispatch to any registered node. `githubPush()` registers a webhook listener (equivalent to polling). `timeout` prevents zombie builds.

**Step 2 — Add environment variables and stages.**

```groovy
    environment {
        NODE_VERSION = '20.11.0'
        APP_NAME     = 'kTuStudentPortal'
    }
    stages {
        stage('Checkout') {
            steps {
                checkout scm
            }
        }
        stage('Setup Node') {
            steps {
                tool name: 'NodeJS-20', type: 'NodeJS'
            }
        }
        stage('Install Dependencies') {
            steps {
                sh 'npm ci'
            }
        }
        stage('Lint') {
            steps { sh 'npm run lint' }
        }
        stage('Unit Tests') {
            steps {
                sh 'npm test -- --coverage'
            }
            post {
                always {
                    junit '**/junit.xml'
                }
            }
        }
    }
}
```

> **Logic Walkthrough:**
> - `npm ci` is preferred over `npm install` in CI because it does a *clean, reproducible* install from `package-lock.json`.
> - The `post { always { junit ... } }` block ensures test results are archived **even if tests fail** — critical for the Jenkins dashboard.

**Step 3 — Failure handling with post-actions.**

```groovy
    post {
        success {
            echo 'Build passed — archiving artifact.'
            archiveArtifacts artifacts: 'dist/**', fingerprint: true
        }
        failure {
            mail to: 'devteam@ktu.edu',
                 subject: "FAILURE: ${env.JOB_NAME} #${env.BUILD_NUMBER}",
                 body: "Check console: ${env.BUILD_URL}"
        }
    }
}
```

> **Logic:** This `post` block implements the **feedback loop** of CI — the developer is notified the moment the pipeline breaks.

---

### 3.2 Walkthrough 2 — Building a GitLab CI Pipeline (Dockerized App)

**Scenario:** Build a Docker image, scan it, and push to GitLab Container Registry.

**Step 1 — Define image and global variables.**

```yaml
stages:
  - build
  - test
  - scan
  - deploy

variables:
  DOCKER_IMAGE: registry.gitlab.com/$CI_PROJECT_PATH
  DOCKER_TAG: $CI_COMMIT_SHORT_SHA

default:
  image: docker:24.0
  services:
    - docker:24.0-dind
```

> **Logic:** `docker-in-docker` (dind) service allows the runner to build images inside the CI job.

**Step 2 — Add the build job.**

```yaml
build_image:
  stage: build
  script:
    - docker build -t $DOCKER_IMAGE:$DOCKER_TAG .
    - docker push $DOCKER_IMAGE:$DOCKER_TAG
  only:
    - main
```

> **Logic:** Tagging by `$CI_COMMIT_SHORT_SHA` (not `latest`) ensures **immutable artifacts** — every commit gets a unique, traceable image.

**Step 3 — Add security scan and deployment.**

```yaml
trivy_scan:
  stage: scan
  image: aquasec/trivy:latest
  script:
    - trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL $DOCKER_IMAGE:$DOCKER_TAG
  allow_failure: true

deploy_staging:
  stage: deploy
  script:
    - kubectl apply -f k8s/staging.yaml
    - kubectl set image deployment/app app=$DOCKER_IMAGE:$DOCKER_TAG -n staging
  environment:
    name: staging
    url: https://staging.ktu-portal.edu
  only:
    - main
```

> **Logic:** `allow_failure: true` means a scan warning won't break the pipeline, but `exit-code 1` in Trivy still flags HIGH/CRITICAL CVEs. The `environment:` block creates a GitLab Environment for tracking deployments.

---

### 3.3 Walkthrough 3 — Travis CI Build Matrix

**Scenario:** Test a Python library on Python 3.9, 3.10, 3.11 across Linux and macOS.

```yaml
language: python
os:
  - linux
  - osx
python:
  - "3.9"
  - "3.10"
  - "3.11"

install:
  - pip install -r requirements.txt
  - pip install pytest coverage

script:
  - pytest --cov=ktulib tests/

after_success:
  - codecov

jobs:
  allow_failures:
    - os: osx
  fast_finish: true
```

> **Logic:** This creates a **2 × 3 = 6 job matrix**. `allow_failures` permits macOS failures without breaking the overall build. `fast_finish` lets the rest of the matrix complete even if one slot is slow.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Tool

| Scenario | Recommended Tool | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Enterprise with mixed tech stack (Java, .NET, Python) | **Jenkins** | Massive plugin ecosystem, mature, language-agnostic |
| Already using GitLab for SCM | **GitLab CI** | Zero-config integration, no extra server |
| Public GitHub open-source library | **Travis CI** (or GitHub Actions today) | Free for OSS, simple YAML, GitHub-native |
| Kubernetes-native microservices | **GitLab CI** or Jenkins X | First-class K8s support, Auto DevOps |
| Need strict on-premises data control | **Jenkins** or **GitLab Self-Managed** | Both can be fully air-gapped |
| Small team, minimal DevOps maturity | **Travis CI / GitLab CI** | Less ops overhead |

---

### 3.5 Quantitative Trade-Off Analysis (Build Time)

A common KTU numerical-style question: *Why does parallelization matter?*

Consider a pipeline with **4 stages** taking **5, 6, 4, and 3 minutes** respectively.

$$
T_{\text{serial}} = 5 + 6 + 4 + 3 = 18 \text{ minutes}
$$

If the test stage (6 min) is split into **3 parallel jobs**:

$$
T_{\text{parallel}} = 5 + \max\left(\frac{6}{3},\, 0\right) + 4 + 3 = 5 + 2 + 4 + 3 = 14 \text{ minutes}
$$

The **speedup factor** is:

$$
S = \frac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{parallel}}} = \frac{18}{14} \approx 1.286
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Parallelization gives diminishing returns as Amdahl's Law dictates. The *serial portion* (build + package + deploy) becomes the bottleneck.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Universal CI/CD Pipeline Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Developer git push] --> B[SCM Webhook Trigger]
    B --> C[CI Server Receives Event]
    C --> D[Agent / Runner Pulls Source]
    D --> E[Stage 1 Build Compile]
    E --> F[Stage 2 Unit Test]
    F --> G[Stage 3 Static Analysis]
    G --> H{All Quality Gates Pass?}
    H -- No --> I[Notify Developer Slack Email]
    H -- Yes --> J[Stage 4 Package Artifact]
    J --> K[Stage 5 Push to Registry]
    K --> L[Stage 6 Deploy to Staging]
    L --> M[Stage 7 Smoke and UAT Tests]
    M --> N{Approval Required?}
    N -- Yes Delivery --> O[Manual Click to Prod]
    N -- No Deployment --> P[Auto Deploy to Production]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Monitoring Prometheus Grafana]
    Q --> A
```

> **Reading Guide:** This is the **canonical DevOps feedback loop**. The arrow from `Q` back to `A` represents *continuous learning* — production telemetry informs the next commit.

### 4.2 Jenkins Master-Agent Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Dev[Developer] -->|git push| Repo[(Git Repository)]
    Repo -->|webhook| Master[Jenkins Master Controller]
    Master -->|schedules| Queue[(Build Queue)]
    Queue --> Agent1[Agent 1 Linux]
    Queue --> Agent2[Agent 2 Windows]
    Queue --> Agent3[Agent 3 Docker K8s]
    Agent1 --> Artifact[(Artifact Store)]
    Agent2 --> Artifact
    Agent3 --> Artifact
    Master --> UI[Web Dashboard Port 8080]
    Master --> JCasC[Configuration as Code YAML]
```

> **Architecture Insight:** The **Master** never executes builds — it only orchestrates. All build artifacts, logs, and test results are aggregated back to the master for the dashboard.

### 4.3 GitLab CI Runner Topology (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GL[GitLab Server] -->|API poll| R1[Shared Runner Pool]
    GL --> R2[Group Runner]
    GL --> R3[Project Runner]
    R1 --> E1[Executor Docker]
    R1 --> E2[Executor Shell]
    R1 --> E3[Executor Kubernetes]
    R2 --> E1
    R3 --> E1
    E1 --> Cache[(Distributed Cache)]
    E2 --> Cache
    E3 --> Cache
```

> **Insight:** GitLab's **shared runner pool** dramatically reduces infrastructure cost — a single pool serves hundreds of projects.

### 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right CI Tool (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need a CI CD Tool] --> Q1{Where is your code hosted?}
    Q1 -- GitLab --> GitLabCI[Use GitLab CI]
    Q1 -- GitHub Public OSS --> Q2{Need easy setup?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> TravisCI[Use Travis CI]
    Q2 -- No --> GH[Use GitHub Actions]
    Q1 -- GitHub Private / Enterprise --> Q3{Need heavy customization?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Jenkins[Use Jenkins Self Hosted]
    Q3 -- No --> GitLabCISelf[Use GitLab CI Self Hosted]
    GitLabCI --> End[Decision Made]
    TravisCI --> End
    GH --> End
    Jenkins --> End
    GitLabCISelf --> End
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Continuous Integration, Continuous Delivery, and Continuous Deployment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Continuous Integration (CI):** Developers frequently merge code into a shared main branch. Each merge triggers an automated build and test cycle. *Goal:* detect integration issues early. *Human intervention:* deployment to production is still manual. **(1 mark)**
- **Continuous Delivery:** Extends CI by automatically packaging and staging every successful build so that it is *always* in a release-ready state. *Human intervention:* a **manual approval** is required to push to production. **(1 mark)**
- **Continuous Deployment:** Goes one step further — every change that passes automated tests is **automatically released to production** without any human gate. *Goal:* zero-touch releases. **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**List any three advantages of using Jenkins as a CI/CD tool.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Massive Plugin Ecosystem:** Over 1,800 community plugins allow integration with virtually any tool (Git, Docker, Kubernetes, SonarQube, Slack, etc.).
2. **Pipeline as Code:** Jenkinsfiles (Groovy DSL) make build definitions version-controlled, reviewable, and reproducible.
3. **Distributed Builds:** Master-Agent architecture enables parallel and cross-platform builds (Windows, Linux, macOS), drastically reducing feedback time.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **Jenkins Master-Agent architecture** with a neat diagram. Describe the role of the Master and any three responsibilities of an Agent node. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a **declarative Jenkinsfile** for a Java Maven web application that:
- Polls SCM every 5 minutes.
- Uses Maven 3.9 as a build tool.
- Runs `mvn clean test`, archives `target/*.war`, and emails the developer on failure. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question A — Model Solution**

**(a) Jenkins Master-Agent Architecture (7 marks):**

Jenkins uses a **distributed Master-Agent (Controller-Node)** architecture to enable scalability and parallel execution.

**Master (Controller) Responsibilities:**
1. **Web UI Hosting:** Serves the dashboard on port `8080` for configuration and monitoring.
2. **Job Scheduling:** Receives build triggers (webhooks, cron) and queues them.
3. **Agent Management:** Maintains a registry of connected agents and dispatches jobs based on labels and availability.
4. **Result Aggregation:** Collects console logs, test reports (`JUnit`, `Cobertura`), and artifacts from agents for centralized display.

**Agent (Node/Slave) Responsibilities (any 3):**
1. **Build Execution:** Runs the actual `sh`/`bat` steps, compiles code, executes tests.
2. **Workspace Provisioning:** Creates an isolated working directory for each build to prevent cross-contamination.
3. **Tool Provisioning:** Hosts specific toolchains (Maven, JDK, Node.js) configured via `tools {}` in Jenkinsfile.
4. **Artifact Production:** Generates binaries (`.war`, `.jar`, Docker images) and uploads them back to the master or to an external registry.

**Diagram (text-based for answer sheet):**
```
   ┌──────────────┐         ┌──────────────┐
   │   Jenkins    │ ──────► │  Agent (Linux)│
   │    Master    │         │  Maven / JDK │
   │  (Port 8080) │ ──────► ├──────────────┤
   │              │         │ Agent (Win)  │
   │  Scheduler   │ ──────► │  MSBuild     │
   └──────────────┘         ├──────────────┤
                            │ Agent (Docker)│
                            │  Container   │
                            └──────────────┘
```

**(Marks Distribution:** Architecture explanation: 3, Master responsibilities: 2, Agent responsibilities: 1, Diagram: 1.)

---

**(b) Declarative Jenkinsfile for Java Maven Web App (7 marks):**

```groovy
pipeline {
    agent any
    triggers {
        pollSCM('H/5 * * * *')   // Poll every 5 minutes
    }
    tools {
        maven 'Maven-3.9'        // Pre-configured in Jenkins Global Tools
    }
    stages {
        stage('Checkout') {
            steps {
                checkout scm
            }
        }
        stage('Build & Test') {
            steps {
                sh 'mvn clean test'
            }
        }
        stage('Package') {
            steps {
                sh 'mvn package -DskipTests'
            }
        }
    }
    post {
        success {
            archiveArtifacts artifacts: 'target/*.war', fingerprint: true
            junit '**/target/surefire-reports/*.xml'
        }
        failure {
            emailext(
                to: 'developer@ktu.edu',
                subject: "Build Failed: ${env.JOB_NAME} #${env.BUILD_NUMBER}",
                body: "Check console: ${env.BUILD_URL}",
                attachLog: true
            )
        }
    }
}
```

**Explanation of each block (for the 7 marks):**
- `agent any` (0.5 mark) — runs on any available node.
- `pollSCM('H/5 * * * *')` (1 mark) — poll SCM every 5 minutes using Jenkins cron syntax.
- `tools { maven 'Maven-3.9' }` (1 mark) — uses the pre-installed Maven 3.9.
- Three stages: Checkout, Build & Test, Package (2 marks).
- `post.success`: archive WAR + JUnit report (1 mark).
- `post.failure`: email developer with log (1 mark).
- Overall Jenkinsfile structure and best practices (0.5 mark).

---

#### **Question B. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Compare **Jenkins, GitLab CI, and Travis CI** based on: (i) Hosting model, (ii) Configuration language, (iii) Plugin/Extension mechanism, (iv) Architecture, (v) Parallel build support, (vi) Best-fit use case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a `.gitlab-ci.yml` file with **three stages** — `build`, `test`, `deploy` — that:
- Uses a Node.js 20 Docker image.
- Runs `npm install`, `npm test`, and on the `main` branch, deploys to a Kubernetes staging cluster. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B — Model Solution**

**(a) Comparative Analysis (7 marks):**

| Criterion | Jenkins | GitLab CI | Travis CI |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **(i) Hosting** | Self-hosted only | SaaS or self-managed | SaaS only |
| **(ii) Config Language** | Groovy DSL (Jenkinsfile) | YAML (`.gitlab-ci.yml`) | YAML (`.travis.yml`) |
| **(iii) Extensions** | 1,800+ community plugins | Built-in features + integrations | Limited deploy providers |
| **(iv) Architecture** | Master + Agents (distributed) | GitLab Server + Runners | Hosted VM workers |
| **(v) Parallel Builds** | Via `parallel {}` block & matrix | Parallel jobs in same stage | Build matrix (env × OS) |
| **(vi) Best Fit** | Enterprise heterogeneous stacks | GitLab-centric K8s teams | Open-source GitHub repos |

**(Marks:** 1 mark per correctly filled row × 6 criteria = 6 marks + 1 mark for table format/clarity.)

---

**(b) `.gitlab-ci.yml` for Node.js App (7 marks):**

```yaml
stages:
  - build
  - test
  - deploy

variables:
  KUBE_NAMESPACE: staging

default:
  image: node:20-alpine
  cache:
    key: ${CI_COMMIT_REF_SLUG}
    paths:
      - node_modules/

install_dependencies:
  stage: build
  script:
    - npm ci
  artifacts:
    paths:
      - node_modules/
    expire_in: 1 hour

run_unit_tests:
  stage: test
  script:
    - npm test
  coverage: '/Lines\s*:\s*(\d+\.\d+)%/'
  artifacts:
    reports:
      junit: junit.xml

deploy_to_staging:
  stage: deploy
  image: bitnami/kubectl:latest
  script:
    - kubectl apply -f k8s/staging/ -n $KUBE_NAMESPACE
    - kubectl set image deployment/webapp webapp=$CI_REGISTRY_IMAGE:$CI_COMMIT_SHORT_SHA -n $KUBE_NAMESPACE
  environment:
    name: staging
    url: https://staging.ktu-portal.edu
  only:
    - main
```

**Explanation (7 marks):**
- `stages` defines order; `default.image` sets the container (1.5 marks).
- `cache` for `node_modules` to speed up builds (1 mark).
- Three jobs: `install_dependencies`, `run_unit_tests`, `deploy_to_staging` (2 marks).
- `coverage:` regex extraction (0.5 mark).
- `environment:` block + `url:` (0.5 mark).
- `only: [main]` branch restriction (0.5 mark).
- `kubectl` commands and image tag using `$CI_COMMIT_SHORT_SHA` for immutability (1 mark).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Continuous Delivery with Continuous Deployment** — Many students lose 1-2 marks by stating they are the same. **Remember:** Delivery has a *manual approval gate* before production; Deployment does not.
> 2. **Jenkinsfile syntax errors** — Writing imperative (scripted) syntax when the question asks for *declarative* loses style marks. Use `pipeline { }` root block.
> 3. **Forgetting `agent` directive** — A declarative pipeline *without* `agent` is **invalid** and will fail validation. Always specify `agent any` or `agent { label '...' }`.
> 4. **In `.gitlab-ci.yml`, confusing `stages` with `stage`** — `stages:` (plural) is a *top-level* key defining global order; `stage:` (singular) is used *inside* each job to assign it to a stage.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** — In a 7-mark architecture question, a *neat diagram* (even hand-drawn) is worth **at least 1-2 marks**. Do not omit it.
> 6. **Not mentioning webhook trigger** — Many students say "Jenkins runs on every push" without explaining the *mechanism* (GitHub webhook or `pollSCM`). Examiners expect this technical depth.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU Exams):**

- **CI** = frequent integration + automated build + automated test on every commit.
- **CD (Deployment)** = automated release to production, *zero manual touch*.
- **CD (Delivery)** = automated release to staging, *manual click* for production.
- **Jenkins** = Java-based, self-hosted, Master-Agent, Groovy `Jenkinsfile`, ~1,800 plugins.
- **GitLab CI** = integrated into GitLab, `.gitlab-ci.yml` (YAML), Runner-based, supports DAG.
- **Travis CI** = SaaS, `.travis.yml` (YAML), build matrix, OSS-friendly (historically).
- **Pipeline Stages (Universal):** Source → Build → Test → Quality Gate → Package → Deploy → Monitor.
- **Best Practice:** Tag artifacts by **commit SHA**, never by `latest`.
- **Jenkinsfile must** include: `pipeline { }`, `agent`, `stages`, and optionally `post`, `triggers`, `tools`.
- **GitLab CI must** include: `stages:`, and each job must have `stage:` and `script:`.
- **Triggers:** Webhooks (push-based) > Polling (pull-based) for efficiency.
- **Architecture Bonus Point:** Mention *Master-Agent* for Jenkins, *Runner-Executor* for GitLab CI.
- **Security Trend (DevSecOps):** Modern pipelines integrate **Trivy**, **Snyk**, or **OWASP** scans as quality gates.
- **Parallelism math:** Use `S = T_serial / T_parallel`; recognize that serial portions limit speedup (Amdahl's Law).
- **Default ports:** Jenkins = **8080**, GitLab = **80/443**, Travis = SaaS (no port).
- **Industry Trend (2024+):** GitHub Actions and GitLab CI are gaining on Jenkins; Jenkins is still dominant in *legacy enterprise* environments.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Infrastructure as Code (IaC): Terraform, Ansible, Docker, and Kubernetes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT742 - SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT WITH AGILE AND DEVOPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Infrastructure as Code (IaC): Terraform, Ansible, Docker, and Kubernetes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Infrastructure as Code (IaC) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Infrastructure as Code (IaC)** is the practice of **provisioning, configuring, and managing computing infrastructure** (servers, networks, storage, load balancers, and platforms) through **machine-readable definition files** rather than manual hardware setup or interactive configuration tools. The infrastructure is treated exactly like application source code — versioned, tested, reviewed, and deployed automatically.

In the context of **DevOps and Agile**, IaC is the **automation backbone** that allows Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) pipelines to spin up and tear down environments in minutes, ensuring **reproducibility**, **scalability**, and **idempotency** across all stages of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The four pillar tools of IaC are **Terraform** (provisioning), **Ansible** (configuration management), **Docker** (containerization), and **Kubernetes** (container orchestration). Each addresses a different layer of the infrastructure stack.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The City Planner's Blueprint"

Imagine you are building a **modern smart city**:

- The **City Master Plan (Terraform)** decides *where* the buildings, roads, and power plants will be constructed.
- The **Interior Designer (Ansible)** ensures that *inside* every building, the lights, plumbing, and security cameras are configured uniformly.
- The **Prefabricated Apartment Units (Docker)** are standardized, portable containers — one box can be lifted from a factory in Bengaluru and placed in Kochi with zero modifications.
- The **City Traffic Control Tower (Kubernetes)** decides how many apartment units to deploy in each locality, monitors their health, replaces failed ones, and scales the population up during festivals.

Without IaC, the city planner would manually pour concrete for every building — slow, error-prone, and impossible to replicate. With IaC, the entire city is described in a **declarative blueprint file** that a machine can execute repeatedly with identical results.

## 1.3 The Four Pillars — One-Line Definitions

| Tool | Layer of Operation | One-Line Definition |
|------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Terraform** | Infrastructure Provisioning | Declarative tool that creates and manages cloud resources (VMs, networks, databases) using HCL. |
| **Ansible** | Configuration Management | Agentless tool that installs software and configures OS-level settings on existing servers using YAML playbooks. |
| **Docker** | Containerization | Platform that packages applications with their dependencies into lightweight, portable containers. |
| **Kubernetes** | Container Orchestration | System that automates deployment, scaling, healing, and networking of containerized applications. |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Metric to Remember for KTU Exams:** Terraform and Kubernetes are **declarative** (you describe the *desired end state*); Ansible is **procedural by default but can be declarative** (you list ordered *tasks*). All four tools aim for **idempotency** — running the same script 10 times produces the same result as running it once.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The IaC Operational Stack — Layered Architecture

Modern DevOps infrastructure follows a **four-layer cake** model. Each tool occupies a specific layer:

### Layer 1 — Provisioning (Terraform)
- **What it does:** Creates the *raw infrastructure* — virtual machines, VPCs, subnets, security groups, S3 buckets, RDS databases.
- **How it does it:** Uses a **declarative language called HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language)** stored in `.tf` files. The tool calls cloud-provider APIs (AWS, Azure, GCP) to instantiate resources.
- **Key workflow:**
  1. **Write** the desired infrastructure in `.tf` files.
  2. **Initialize** with `terraform init` (downloads provider plugins).
  3. **Plan** with `terraform plan` (preview of changes).
  4. **Apply** with `terraform apply` (executes the plan).
  5. **Destroy** with `terraform destroy` (cleans up).

### Layer 2 — Configuration Management (Ansible)
- **What it does:** After Terraform creates an empty VM, Ansible *configures* it — installs Nginx, opens firewall ports, deploys application code, creates users.
- **How it does it:** Uses **YAML-based playbooks** consisting of ordered *tasks*. Communication happens over **SSH** (no agent needed on target machines — hence *agentless*).
- **Core components:**
  - **Inventory** — list of target machines.
  - **Playbook** — YAML file containing plays and tasks.
  - **Module** — reusable unit of work (e.g., `apt`, `copy`, `service`).
  - **Role** — packaged collection of tasks, handlers, templates, and variables.

### Layer 3 — Containerization (Docker)
- **What it does:** Packages the application + its runtime + libraries into a **Docker Image** (a read-only template), which runs as a **Docker Container** (a running instance).
- **How it does it:** Uses a **Dockerfile** (recipe) → `docker build` → Image → `docker run` → Container.
- **Key concepts:**
  - **Image:** Immutable, layered blueprint.
  - **Container:** Runnable instance of an image.
  - **Docker Hub / Registry:** Public/private repository for images.
  - **Docker Compose:** Tool to define and run multi-container applications using a `docker-compose.yml` file.

### Layer 4 — Orchestration (Kubernetes — K8s)
- **What it does:** Manages hundreds/thousands of containers across a cluster of machines — handles **auto-scaling**, **self-healing**, **rolling updates**, **service discovery**, and **load balancing**.
- **How it does it:** Uses **YAML manifests** describing *Pods*, *Deployments*, *Services*, *Ingress*, etc. The cluster has a **Control Plane** (master) and **Worker Nodes** (minions).
- **Core building blocks:**
  - **Pod:** Smallest deployable unit, holds 1+ tightly coupled containers.
  - **Deployment:** Manages a replicated set of Pods.
  - **Service:** Stable network endpoint to access a set of Pods.
  - **Namespace:** Virtual cluster for resource isolation.
  - **kubectl:** CLI to interact with the cluster API.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | **Terraform** | **Ansible** | **Docker** | **Kubernetes** |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Primary Function** | Provisioning | Configuration | Containerization | Orchestration |
| **Language** | HCL (`.tf`) | YAML (`.yml`) | Dockerfile + YAML | YAML manifests |
| **Execution Style** | Declarative | Procedural / Task-based | Imperative + Declarative | Declarative |
| **Architecture** | Client-only | Agentless (uses SSH) | Client-Server (Docker Engine) | Master-Worker (Control Plane + Nodes) |
| **State Management** | Maintains a **State File** (`terraform.tfstate`) | Stateless (idempotent tasks) | Layered image filesystem | etcd key-value store |
| **Idempotency** | Yes (via state diff) | Yes (modules check before applying) | Yes (image immutable) | Yes (reconciliation loop) |
| **Cloud Agnostic** | Yes (2000+ providers) | Yes (works on any SSH-reachable host) | Yes (any OS with Docker) | Yes (runs on any cluster) |
| **Typical Command** | `terraform apply` | `ansible-playbook site.yml` | `docker run -d -p 80:80 nginx` | `kubectl apply -f deploy.yaml` |
| **Invented by** | HashiCorp (2014) | Red Hat (2012) | Solomon Hykes / dotCloud (2013) | Google (2014) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Table Matters for KTU 2024:** Questions frequently ask *"Compare Terraform and Ansible"* or *"Differentiate between Docker and Kubernetes"*. Memorize the **primary function** and **execution style** for each tool — that is worth 4-6 marks by itself.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking & FinTech:** Kubernetes runs thousands of microservices handling real-time transactions with strict SLAs.
- **E-Commerce (Flipkart, Amazon):** Terraform manages regional cloud infrastructure; Docker ensures identical behavior from dev to production.
- **Telecom (Jio, Airtel):** Ansible automates configuration of millions of network devices.
- **Healthcare (NABH-compliant systems):** IaC guarantees audit trails — every infrastructure change is versioned in Git.

## 2.4 Idempotency — The Unifying Principle

All four tools are built around a single mathematical property: **idempotency**.

$$f(f(x)) = f(x)$$

Applying the configuration 1 time = applying it 100 times. This is why IaC is safe to re-run in CI/CD pipelines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation: The IaC Pipeline State Transition

Let us model an IaC deployment as a **state machine**. Each tool transitions the system from one state to another.

### Analytical State Model

$$\text{System State} = \{S_0, S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_n\}$$

Where:
- $S_0$ = Bare-metal / empty cloud account.
- $S_1$ = Provisioned VMs (after Terraform).
- $S_2$ = Configured OS + software (after Ansible).
- $S_3$ = Packaged application in containers (after Docker).
- $S_4$ = Running, scalable, self-healing application (after Kubernetes).

The transition function for each tool is:

$$T_{\text{Terraform}}: S_0 \rightarrow S_1$$
$$T_{\text{Ansible}}: S_1 \rightarrow S_2$$
$$T_{\text{Docker}}: S_2 \rightarrow S_3$$
$$T_{\text{Kubernetes}}: S_3 \rightarrow S_4$$

The **full IaC pipeline** is the composition:

$$T_{\text{Full}} = T_{\text{K8s}} \circ T_{\text{Docker}} \circ T_{\text{Ansible}} \circ T_{\text{Terraform}}$$

Applied to the initial state:

$$T_{\text{Full}}(S_0) = S_4$$

### Step-by-Step Transition Logic

**Step 1 — Terraform Stage ($S_0 \rightarrow S_1$):**
The developer writes a declarative HCL file:

```hcl
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t2.micro"
  count         = 3
}
```

The text row explains: **Conversion logic** — Terraform translates HCL into API calls to AWS. The `count = 3` means it will provision 3 EC2 instances. The state file records the *current* real-world state, and on re-run, Terraform diff's desired vs. current state.

**Step 2 — Ansible Stage ($S_1 \rightarrow S_2$):**
A YAML playbook is executed against the 3 instances:

```yaml
- hosts: webservers
  tasks:
    - name: Install Nginx
      apt: name=nginx state=present
    - name: Start Nginx
      service: name=nginx state=started
```

The text row explains: **Conversion logic** — Ansible connects via SSH to each host in the `webservers` inventory group, installs the `nginx` package using the `apt` module, and starts the service. Idempotency check: if Nginx is already installed, no change occurs.

**Step 3 — Docker Stage ($S_2 \rightarrow S_3$):**
A Dockerfile builds an image:

```dockerfile
FROM ubuntu:22.04
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y nginx
COPY index.html /var/www/html/
CMD ["nginx", "-g", "daemon off;"]
```

The text row explains: **Conversion logic** — `FROM` sets the base image, `RUN` executes shell commands during build, `COPY` adds local files, `CMD` specifies the default run command. The image is stored in layers — each instruction is a layer — and is fully self-contained.

**Step 4 — Kubernetes Stage ($S_3 \rightarrow S_4$):**
A deployment manifest runs the image:

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-app
spec:
  replicas: 5
  selector:
    matchLabels: { app: web }
  template:
    metadata:
      labels: { app: web }
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web
        image: my-nginx:v1
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
```

The text row explains: **Conversion logic** — Kubernetes' **reconciliation loop** constantly compares *desired state* (5 replicas) with *actual state* (current pod count). If a pod crashes, a new one is auto-created; if traffic surges, Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) adds more.

### Analytical Matrix — Tool Responsibilities

| Stage | Tool | Input Artifact | Output Artifact | Failure Recovery |
|-------|------|---------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Terraform | `.tf` HCL files | Cloud resources | `terraform destroy` + re-apply |
| 2 | Ansible | `.yml` playbook | Configured servers | Re-run playbook (idempotent) |
| 3 | Docker | `Dockerfile` | Docker image | Rebuild image with new tag |
| 4 | Kubernetes | YAML manifests | Running Pods | Auto-restart via controller |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
