Here, **860% return** clearly justifies deploying the WAF. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Common Vulnerabilities | Vulnerability | CWE ID | Affected CIA Pillar | Primary Mitigation | DevOps Stage to Detect | |---|---|---|---|---| | SQL Injection | CWE-89 | Confidentiality, Integrity | Parameterized queries, ORMs | SAST (Static Analysis) | | XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) | CWE-79 | Confidentiality, Integrity | Output encoding, CSP headers | SAST + DAST | | CSRF | CWE-352 | Integrity | CSRF tokens, SameSite cookies | DAST (Dynamic Analysis) | | Buffer Overflow | CWE-120 | All three | ASLR, stack canaries, safe languages | Fuzz testing, code review | | Insecure Deserialization | CWE-502 | All three | Schema validation, signing | SAST, SCA | | Hardcoded Credentials | CWE-798 | Confidentiality | Secrets managers (Vault, AWS KMS) | Secret scanning (TruffleHog) | | Broken Access Control | CWE-284 | Confidentiality, Integrity | RBAC, deny-by-default | Authorization testing | | XXE (XML External Entity) | CWE-611 | Confidentiality | Disable external entities in parser | SAST, DAST | | SSRF | CWE-918 | Confidentiality | URL allowlists, network segmentation | DAST | | Insecure Randomness | CWE-338 | Confidentiality, Integrity | Use CSPRNG (e.g., `java.security.SecureRandom`) | SAST, code review | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** CWE = Common Weakness Enumeration. Knowing CWE IDs is a quick way to score extra marks in 14-mark answers. ## 3.3 Step-by-Step: How SQL Injection Works (Process Flow) 1. **Reconnaissance** → Attacker identifies an input field (e.g., login form). 2. **Probing** → Attacker enters a single quote `'` to test for error. 3. **Discovery** → If server returns a SQL error, the input is concatenated directly. 4. **Exploitation** → Attacker uses payload: `' OR '1'='1' -- ` 5. **Resulting Query** (vulnerable backend): ```sql SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '' OR '1'='1' -- ' AND password = 'anything'; ``` 6. **Outcome** → `OR '1'='1'` is always true, `--` comments out the rest. Authentication is bypassed. 7. **Data Exfiltration** → Attacker uses `UNION SELECT` to dump other tables. **Mitigation Steps:** - Use **parameterized queries / prepared statements**. - Employ **ORM frameworks** (Hibernate, SQLAlchemy). - Apply **input validation** (whitelist). - Use **least-privilege database accounts**. - Deploy a **Web Application Firewall (WAF)**. ## 3.4 Risk Assessment Matrix (5x5 Standard) | | **Impact: Low (1)** | **Impact: Medium (2)** | **Impact: High (3)** | **Impact: Critical (4)** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Likelihood: Very High (5)** | Medium (5) | High (10) | High (15) | Critical (20) | | **Likelihood: High (4)** | Medium (4) | Medium (8) | High (12) | High (16) | | **Likelihood: Medium (3)** | Low (3) | Medium (6) | Medium (9) | High (12) | | **Likelihood: Low (2)** | Low (2) | Low (4) | Medium (6) | Medium (8) | | **Likelihood: Very Low (1)** | Low (1) | Low (2) | Low (3) | Medium (4) | **Risk Score = Likelihood × Impact** > **Threshold Rule:** Score ≥ 15 → Immediate mitigation required. Score 8–14 → Mitigation planned. Score ≤ 7 → Accept or monitor. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The CIA Triad — Visual Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Software Security Foundations] --> B[Confidentiality] A --> C[Integrity] A --> D[Availability] B --> B1[Encryption AES TLS] B --> B2[Access Control RBAC] B --> B3[Data Masking] C --> C1[Hashing SHA-256] C --> C2[Digital Signatures] C --> C3[Audit Logs] D --> D1[Redundancy] D --> D2[Load Balancing] D --> D3[DR BCP Plans] style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff style C fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff style D fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff style B1 fill:#deebf7 style B2 fill:#deebf7 style B3 fill:#deebf7 style C1 fill:#deebf7 style C2 fill:#deebf7 style C3 fill:#deebf7 style D1 fill:#deebf7 style D2 fill:#deebf7 style D3 fill:#deebf7 ``` ## 4.2 DevSecOps Pipeline — Shift-Left Security Model ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Plan] --> P2[Code] --> P3[Build] --> P4[Test] --> P5[Release] --> P6[Deploy] --> P7[Monitor] P1 -.->|Threat Modeling| S1[Security Review] P2 -.->|Secret Scan| S2[SAST Pre-commit] P3 -.->|SCA Dependency Scan| S3[SBOM Generation] P4 -.->|DAST ZAP| S4[Pen Testing] P5 -.->|Sign Artifacts| S5[Integrity Check] P6 -.->|IaC Scan| S6[Container Scan] P7 -.->|SIEM Alerts| S7[RASP WAF Active] style S1 fill:#c00000,color:#fff style S2 fill:#c00000,color:#fff style S3 fill:#c00000,color:#fff style S4 fill:#c00000,color:#fff style S5 fill:#c00000,color:#fff style S6 fill:#c00000,color:#fff style S7 fill:#c00000,color:#fff style P1 fill:#70ad47,color:#fff style P2 fill:#70ad47,color:#fff style P3 fill:#70ad47,color:#fff style P4 fill:#70ad47,color:#fff style P5 fill:#70ad47,color:#fff style P6 fill:#70ad47,color:#fff style P7 fill:#70ad47,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Attack Anatomy — SQL Injection Process Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Browser participant W as Web Server participant DB as Database U->>W: GET /login?user=admin' OR '1'='1' -- W->>DB: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='admin' OR '1'='1' --' DB-->>W: Returns ALL user rows W-->>U: 200 OK Login success as admin Note over W,DB: No parameterized query
No input validation
No WAF in place ``` ## 4.4 OWASP Top 10 Classification Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB OWASP[OWASP Top 10 2021] OWASP --> C1[A01 Access Control] OWASP --> C2[A02 Crypto Failures] OWASP --> C3[A03 Injection] OWASP --> C4[A04 Insecure Design] OWASP --> C5[A05 Misconfiguration] OWASP --> C6[A06 Vulnerable Components] OWASP --> C7[A07 Auth Failures] OWASP --> C8[A08 Integrity Failures] OWASP --> C9[A09 Logging Failures] OWASP --> C10[A10 SSRF] C3 --> X1[SQL Injection] C3 --> X2[NoSQL Injection] C3 --> X3[Command Injection] C3 --> X4[LDAP Injection] C1 --> Y1[IDOR] C1 --> Y2[Privilege Escalation] C1 --> Y3[Missing Function Level Access] style OWASP fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff style C1 fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff style C2 fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff style C3 fill:#c00000,color:#fff style C4 fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff style C5 fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff style C6 fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff style C7 fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff style C8 fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff style C9 fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff style C10 fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff style X1 fill:#fff2cc style X2 fill:#fff2cc style X3 fill:#fff2cc style X4 fill:#fff2cc style Y1 fill:#deebf7 style Y2 fill:#deebf7 style Y3 fill:#deebf7 ``` ## 4.5 Defense-in-Depth Layered Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Attacker] -->|Layer 1| B[Perimeter Firewall WAF] B -->|Layer 2| C[Network Segmentation IDS IPS] C -->|Layer 3| D[Application Input Validation] D -->|Layer 4| E[Authentication MFA RBAC] E -->|Layer 5| F[Encryption At Rest In Transit] F -->|Layer 6| G[Audit Logging SIEM] G -->|Layer 7| H[Critical Data] style A fill:#c00000,color:#fff style B fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff style C fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff style D fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff style E fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff style F fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff style G fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff style H fill:#70ad47,color:#fff ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define the CIA Triad. [KTU University Exam — May 2023] **Model Answer:** The CIA Triad is the foundational model of information security, comprising three core principles: - **Confidentiality** — Ensuring that data is accessed only by authorized users. Achieved through encryption, access controls, and data classification. - **Integrity** — Ensuring that data is accurate and unaltered during storage or transit. Achieved through hashing (SHA-256), digital signatures, and audit logs. - **Availability** — Ensuring that systems and data are accessible when needed by legitimate users. Achieved through redundancy, load balancing, and DDoS protection. A breach of any one of these three properties constitutes a security incident. --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between Vulnerability, Threat, and Risk. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **Model Answer:** | Term | Definition | Example | |---|---|---| | **Vulnerability** | A weakness or flaw in the system that can be exploited | Unpatched Apache Log4j library | | **Threat** | Any potential cause of an unwanted incident | A hacker scanning for vulnerable servers | | **Risk** | The probability and impact of a threat exploiting a vulnerability | 60% chance of data breach costing ₹50 lakh in a year | > **Formula:** $Risk = Probability \times Impact$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A: (14 Marks Total) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model] **(a)** Explain the **OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities (2021 edition)** with a brief description of each. Mention at least one mitigation strategy for the top 3. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the importance of integrating security in the **Agile/DevOps lifecycle**. Explain the concept of **DevSecOps** and **shift-left security** with a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Answer for (a): The **Open Web Application Security Project (OWASP) Top 10 (2021)** represents the most critical security risks to web applications: 1. **A01 — Broken Access Control:** Users can act outside intended permissions (e.g., regular user accessing admin pages). *Mitigation:* Implement RBAC, deny-by-default, log access control failures. 2. **A02 — Cryptographic Failures:** Weak or missing encryption of sensitive data. *Mitigation:* Use TLS 1.3 for transit, AES-256 for rest, never store passwords in plain text — use bcrypt/Argon2. 3. **A03 — Injection:** Untrusted input is sent to interpreters (SQL, OS, LDAP). *Mitigation:* Use parameterized queries, ORMs, input validation, escape special characters. 4. **A04 — Insecure Design:** Architectural flaws that cannot be fixed by good coding. 5. **A05 — Security Misconfiguration:** Default credentials, open S3 buckets, verbose errors. 6. **A06 — Vulnerable and Outdated Components:** Using libraries with known CVEs. 7. **A07 — Identification and Authentication Failures:** Weak passwords, missing MFA. 8. **A08 — Software and Data Integrity Failures:** Unsigned updates, insecure CI/CD. 9. **A09 — Security Logging and Monitoring Failures:** No alerts on breaches. 10. **A10 — Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF):** Server fetches remote URLs without validation. **Top 3 Mitigations in Detail:** - **SQL Injection:** Use prepared statements like `PreparedStatement pstmt = conn.prepareStatement("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?"); pstmt.setInt(1, userId);` - **XSS:** Apply output encoding (`<`, `>`), enable **Content Security Policy (CSP)** headers. - **Broken Access Control:** Implement centralized authorization checks; never trust client-side IDs. --- ### Model Answer for (b): In traditional **waterfall development**, security was bolted on at the end (penetration testing before release). In **Agile**, where deployments happen every sprint, this approach is fatal. **DevSecOps** integrates security as a shared responsibility across development, security, and operations teams. **Key Principles of DevSecOps:** 1. **Shift-Left Security:** Security testing starts at requirements and design, not deployment. Threat modeling in sprint planning. 2. **Automation:** Security scans (SAST, DAST, SCA) are part of the CI/CD pipeline. Tools: SonarQube, OWASP ZAP, Snyk, Trivy. 3. **Continuous Monitoring:** Runtime application self-protection (RASP), SIEM, anomaly detection. 4. **Compliance as Code:** Policies (e.g., Open Policy Agent) enforce security at deployment. 5. **Immutable Infrastructure:** Containers and infrastructure-as-code reduce configuration drift. **Diagram (Draw this in exam):** ``` Plan → Code → Build → Test → Release → Deploy → Monitor | | | | | | | Threat Secret SCA DAST Sign IaC SIEM Model Scan WAF Artifacts Scan Alerts ``` **Benefits:** Reduced MTTD/MTTR, faster compliance audits, fewer production incidents, lower remediation cost (fixing in design is 100× cheaper than in production). --- ### QUESTION B: (14 Marks Total) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] **(a)** What is **SQL Injection**? Explain with a suitable example how it works. List **four** mitigation techniques. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A hospital stores patient records valued at ₹1,00,00,000. A ransomware attack would encrypt 80% of the data. Such attacks occur 3 times per year. The cost of deploying endpoint detection and response (EDR) is ₹8,00,000 per year and reduces risk by 90%. Calculate **SLE, ALE, and ROSI**. Should the hospital invest in EDR? Justify. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Answer for (a): **SQL Injection (SQLi)** is a code injection technique where an attacker inserts malicious SQL statements into an input field that is later passed to a backend database for execution. It is listed as **OWASP A03: Injection**. **Working Example — Vulnerable Login Form:** ```java String query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '" + request.getParameter("user") + "' AND password = '" + request.getParameter("pwd") + "'"; Statement stmt = connection.createStatement(); ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery(query); ``` **Attacker Input:** - Username: `' OR '1'='1' -- ` - Password: `anything` **Resulting Query:** ```sql SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '' OR '1'='1' -- ' AND password = 'anything'; ``` The condition `OR '1'='1'` is always true, and `--` comments out the rest. The attacker logs in as the first user (often admin). **Four Mitigation Techniques:** 1. **Parameterized Queries / Prepared Statements** — The input is treated as data, not executable code. 2. **Stored Procedures** — Pre-compiled SQL with parameters. 3. **Input Validation (Whitelisting)** — Reject inputs not matching expected patterns (e.g., alphanumeric only for usernames). 4. **ORM Frameworks** — Hibernate, SQLAlchemy abstract SQL and prevent concatenation. 5. (Bonus) **Least Privilege DB Accounts** — App user should not have DROP/DELETE rights. 6. (Bonus) **Web Application Firewall (WAF)** — Filters known SQLi patterns. --- ### Model Answer for (b): **Given Data:** - Asset Value (AV) = ₹1,00,00,000 - Exposure Factor (EF) = 80% = 0.80 - ARO = 3 attacks/year - Cost of Control (CoC) = ₹8,00,000/year - Risk Reduction = 90% **Step 1: Calculate Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** $$SLE = Asset\ Value \times Exposure\ Factor$$ $$SLE = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.80 = 80{,}00{,}000$$ *Explanation:* A single ransomware attack destroys data worth ₹80 lakh. **Step 2: Calculate Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) Before Control** $$ALE_{before} = SLE \times ARO$$ $$ALE_{before} = 80{,}00{,}000 \times 3 = 2{,}40{,}00{,}000$$ *Explanation:* Expected annual loss is ₹2.4 crore without protection. **Step 3: Calculate ALE After Control (90% reduction)** $$ALE_{after} = 2{,}40{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.90) = 24{,}00{,}000$$ *Explanation:* Residual annual risk is ₹24 lakh. **Step 4: Calculate ROSI** $$ROSI = \frac{(ALE_{before} - ALE_{after}) - Cost\ of\ Control}{Cost\ of\ Control}$$ $$ROSI = \frac{(2{,}40{,}00{,}000 - 24{,}00{,}000) - 8{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000}$$ $$ROSI = \frac{2{,}16{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} = \frac{2{,}08{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} = 26$$ $$ROSI\ \% = 26 \times 100 = 2600\%$$ **Conclusion:** Yes, the hospital **must** invest in EDR. The ROSI is a massive **2600%**, meaning for every ₹1 spent, ₹26 of risk is avoided. This is an excellent security investment that also satisfies **HIPAA** compliance requirements for healthcare data. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > 1. **Confusing Threat with Risk** — Threat is the *what could happen*; Risk is the *probability × impact*. Mixing them loses 1-2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting Units in Risk Calculations** — Always write ₹, %, or year alongside numbers. Examiners deduct marks for vague answers. > 3. **Listing OWASP items without mitigation** — KTU wants the **defense**, not just the attack. > 4. **Writing SQLi code without showing the *resulting* malicious query** — A "before-after" comparison scores full marks. > 5. **Spelling CIA as "CIA Triad" correctly** but then writing "Authentication" for A — don't confuse CIA with the AAA (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting). > 6. **Skipping the diagram in DevSecOps questions** — Even a hand-drawn DevOps loop with security callouts scores 2-3 extra marks. > 7. **Forgetting the 2021 edition year of OWASP** — Examiners may deduct a mark for an outdated 2017 list. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision — Read 5 Times Before Exam:** - ✅ **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — define each with one example. - ✅ **Vulnerability ≠ Threat ≠ Risk** — Use the house analogy: broken window / burglar / probability of theft. - ✅ **OWASP Top 10 (2021)** — Memorize the order from A01 to A10. A03 (Injection) is the most tested. - ✅ **SQL Injection** — Always explain with the `' OR '1'='1' --` payload and show the *resolved* SQL. - ✅ **XSS types** = Stored, Reflected, DOM-based. Mitigation = output encoding + CSP. - ✅ **CSRF mitigation** = CSRF tokens + SameSite=Strict cookies. - ✅ **DevSecOps** = Security as a shared responsibility; SAST, DAST, SCA in CI/CD. - ✅ **Shift-Left Security** = Find bugs early, when cost of fix is 100× lower. - ✅ **Saltzer-Schroeder Principles** = Least Privilege, Defense in Depth, Fail Securely (these are guaranteed short-answer questions). - ✅ **Risk Formula** = $Risk = Probability \times Impact$ — Memorize. - ✅ **SLE** = $Asset\ Value \times EF$ - ✅ **ALE** = $SLE \times ARO$ - ✅ **ROSI** = $\frac{(ALE_{before} - ALE_{after}) - CoC}{CoC}$ — Be ready for a 7-mark numerical. - ✅ **Buffer Overflow** mitigation = ASLR, stack canaries, modern languages (Rust, Go, Java). - ✅ **IDOR mitigation** = Use UUIDs instead of sequential IDs, enforce server-side authorization on every request. - ✅ **Insecure Deserialization** (CWE-502) — A08: integrity failures — sign or validate serialized data. - ✅ **Password Storage** — NEVER plain text or MD5. Use **bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2** with salt. - ✅ **HTTPS Everywhere** — TLS 1.3 is the current standard; TLS 1.0/1.1 are deprecated. - ✅ **MFA** — Multi-Factor Authentication is the single most effective control against A07. - ✅ **Compliance Frameworks** = ISO 27001, SOC 2, GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, India IT Act 2000. - ✅ **CVE** = Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures; **CVSS** scores 0.0–10.0. - ✅ **NIST Cybersecurity Framework** = Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover (5 functions). - ✅ **Defense in Depth** = Multiple layers; one control failing should not cause total breach. - ✅ **Pen Testing** = Black-box, White-box, Grey-box — know the difference.

topics.title: Secure Coding Practices: OWASP Top 10 and Secure Coding Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT742 - SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT WITH AGILE AND DEVOPS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Secure Coding Practices: OWASP Top 10 and Secure Coding Standards # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition — Secure Coding Practices > [!IMPORTANT] > **Secure Coding** is the practice of writing software in a way that guards against the accidental introduction of security vulnerabilities, by applying a systematic set of coding standards, design principles, defensive programming techniques, and continuous validation throughout the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC). The **Open Web Application Security Project (OWASP)** is a non-profit foundation that produces freely-available articles, methodologies, documentation, tools, and technologies in the field of **application security (AppSec)**. Its flagship publication, the **OWASP Top 10**, is a standard awareness document representing a broad consensus on the most critical security risks to web applications, refreshed approximately every 3–4 years. The current edition is **OWASP Top 10 : 2021**. A **Secure Coding Standard** is a set of rules and guidelines (e.g., **CERT Secure Coding Standards**, **OWASP Secure Coding Guidelines**, **SEI CERT C/C++/Java**) that prescribe how source code must be structured, validated, and documented to minimize exploitable weaknesses. These standards are mandatory references in regulated industries (Banking, Healthcare, Defense) and are evaluated by auditors against compliance benchmarks like **NIST SSDF (SP 800-218)** and **ISO/IEC 27001**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Vault Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: Building a Bank vs. Building a Web App** Imagine you are commissioned to build a **bank vault**: * The **business logic** is the vault's primary function — storing valuables. * The **walls, locks, and alarms** are the *security controls*. In software, these are authentication, encryption, access control, and input validation. * **Secure coding** means: the architect never leaves the back door open, never uses cheap wood for the door frame, never publishes the lock combination on Instagram, and never relies on the honor system for access. If a developer writes code without secure coding practices, it is like building a vault with **a glass door, a default PIN of "0000", and a sign that reads "crack me here."** A single SQL Injection, a missing `if` check, or a hardcoded password can collapse the entire vault — even if the rest of the application is beautifully engineered. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** > In Agile + DevOps environments, secure coding is operationalized through **Security-as-Code** — embedding security checks directly into **CI/CD pipelines** (SAST, DAST, SCA, IaC scanning) so that vulnerabilities are caught *before* deployment, not after a breach. ## 1.3 Why Secure Coding Matters in Agile & DevOps Modern DevOps pipelines deploy code **multiple times per day**. Without secure coding: * Vulnerabilities discovered in production require hotfixes (costly and slow). * They bypass traditional quarterly security reviews. * They propagate through **microservices, containers, and APIs** at scale. Hence, secure coding is the **first line of defense** in the **DevSecOps** triad (Development + Security + Operations). --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The OWASP Top 10 : 2021 — Operational Breakdown Each entry of the OWASP Top 10 follows a standard documentation pattern: * **Threat Agents / Attack Vectors** — *Who* exploits it and *How*. * **Security Weakness** — *Where* in code/system the flaw exists. * **Impact** — *What* the business consequence is. * **Recommendations / Mitigations** — *What* to do about it. ### 2.1.1 A01:2021 — Broken Access Control **Core Idea:** Users can act outside their intended permissions. **'Why' (Root Cause):** Authorization is checked incorrectly, lazily, or only at UI layer (not at server-side business logic). **'How' to prevent:** 1. Deny by default (whitelist, not blacklist). 2. Implement **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** or **Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)**. 3. Enforce least privilege at API gateways. 4. Disable directory listings; never expose object IDs in URLs without ownership verification. ### 2.1.2 A02:2021 — Cryptographic Failures **Core Idea:** Sensitive data is unprotected due to weak/missing encryption. **'How' to prevent:** 1. Use **TLS 1.3** for data in transit; **AES-256-GCM** for data at rest. 2. Never invent custom cryptographic algorithms. 3. Use salted password hashing: **bcrypt**, **Argon2id**, or **scrypt** — never **MD5/SHA-1** for passwords. 4. Manage keys via **KMS / HSM**, never hardcoded. ### 2.1.3 A03:2021 — Injection **Core Idea:** Untrusted input is interpreted as code/commands. **Variants:** SQL Injection, NoSQL Injection, OS Command Injection, LDAP Injection, XPath Injection. **'How' to prevent:** 1. **Parameterized queries / prepared statements** (the #1 mitigation). 2. **ORM frameworks** with proper escaping. 3. **Input validation** (whitelisting) at the boundary. 4. **Output encoding** when displaying user data in HTML/JS/URLs. ### 2.1.4 A04:2021 — Insecure Design **Core Idea:** Flaws in the *design* phase, not in code. Cannot be fixed by better code alone. **'How' to prevent:** 1. **Threat modeling** (STRIDE, PASTA) during sprint planning. 2. **Security user stories** and **abuse cases** in the backlog. 3. **Reference architectures** vetted for security. 4. Use the **OWASP ASVS (Application Security Verification Standard)** for design-time requirements. ### 2.1.5 A05:2021 — Security Misconfiguration **Core Idea:** Default settings, open admin panels, verbose error messages, unnecessary features enabled. **'How' to prevent:** 1. Harden the **CI/CD** and runtime images (minimal base images). 2. Disable **directory listings, sample apps, debug endpoints** in production. 3. Automate configuration via **Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC)** with policy scanning (e.g., Checkov, tfsec). 4. Apply principle of least privilege to **cloud IAM roles**. ### 2.1.6 A06:2021 — Vulnerable and Outdated Components **Core Idea:** Using libraries with known CVEs (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures). **'How' to prevent:** 1. Maintain a **Software Bill of Materials (SBOM)** — mandatory under **US Executive Order 14028**. 2. Use **Software Composition Analysis (SCA)** tools: Snyk, Dependabot, OWASP Dependency-Check. 3. Patch regularly; subscribe to **CVE feeds**. ### 2.1.7 A07:2021 — Identification and Authentication Failures **Core Idea:** Weak login mechanisms, credential stuffing, session hijacking. **'How' to prevent:** 1. Implement **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)**. 2. Use **rate limiting** and **CAPTCHA** to prevent credential stuffing. 3. Secure **session tokens** (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookies). 4. Enforce strong password policies + **NIST SP 800-63B** guidelines. ### 2.1.8 A08:2021 — Software and Data Integrity Failures **Core Idea:** Code or data updates are not verified, allowing supply-chain attacks (e.g., **SolarWinds, Log4Shell, Codecov breach**). **'How' to prevent:** 1. Use **digital signatures** to verify software updates. 2. Use **SLSA (Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts)** framework. 3. Verify **CI/CD pipeline integrity** (e.g., signed commits, MFA on prod deploys). ### 2.1.9 A09:2021 — Security Logging and Monitoring Failures **Core Idea:** Insufficient logs make breach detection take **>200 days on average** (IBM 2023 report). **'How' to prevent:** 1. Log authentication, access control, and validation failures centrally. 2. Integrate with **SIEM** (Splunk, ELK, Azure Sentinel). 3. Set up **alerts and incident response playbooks**. ### 2.1.10 A10:2021 — Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) **Core Idea:** Attacker coerces the server to make HTTP requests to internal resources. **'How' to prevent:** 1. Whitelist allowed domains/IPs. 2. Disable **HTTP redirects** in outbound requests. 3. Use **network segmentation** to isolate internal services. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Secure Coding Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarizes the **CERT / OWASP recommended coding practices** that are frequently tested in KTU ESE. | # | Vulnerability Class | Safe Pattern (Use) | Dangerous Pattern (Avoid) | KTU Exam Weight | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | SQL Injection | `PreparedStatement` with `?` placeholders | String concatenation of user input into SQL | **Very High** | | 2 | XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) | Output encoding via framework escaping (e.g., React auto-escapes) | `innerHTML = userInput` | High | | 3 | CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) | Anti-CSRF tokens (synchronizer pattern) | GET requests that mutate state | Medium | | 4 | Insecure Deserialization | Use JSON with schema validation | Java `ObjectInputStream` on untrusted data | Medium | | 5 | Buffer Overflow | Bounded buffer APIs, language-managed memory | `strcpy`, `gets` in C/C++ | Medium | | 6 | Hardcoded Secrets | Environment variables / Secrets Manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) | `password = "admin123"` in source | **Very High** | | 7 | Weak Crypto | AES-256, RSA-2048+, TLS 1.3, Argon2id | MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, ECB mode | High | | 8 | Broken Auth | MFA, OAuth 2.0 + OIDC, password hashing with salt | Plain-text passwords, session IDs in URLs | High | | 9 | Path Traversal | Canonicalize paths + whitelist filenames | `new File(userInput)` | Medium | | 10 | Logging Failures | Log to central SIEM with structured fields | Catching exception and `e.printStackTrace()` | Low | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industry Adoption Map for Secure Coding Standards** > * **CERT C / CERT C++ / CERT Java** → Used by **DOD, automotive (MISRA derivative), avionics, embedded systems**. > * **OWASP ASVS** → Used in **fintech and e-commerce** as a verifiable checklist. > * **PCI-DSS Requirement 6.3** → Mandates secure coding for any code handling credit cards. > * **HIPAA Security Rule** → Requires secure coding for healthcare (PHI protection). > * **NIST SSDF (SP 800-218)** → US Federal procurement baseline. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Mitigation Walkthrough ## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Vulnerabilities to Defenses Since this topic is qualitative (not numerical), the "derivation" is replaced by an **analytical walk-through** of how a typical Agile team transforms a vulnerability into a **secure coding rule** within a sprint. ### Step 1: Identify — Threat Modeling (STRIDE per Element) STRIDE is a Microsoft-developed mnemonic for **threat classification**: $$\text{STRIDE} = \{S, T, R, I, D, E\}$$ Where: * **S** — Spoofing (identity forgery) * **T** — Tampering (data modification) * **R** — Repudiation (denial of action) * **I** — Information Disclosure * **D** — Denial of Service * **E** — Elevation of Privilege > For each **Data Flow Diagram (DFD) element**, the team tags applicable STRIDE threats during sprint planning. ### Step 2: Codify — Write the Secure Coding Rule **Example Rule (S-1)**: *"All SQL queries MUST use parameterized statements; raw concatenation is forbidden."* This rule becomes a **linting check** (e.g., Semgrep, SonarQube rule `java:S2077`). ### Step 3: Automate — Embed in CI/CD The rule is enforced in the pipeline: $$ \text{Pipeline Gate} = \begin{cases} \text{FAIL} & \text{if SAST finds } \geq 1 \text{ High/Critical CVE} \\ \text{PASS} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ ### Step 4: Validate — Penetration Testing in Staging A **DAST** (Dynamic Application Security Testing) tool — OWASP ZAP — probes the running app for the same class of bugs. ### Step 5: Monitor — Production Telemetry **RASP** (Runtime Application Self-Protection) or **WAF** rules monitor for exploitation attempts. ## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example: Mitigating SQL Injection (A03) We demonstrate the **transformation from insecure to secure code**, then derive the **risk score reduction**. ### Step A: Insecure Code (Vulnerable) ```java String query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '" + userInput + "' AND password = '" + passInput + "'"; Statement stmt = connection.createStatement(); ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery(query); ``` **Risk:** An attacker inputs `admin' --` as username, bypassing authentication. The SQL becomes: $$ \text{SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = 'admin' --' AND password = '...'} $$ The `--` comments out the rest, granting unauthorized access. ### Step B: Secure Code (Mitigated) ```java String query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = ? AND password = ?"; PreparedStatement pstmt = connection.prepareStatement(query); pstmt.setString(1, userInput); pstmt.setString(2, passInput); ResultSet rs = pstmt.executeQuery(); ``` The user input is bound as a **parameter** — the database treats it strictly as a value, never as SQL syntax. ### Step C: Quantitative Risk Derivation (CVSS-style) We use the **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) v3.1** Base Score formula conceptually: $$ \text{BaseScore} = \text{round}(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\ 10)) $$ Where for SQL Injection in an auth context: * **Impact** = 6.42 (Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability all High) * **Exploitability** = 2.8 (Network, Low complexity, No privileges) $$ \text{BaseScore}_{\text{pre-fix}} = \text{round}(\min(6.42 + 2.8,\ 10)) = \text{round}(9.22) = 9.3 \;\; (\text{Critical}) $$ After applying parameterized query + input validation + WAF: * **Impact** drops to 2.7 (compensating controls absorb the risk) * **Exploitability** drops to 1.6 (WAF blocks attack pattern) $$ \text{BaseScore}_{\text{post-fix}} = \text{round}(\min(2.7 + 1.6,\ 10)) = \text{round}(4.3) = 4.3 \;\; (\text{Medium}) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Risk Reduction:** 9.3 → 4.3 ⇒ ~54% reduction in quantified risk, dropping severity from *Critical* to *Medium*. This kind of calculation justifies the business ROI of secure coding to non-technical stakeholders. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Tree — When to Use Which Mitigation $$ \text{If vulnerability} = \text{SQLi} \Rightarrow \text{Use parameterized queries} + \text{ORM} + \text{WAF rule} $$ $$ \text{If vulnerability} = \text{XSS} \Rightarrow \text{Use framework auto-escaping} + \text{CSP headers} + \text{sanitization libs} $$ $$ \text{If vulnerability} = \text{IDOR} \Rightarrow \text{Use UUIDs} + \text{server-side ownership check on every fetch} $$ $$ \text{If vulnerability} = \text{SSRF} \Rightarrow \text{Use URL whitelist} + \text{disable redirects} + \text{network isolation} $$ --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — OWASP Top 10 2021 Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD O1["OWASP TOP 10 2021"] O1 --> A01["A01 BROKEN ACCESS CONTROL"] O1 --> A02["A02 CRYPTOGRAPHIC FAILURES"] O1 --> A03["A03 INJECTION"] O1 --> A04["A04 INSECURE DESIGN"] O1 --> A05["A05 SECURITY MISCONFIGURATION"] O1 --> A06["A06 VULNERABLE COMPONENTS"] O1 --> A07["A07 AUTH FAILURES"] O1 --> A08["A08 INTEGRITY FAILURES"] O1 --> A09["A09 LOGGING FAILURES"] O1 --> A10["A10 SSRF"] A03 --> A03a["SQL INJECTION"] A03 --> A03b["XSS"] A03 --> A03c["OS COMMAND INJECTION"] A03 --> A03d["LDAP XPATH INJECTION"] A07 --> A07a["CREDENTIAL STUFFING"] A07 --> A07b["WEAK SESSION MGMT"] A07 --> A07c["MISSING MFA"] A08 --> A08a["SUPPLY CHAIN ATTACK"] A08 --> A08b["UNSIGNED UPDATES"] A08 --> A08c["INSECURE CI CD"] style O1 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style A01 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style A02 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style A03 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style A04 fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff style A05 fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff style A06 fill:#ed7d31,color:#ffffff style A07 fill:#ffc000,color:#000000 style A08 fill:#ffc000,color:#000000 style A09 fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff style A10 fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Secure SDLC (DevSecOps) Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR PLAN["1 PLAN
Threat Modeling
Security Stories"] --> CODE["2 CODE
Secure Coding Standards
Peer Review"] CODE --> BUILD["3 BUILD
SAST Scan
SCA Dependency Check"] BUILD --> TEST["4 TEST
DAST Scan
Pen Test"] TEST --> DEPLOY["5 DEPLOY
IaC Policy Scan
Image Signing"] DEPLOY --> OPERATE["6 OPERATE
RASP
WAF Monitoring"] OPERATE --> MONITOR["7 MONITOR
SIEM Alerts
Incident Response"] MONITOR -.feedback.-> PLAN style PLAN fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style CODE fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff style BUILD fill:#548235,color:#ffffff style TEST fill:#548235,color:#ffffff style DEPLOY fill:#bf8f00,color:#ffffff style OPERATE fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff style MONITOR fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Defensive Layers (Defense in Depth) ```mermaid graph TB USER["USER INPUT"] --> VAL["L1 INPUT VALIDATION
Whitelist Schema Check"] VAL --> AUTH["L2 AUTHENTICATION
MFA OAuth 2.0"] AUTH --> AUTZ["L3 AUTHORIZATION
RBAC ABAC Least Privilege"] AUTZ --> ENC["L4 ENCRYPTION
TLS 1.3 AES 256"] ENC --> LOG["L5 AUDIT LOGGING
SIEM Correlation"] LOG --> DATA["PROTECTED DATASTORE"] style USER fill:#d9d9d9 style VAL fill:#fff2cc style AUTH fill:#fff2cc style AUTZ fill:#fff2cc style ENC fill:#fff2cc style LOG fill:#fff2cc style DATA fill:#a9d08e ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Each layer is independent. A failure in one (e.g., bypassed authentication) does not collapse the others — this is **Defense in Depth**, a fundamental secure design principle. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define Secure Coding. List any FOUR secure coding standards. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** Secure Coding is the practice of developing software with defensive techniques to prevent security vulnerabilities. The four widely-used secure coding standards are: 1. **OWASP Secure Coding Guidelines** — Web application focused. 2. **CERT Secure Coding Standards** — Language-specific (C, C++, Java, Python, Perl). 3. **MISRA C/C++** — Mandatory for automotive and embedded safety-critical systems. 4. **NIST SSDF (SP 800-218)** — US Federal baseline for secure software development. > *(0.5 marks for definition + 0.5 × 4 = 2 marks for standards + 0.5 for any one-line elaboration.)* ### Q2. What is OWASP Top 10? Why is it updated periodically? `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **OWASP Top 10** is a standard awareness document listing the **ten most critical web application security risks**, published by the Open Web Application Security Project. It is updated periodically (2010, 2013, 2017, 2021, upcoming 2025) because: 1. The **threat landscape evolves** — new attack classes emerge (e.g., SSRF was added in 2021; Insecure Design replaced XXE-related entries). 2. **Technology stacks change** — cloud-native and API-first architectures introduce new risk categories. 3. **Data-driven prioritization** — Updated based on contributed vulnerability data from hundreds of organizations worldwide. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > * Students often *list* OWASP categories but **fail to explain the root cause** — examiners deduct 2–3 marks. > * Always pair each vulnerability with **one prevention technique** to score full marks. > * For 14-mark questions, include a **diagram or example code snippet** to qualify for the "presentation" mark. > * Avoid generic answers like "use encryption" — specify **algorithm, key length, and protocol** (e.g., *AES-256-GCM over TLS 1.3*). --- ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 #### (a) Explain the OWASP Top 10 2021 categories in detail. Discuss A01 (Broken Access Control) and A03 (Injection) with suitable examples. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** **OWASP Top 10 2021 — Overview:** The OWASP Top 10 is a globally-recognized ranking of the most critical web application security risks, derived from industry data and community consensus. The 2021 edition contains: A01 Broken Access Control, A02 Cryptographic Failures, A03 Injection, A04 Insecure Design, A05 Security Misconfiguration, A06 Vulnerable & Outdated Components, A07 Identification & Authentication Failures, A08 Software & Data Integrity Failures, A09 Security Logging & Monitoring Failures, A10 Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF). **A01 – Broken Access Control (3 marks):** Broken Access Control occurs when users can act outside their intended permissions — e.g., a regular user accessing an admin panel, or fetching another user's data by tampering with an ID parameter (an **IDOR – Insecure Direct Object Reference** attack). *Example:* `GET /api/users/1002/profile` — An attacker changes `1002` to `1003` and views another user's data if the server does not verify ownership. **Prevention:** 1. Deny access by default; use RBAC. 2. Enforce server-side ownership checks on every record access. 3. Disable directory listings and CORS misconfigurations. 4. Log access control failures and alert on repeated violations. **A03 – Injection (4 marks):** Injection flaws occur when untrusted data is sent to an interpreter as part of a command or query. The most common types are **SQL Injection, XSS, OS Command Injection, LDAP Injection**. *SQL Injection Example:* Input ` ' OR '1'='1` in a login field can bypass authentication by modifying the WHERE clause. *Prevention:* 1. Use **parameterized queries / prepared statements**. 2. Apply **whitelist input validation** at boundaries. 3. Use **ORM frameworks** with built-in escaping. 4. Apply **output encoding** for XSS contexts. 5. Use **LIMIT and other SQL safeguards** in queries to prevent mass data extraction. --- #### (b) Discuss the importance of Secure Coding Standards in Agile and DevOps. Explain how SAST, DAST, and SCA are integrated into a CI/CD pipeline. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** **Importance of Secure Coding Standards in Agile/DevOps (3 marks):** In Agile sprints, code is delivered in 2–4 week iterations. Without standardized secure coding rules, vulnerabilities accumulate rapidly across sprints. Secure coding standards: 1. Provide a **shared vocabulary** between developers, security engineers, and auditors. 2. Enable **automated enforcement** via linters and scanners. 3. Support **shift-left security** — defects are caught at the developer desktop, not in production. 4. Satisfy **regulatory compliance** (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, SOC 2). **CI/CD Security Integration (4 marks):** | Stage | Tool Type | Function | Example Tools | |---|---|---|---| | Developer IDE | Pre-commit hook / Linter | Detect secrets, syntax-level issues | `gitleaks`, `eslint-plugin-security` | | Source Code Commit | **SAST** (Static App Security Testing) | Analyzes source code without running it | SonarQube, Checkmarx, Semgrep | | Build | **SCA** (Software Composition Analysis) | Scans third-party dependencies for known CVEs | Snyk, OWASP Dependency-Check, Dependabot | | Test/Staging | **DAST** (Dynamic App Security Testing) | Black-box scans the running app | OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite, Invicti | | Container Build | **IaC & Image Scanning** | Scans Dockerfiles, Terraform, Kubernetes manifests | Trivy, tfsec, Checkov, Anchore | | Production | **RASP / WAF** | Runtime protection at app and network layer | Imperva RASP, AWS WAF, Cloudflare | | Post-deploy | **SIEM Monitoring** | Aggregates logs for threat detection | Splunk, ELK, Azure Sentinel | The **pipeline gate policy**: * **High/Critical CVE found** ⇒ Build **fails** automatically. * **Medium** ⇒ Warn + require security review sign-off. * **Low** ⇒ Log and track in backlog. This creates a **continuous security feedback loop** consistent with the **DevSecOps** philosophy. --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice) #### (a) Explain the categories A02 (Cryptographic Failures) and A05 (Security Misconfiguration) from OWASP Top 10 2021. Provide examples and prevention techniques. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Model Solution:** **A02 – Cryptographic Failures (3.5 marks):** Previously known as *Sensitive Data Exposure*, this category covers failures related to cryptography (or lack thereof) that lead to exposure of sensitive data such as credentials, PII, financial, or health data. *Example Scenarios:* * Storing passwords using **MD5** or **SHA-1** without salt. * Transmitting data over **HTTP** instead of HTTPS. * Using **deprecated TLS 1.0/1.1** ciphers. * Storing encryption keys in source code or config files. *Prevention:* 1. Classify data and apply controls per classification (e.g., PCI cardholder data must be encrypted at rest and in transit). 2. Use **TLS 1.3** for all data in transit. 3. Use **AES-256-GCM** for data at rest; **Argon2id** or **bcrypt** for password hashing. 4. Manage keys via **KMS/HSM** with proper rotation policies. 5. Disable weak ciphers in server configuration (e.g., RC4, 3DES, MD5-based MACs). **A05 – Security Misconfiguration (3.5 marks):** The most commonly seen issue — caused by insecure default configurations, open cloud storage, verbose error messages, or unused features left enabled. *Example Scenarios:* * AWS S3 bucket left public. * Default credentials (e.g., `admin/admin`) on Tomcat manager. * Stack traces exposed in production error pages leaking DB schema. * Unnecessary services (FTP, Telnet) running on production servers. *Prevention:* 1. Harden all environments using **CIS Benchmarks** (e.g., CIS Docker, CIS Kubernetes). 2. Automate configuration using **Infrastructure-as-Code** with policy-as-code scanning. 3. Disable **directory listings, sample applications, and debug endpoints** in production. 4. Apply **principle of least privilege** to cloud IAM roles. 5. Use a **single source of truth** for configs (e.g., Helm values, Terraform) — never edit prod manually. --- #### (b) What are Software and Data Integrity Failures (A08)? Explain SSRF (A10) with a real-world scenario. How can supply chain attacks be prevented? (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Model Solution:** **A08 – Software and Data Integrity Failures (2.5 marks):** This new category in 2021 focuses on assumptions about software updates, critical data, and CI/CD pipelines that lack integrity verification. The most famous example is the **SolarWinds (2020)** supply-chain attack, where attackers compromised the build pipeline and injected a backdoor into Orion software updates distributed to 18,000+ customers. *Other Examples:* * Insecure deserialization leading to Remote Code Execution. * Auto-update mechanisms without signature verification. * Untrusted plugins or CI/CD plugins installed from public registries. *Prevention:* 1. Use **digital signatures** to verify software updates (e.g., `cosign` for container images). 2. Adopt the **SLSA framework** (Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts) — levels 1–3. 3. Enforce **MFA on production deployments** and **signed commits**. 4. Use **reproducible builds** to detect tampering. **A10 – Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) (2.5 marks):** SSRF occurs when a web application fetches a remote resource based on user-supplied URL without validating it. Attackers can pivot to internal services (cloud metadata, internal admin panels) that are not directly accessible from the internet. *Real-World Scenario:* The **Capital One breach (2019)** exploited SSRF on an AWS WAF to access the **EC2 Instance Metadata Service (IMDS)** at `169.254.169.254`, retrieving IAM role credentials and exfiltrating 100+ million customer records. *Prevention:* 1. **Whitelist** allowed domains and IP ranges; reject internal RFC 1918 addresses. 2. Disable **HTTP redirects** in outbound HTTP clients. 3. Use **network segmentation** — separate public and private subnets. 4. Enforce **IMDSv2** on AWS (session-based, not request-based). 5. Validate URL schemes (block `file://`, `gopher://`, `dict://`). **Supply Chain Attack Prevention (2 marks):** * Maintain an **SBOM** (Software Bill of Materials) using SPDX or CycloneDX formats. * Pin dependency versions; use **lock files** (`package-lock.json`, `Pipfile.lock`). * Subscribe to **CVE feeds**; use SCA tools in CI/CD. * Verify package signatures using `npm audit signatures` or Sigstore. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):** * ✅ **OWASP** = Open Web Application Security Project; **Top 10** is the awareness document. * ✅ Current edition: **OWASP Top 10 : 2021** (previous editions: 2010, 2013, 2017). * ✅ **A01:2021** (Broken Access Control) jumped from #5 in 2017 to #1 — caused 318k+ occurrences in contributed dataset. * ✅ **Injection** demoted from #1 to #3 due to widespread framework adoption, but still very dangerous. * ✅ **SSRF** is the newest category, added in 2021. * ✅ **A04 Insecure Design** is a *design-time* issue, cannot be patched by code — fix in architecture. * ✅ Always prefer **parameterized queries** for SQL; never string-concatenate. * ✅ Use **AES-256-GCM** for symmetric encryption; **Argon2id** for password hashing. * ✅ **Defense in Depth** = multiple independent security layers. * ✅ **Shift-Left Security** = test security early in SDLC, not after deployment. * ✅ **DevSecOps** = DevOps + Security embedded into every pipeline stage. * ✅ **SAST** = static code analysis (white-box); **DAST** = dynamic, runs against live app (black-box); **SCA** = third-party dependency scan. * ✅ **STRIDE** = Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Info Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege. * ✅ **CVSS** scores range 0.0–10.0: 0.1–3.9 Low, 4.0–6.9 Medium, 7.0–8.9 High, 9.0–10.0 Critical. * ✅ **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — the foundational security model. * ✅ **Least Privilege** = grant minimum permissions required; **Zero Trust** = never trust, always verify. * ✅ Secure coding standards: **OWASP Guidelines, CERT, MISRA, NIST SSDF (SP 800-218)**. * ✅ In Agile: convert threats into **security user stories** during sprint grooming. * ✅ In CI/CD: **fail the build** on High/Critical CVEs — security gate policy. * ✅ Famous breaches to remember: **SolarWinds (2020)**, **Capital One SSRF (2019)**, **Log4Shell (2021)**. > **End of Module 3 Notes — Secure Coding Practices: OWASP Top 10 and Secure Coding Standards**

topics.title: Threat Modeling: Identifying Threats and Security Design Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT742 - SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT WITH AGILE AND DEVOPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Threat Modeling: Identifying Threats and Security Design Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Threat Modeling?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Threat Modeling is a structured, systematic engineering process used during software design to **proactively identify, enumerate, prioritize, and mitigate potential security threats** that could compromise the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of a system. It answers four fundamental questions: *What are we building? What can go wrong? What are we going to do about it? Did we do a good job?*

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, threat modeling is positioned as a **design-phase activity** within the DevSecOps pipeline — not an afterthought. It is typically executed during the "Design" stage of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC), well before a single line of code is committed to the main branch.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The House Analogy

Imagine you are building a new house. Before construction begins, a security consultant walks the architectural blueprint and asks:

1. *Where are the valuable items?* (Assets)
2. *Which doors, windows, and walls can an intruder exploit?* (Entry Points / Attack Surface)
3. *What techniques might a burglar use — lock picking, window breaking, social engineering the gardener?* (Threats)
4. *What is the consequence if the master bedroom safe is cracked?* (Impact / Risk)

Threat modeling in software is exactly this — but the "house" is your microservice architecture, the "valuable items" are your databases and APIs, and the "burglar" is a malicious actor (hacker, insider, or botnet).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Frameworks (KTU syllabus focus):** The two dominant frameworks taught in KTU Module 3 are **STRIDE** (developed at Microsoft) and **DREAD** (risk quantification). Additionally, **Saltzer & Schroeder's Security Design Principles** provide foundational axioms for secure system design.

### 1.3 The Six STRIDE Threat Categories

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STRIDE Mnemonic (Microsoft, 1999):**
> - **S** — **Spoofing** (Identity violation)
> - **T** — **Tampering** (Data integrity violation)
> - **R** — **Repudiation** (Non-repudiation violation)
> - **I** — **Information Disclosure** (Confidentiality violation)
> - **D** — **Denial of Service** (Availability violation)
> - **E** — **Elevation of Privilege** (Authorization violation)

Each letter in **STRIDE** directly maps to one of the three pillars of information security known as the **CIA Triad** plus three additional properties:

| STRIDE Category | Violated Property | Example Attack |
|-----------------|-------------------|----------------|
| Spoofing | Authentication | Phishing, IP spoofing |
| Tampering | Integrity | SQL injection, MITM |
| Repudiation | Non-repudiation | Log deletion, unsigned transactions |
| Information Disclosure | Confidentiality | Heartbleed, data leaks |
| Denial of Service | Availability | DDoS, resource exhaustion |
| Elevation of Privilege | Authorization | Buffer overflow → root shell |

### 1.4 What Are Security Design Principles?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** Security Design Principles are time-tested, foundational axioms articulated by Jerome Saltzer and Michael Schroeder in their seminal 1975 paper *"The Protection of Information in Computer Systems"* that guide engineers in building systems which are *secure by design* rather than relying on bolt-on, after-the-fact security patches.

The eight original Saltzer-Schroeder principles, supplemented by modern additions like **Defense in Depth** and **Secure by Default**, form the philosophical backbone of secure system architecture.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Phases of Threat Modeling (Microsoft SDL Methodology)

Microsoft's Security Development Lifecycle (SDL) prescribes a **four-phase iterative loop** for threat modeling. KTU frequently tests this workflow in Part B questions.

**Phase 1 — Diagram the System (What are we building?)**

Engineers construct **Data Flow Diagrams (DFDs)** representing the system's:
- External entities (users, third-party APIs)
- Processes (microservices, daemons)
- Data stores (databases, queues, caches)
- Data flows (REST calls, message brokers)
- Trust boundaries (zones where privilege changes)

**Phase 2 — Identify Threats (What can go wrong?)**

Apply the **STRIDE** mnemonic to every DFD element. Each DFD element type maps to specific STRIDE categories:

| DFD Element | Applicable STRIDE Categories |
|-------------|------------------------------|
| External Entity | Spoofing, Repudiation |
| Process | Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege (ALL SIX) |
| Data Store | Tampering, Information Disclosure, DoS (sometimes Repudiation) |
| Data Flow | Tampering, Information Disclosure, DoS |

**Phase 3 — Document Threats**

Each identified threat is logged with:
- Threat ID
- Threat description (using STRIDE category)
- Affected asset / DFD element
- Attack technique (e.g., "Buffer overflow in /api/login")
- Mitigation strategy

**Phase 4 — Rate Threats (using DREAD or CVSS)**

Risks are quantified and prioritized. **DREAD** scores threats from 1 to 10 across five dimensions, producing an aggregate risk score.

### 2.2 DREAD Risk Rating Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DREAD Decomposition:** Damage Potential, Reproducibility, Exploitability, Affected Users, Discoverability — each rated 1 (Low) to 10 (Critical).

**Damage Potential (D):** How much harm is caused if the threat succeeds?
- 10 = Complete system / data destruction
- 5 = Sensitive data disclosure
- 1 = Minimal cosmetic impact

**Reproducibility (R):** How easily can the attack be reproduced?
- 10 = Always reproducible, even by a script kiddie
- 5 = Requires specific conditions or timing
- 1 = Extremely difficult or requires insider knowledge

**Exploitability (E):** How skilled / resourced must the attacker be?
- 10 = No technical skill required (e.g., typing a URL)
- 5 = Intermediate expertise required
- 1 = Deep expertise, custom zero-day exploit

**Affected Users (A):** What percentage of users are impacted?
- 10 = All users / entire customer base
- 5 = Partial subset (e.g., premium users)
- 1 = Negligible fraction

**Discoverability (D):** How easily can the vulnerability be found?
- 10 = Publicly advertised (e.g., exposed port)
- 5 = Discoverable via standard tools (Nmap, Burp Suite)
- 1 = Hidden, requires code audit

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Risk Quantification

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU Board Exams:**

| Equation ID | Formula | Description |
|-------------|---------|-------------|
| **R-DREAD** | $Risk = \dfrac{D + R + E + A + D_{isc}}{5}$ | DREAD aggregate score (range 1–10) |
| **R-Loss** | $Risk = Probability \times Impact$ | Classical risk equation (FAIR, ISO 31000) |
| **R-CIA** | $CIA = (C, I, A) \rightarrow \text{Triad Weight}$ | Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability |
| **ALE** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Annual Loss Expectancy (NIST SP 800-30) |
| **SLE** | $SLE = Asset\ Value \times Exposure\ Factor$ | Single Loss Expectancy |

For STRIDE ↔ CIA mapping, the canonical mapping is:

$$STRIDE = f(CIA) = \{S \rightarrow A_{uth}, T \rightarrow I, R \rightarrow N, I \rightarrow C, D \rightarrow A, E \rightarrow A_{uthz}\}$$

where **N** denotes **Non-repudiation**.

### 2.4 Saltzer & Schroeder's Eight Security Design Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 8 Foundational Principles (1975) — Mandatory for KTU 14-mark questions.**

1. **Least Privilege** — Every process, user, or program should operate using the minimum set of privileges necessary to complete its task. *Example:* A microservice reading a product catalog should not have `DELETE` permission on the `orders` table.

2. **Fail-Safe Defaults** — The default state of the system should be denial of access; access should only be granted explicitly. *Example:* Firewall default-deny rule, database row-level security turned ON by default.

3. **Economy of Mechanism** — Security mechanisms should be as simple as possible (KISS principle for security). Complex designs have more bugs and are harder to audit.

4. **Complete Mediation** — Every access to every resource must be checked for authorization — no caching of permissions across requests. *Example:* Re-validating JWT on every API call, not just at login.

5. **Open Design** — Security should not depend on secrecy of the mechanism (e.g., no "security through obscurity"). Algorithms like AES, RSA, and TLS are open yet secure.

6. **Separation of Privilege** — A critical operation should require multiple independent conditions to be satisfied. *Example:* Two-person integrity (TPI) for nuclear launch, dual-key release in banking apps.

7. **Least Common Mechanism** — Minimize shared resources between users to prevent covert channels. *Example:* Dedicated tenants in SaaS multi-tenancy vs. shared connection pools.

8. **Psychological Acceptability** — Security mechanisms must be user-friendly; if they are too cumbersome, users will circumvent them. *Example:* Biometric login vs. 64-character passwords changed daily.

### 2.5 Additional Modern Security Principles

9. **Defense in Depth** — Layer multiple, independent security controls so that the failure of one control does not lead to system compromise. Layers typically include: *perimeter → network → host → application → data*.

10. **Secure by Default** — Ship products with the most secure settings pre-configured; opt-in (not opt-out) for risky features.

11. **Zero Trust Architecture** — Never trust, always verify. Every request, internal or external, is authenticated and authorized.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microsoft SDL**: Threat modeling is mandatory before any Windows feature ships.
- **Amazon Builder's Library**: Uses threat modeling for every microservice design review.
- **OWASP SAMM (Software Assurance Maturity Model)**: Threat modeling is a Level-2 maturity practice.
- **DevSecOps Pipelines**: Tools like **Microsoft Threat Modeling Tool**, **OWASP Threat Dragon**, and **IriusRisk** automate STRIDE analysis on architecture diagrams.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the DREAD Aggregate Risk Score

The DREAD framework produces a numerical risk score in the range $[1, 10]$ by averaging five independent ordinal ratings.

**Step 1 — Define the rating scale.**

Each dimension $X_i \in \{D, R, E, A, D_{isc}\}$ is assigned an integer score from the bounded set:

$$X_i \in \{1, 2, 3, ..., 10\}, \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$$

where 1 represents the lowest severity and 10 represents catastrophic severity.

**Step 2 — Sum the individual ratings.**

Let $D_{mg}$ denote Damage Potential, $R_{ep}$ denote Reproducibility, $E_{xp}$ denote Exploitability, $A_{ff}$ denote Affected Users, and $D_{isc}$ denote Discoverability. The total severity is:

$$\Sigma = D_{mg} + R_{ep} + E_{xp} + A_{ff} + D_{isc}$$

**Step 3 — Normalize via arithmetic mean.**

To obtain a score on a standardized 1–10 scale comparable across threats:

$$\begin{aligned}
Risk_{DREAD} &= \dfrac{\Sigma}{5} \\
&= \dfrac{D_{mg} + R_{ep} + E_{xp} + A_{ff} + D_{isc}}{5}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the qualitative threshold.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Risk_{DREAD} &\in [1, 3) \rightarrow \text{Low Risk (Mitigate later)} \\
Risk_{DREAD} &\in [3, 7) \rightarrow \text{Medium Risk (Plan mitigation)} \\
Risk_{DREAD} &\in [7, 10] \rightarrow \text{Critical Risk (Immediate fix)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

Consider a threat: *SQL Injection in the `/login` endpoint of an e-commerce web app.*

| Dimension | Rating | Justification |
|-----------|--------|---------------|
| Damage Potential (D) | 8 | Full database compromise possible |
| Reproducibility (R) | 9 | sqlmap automates it 100% of the time |
| Exploitability (E) | 7 | Public tools available, intermediate skill |
| Affected Users (A) | 10 | All customer accounts |
| Discoverability (D) | 8 | Easily found via Nmap + Burp Suite |

Applying the formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Sigma &= 8 + 9 + 7 + 10 + 8 = 42 \\
Risk_{DREAD} &= \dfrac{42}{5} = 8.4
\end{aligned}$$

**Classification:** $Risk_{DREAD} = 8.4 \in [7, 10]$ → **Critical Risk — Immediate remediation required.**

The standard remediation pattern is:
1. Use **parameterized queries / prepared statements**.
2. Apply **input validation** with allow-lists.
3. Deploy a **Web Application Firewall (WAF)** as a compensating control (Defense in Depth).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — STRIDE Applied to a Login Microservice

**System under analysis:** A Java Spring Boot login microservice that authenticates users against a PostgreSQL database, issues JWT tokens, and logs events to a central ELK stack.

| DFD Element | Type | STRIDE Threat | Attack Vector | Mitigation |
|-------------|------|---------------|---------------|------------|
| User Browser | External Entity | Spoofing | Credential stuffing | Rate limiting + MFA |
| Login Endpoint `/auth/login` | Process | Tampering | HTTP parameter pollution | Schema validation |
| Login Endpoint `/auth/login` | Process | Information Disclosure | Verbose error messages | Generic error responses |
| Login Endpoint `/auth/login` | Process | Denial of Service | Slowloris, brute force | WAF + account lockout |
| JWT Token | Data Flow | Tampering | Algorithm confusion (alg=none) | Enforce RS256, verify signature |
| PostgreSQL DB | Data Store | Information Disclosure | SQL Injection | Prepared statements |
| Audit Log File | Data Store | Repudiation | Log tampering | Append-only WORM storage + HMAC |
| Login Endpoint `/auth/login` | Process | Elevation of Privilege | Role injection in JWT claims | Server-side claim validation |

### 3.3 Derivative Risk Equation (NIST SP 800-30 Style)

In many real-world enterprises, **DREAD** is replaced by **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System)**. The KTU board occasionally tests the conceptual equivalence:

$$Risk = Probability \times Impact$$

For a SQL injection in a payment processing module:
- $Probability = 0.8$ (high, given public exploit tools)
- $Impact = 0.95$ (critical, financial data)

$$Risk = 0.8 \times 0.95 = 0.76$$

A **Risk > 0.7** triggers immediate escalation to the CISO office.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 STRIDE Threat Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Architecture Diagram] --> B[Enumerate DFD Elements]
    B --> C1[External Entity]
    B --> C2[Process]
    B --> C3[Data Store]
    B --> C4[Data Flow]
    C1 --> D1[Spoofing? Repudiation?]
    C2 --> D2[All Six STRIDE Threats]
    C3 --> D3[Tampering? Info Disclosure? DoS?]
    C4 --> D4[Tampering? Info Disclosure? DoS?]
    D1 --> E[Document Threat Record]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Apply DREAD Rating]
    F --> G{Score >= 7?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Immediate Fix Required]
    G -->|No| I[Backlog for Future Sprint]
    H --> J[Re-validate via Penetration Test]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Update Threat Model]
```

### 4.2 Defense in Depth — Layered Security Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1 Perimeter] --> L2[Layer 2 Network]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Host]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Application]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 Data]
    L1 --> N1[Firewall DDoS Protection WAF]
    L2 --> N2[VLAN Segmentation IDS IPS VPN]
    L3 --> N3[OS Hardening EDR Antivirus Patching]
    L4 --> N4[Input Validation AuthN Z AuthZ Session Mgmt]
    L5 --> N5[Encryption at Rest TLS Backups Tokenization]
    L5 --> L6[Attacker Must Breach ALL Five Layers]
```

### 4.3 Saltzer-Schroeder Design Principles Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Least Privilege] --> P2[Fail Safe Defaults]
    P2 --> P3[Economy of Mechanism]
    P3 --> P4[Complete Mediation]
    P4 --> P5[Open Design]
    P5 --> P6[Separation of Privilege]
    P6 --> P7[Least Common Mechanism]
    P7 --> P8[Psychological Acceptability]
    P8 --> P9[Defense in Depth Modern]
    P9 --> P10[Zero Trust Modern]
    P10 --> S[Secure Software Architecture]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` Define threat modeling and list any four threat categories of the STRIDE model.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** Threat modeling is a structured engineering process used to identify, classify, and prioritize potential security threats to a software system by analyzing its architecture, assets, and trust boundaries — typically represented as Data Flow Diagrams (DFDs).
>
> **Four STRIDE Categories (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **S — Spoofing:** Illegal access via identity impersonation (e.g., phishing, IP spoofing).
> 2. **T — Tampering:** Malicious modification of data in transit or at rest (e.g., MITM, SQL injection).
> 3. **R — Repudiation:** A user denies performing an action because the system lacks audit trails (e.g., unsigned log entries).
> 4. **D — Denial of Service:** Disruption of system availability for legitimate users (e.g., SYN flood, DDoS).

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` State any three Saltzer-Schroeder security design principles with one-line examples.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 each):**
>
> 1. **Least Privilege:** A microservice should only have the database permissions it strictly needs (e.g., `SELECT` on the `products` table, not `DROP`).
> 2. **Fail-Safe Defaults:** A firewall should deny all inbound traffic by default and only permit explicitly allowed ports.
> 3. **Open Design:** The AES-256 encryption algorithm is publicly known yet cryptographically secure — security must not rely on algorithm secrecy.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks in Part B questions by (1) writing only the principle *name* without a *technical example*, (2) forgetting to **map STRIDE to CIA Triad**, and (3) omitting the **DREAD numerical calculation** when the question explicitly asks to "rate" a threat. Always include **both theory and a worked example**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Threat Modeling Methodology

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the four-phase process of threat modeling as prescribed by Microsoft's Security Development Lifecycle (SDL). Illustrate with a Data Flow Diagram (DFD) for an online banking application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the STRIDE methodology to the DFD in part (a) and produce a **threat register** with at least six threats. Rate each threat using the DREAD framework and recommend mitigations. **(7 marks)**

---

> **Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Phases of Threat Modeling — 7 marks**

**Phase 1 — Diagram the System (2 marks):**
Identify assets, entry/exit points, and trust boundaries. Use Data Flow Diagrams (DFDs) with four symbols: external entity (rectangle), process (circle), data store (two parallel lines), and data flow (arrow). Trust boundaries are drawn as dashed vertical lines separating zones of differing privilege.

For an **Online Banking Application**, the DFD includes:
- **External Entities:** Customer browser, Bank's core banking API, SMS gateway, Email service
- **Processes:** Login service, Account balance service, Fund transfer service, OTP validation service
- **Data Stores:** Customer DB (PostgreSQL), Transaction logs (Kafka), Session cache (Redis)
- **Trust Boundaries:** Internet ↔ DMZ, DMZ ↔ Internal Bank Network

**Phase 2 — Identify Threats (1 mark):**
Apply the STRIDE mnemonic to each DFD element. For example, the Login service process is vulnerable to all six STRIDE categories, while the Redis cache (data store) is primarily vulnerable to Information Disclosure and Tampering.

**Phase 3 — Document Threats (1 mark):**
Each threat is logged in a **threat register** with: Threat ID, STRIDE category, DFD element, attack technique, mitigation status, and risk score.

**Phase 4 — Rate Threats (1 mark):**
Apply DREAD to each threat and assign a normalized 1–10 score. Threats scoring ≥ 7 are remediated immediately.

**Iteration (1 mark):**
Threat modeling is iterative. After each code release, the model is re-validated against actual penetration test results.

**Phase 5 — Validate via Testing (1 mark):**
Use penetration testing and fuzzing to confirm that identified threats are real and mitigations work.

---

**(b) STRIDE Threat Register for Online Banking — 7 marks**

| T-ID | STRIDE Category | DFD Element | Attack Vector | DREAD Score | Mitigation |
|------|------------------|-------------|---------------|-------------|------------|
| T-01 | Spoofing | Login service | Credential stuffing via stolen passwords | (9+9+8+10+9)/5 = **9.0** | MFA + rate limiting |
| T-02 | Tampering | Fund transfer API | HTTP request smuggling to alter beneficiary | (9+7+6+10+7)/5 = **7.8** | TLS + HMAC request signing |
| T-03 | Repudiation | Transaction log store | User denies initiating transfer | (7+8+7+9+6)/5 = **7.4** | WORM audit log + digital signatures |
| T-04 | Info Disclosure | Customer DB | SQL injection leaking balances | (9+9+8+10+8)/5 = **8.8** | Parameterized queries + WAF |
| T-05 | Denial of Service | Login service | Credential stuffing flooding | (6+10+9+10+10)/5 = **9.0** | CAPTCHA + IP throttling |
| T-06 | Elevation of Privilege | Balance API | IDOR — user queries another's account | (9+9+8+10+7)/5 = **8.6** | Server-side authorization check |

**Risk Conclusion (1 mark):** All six threats score ≥ 7.4 → **Critical / High Risk** → must be fixed before production release.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Security Design Principles

`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain any **four** of Saltzer and Schroeder's security design principles with real-world engineering examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Defense in Depth** strategy with a labeled diagram of at least five security layers. How does it complement the **Zero Trust** principle in a modern cloud-native DevSecOps pipeline? **(7 marks)**

---

> **Model Solution:**

**(a) Saltzer-Schroeder Principles — 7 marks (1.75 each)**

1. **Least Privilege (1.75 marks):** Each subject (user, process, service) is granted only the minimum permissions necessary. *Engineering example:* In Kubernetes, a pod's `ServiceAccount` is bound to a `Role` that grants only `get` and `list` on a specific ConfigMap — never `cluster-admin`. AWS IAM follows the same pattern with narrowly scoped policies.

2. **Fail-Safe Defaults (1.75 marks):** The default access decision is *deny*. Access must be explicitly granted. *Engineering example:* AWS Security Groups default to denying all inbound traffic; PostgreSQL `pg_hba.conf` requires explicit `host` entries before remote connections are allowed.

3. **Complete Mediation (1.75 marks):** Every access to every object is checked for authorization — permissions are not cached indefinitely. *Engineering example:* OAuth 2.0 access tokens are re-validated on every API call via the introspection endpoint, not just at session start.

4. **Open Design (1.75 marks):** The security of a mechanism must not depend on the secrecy of its design. *Engineering example:* The TLS 1.3 protocol is fully published in RFC 8446 and remains secure because of robust cryptography, not because the protocol is hidden.

*(Alternative valid choices: Economy of Mechanism, Separation of Privilege, Least Common Mechanism, Psychological Acceptability.)*

---

**(b) Defense in Depth + Zero Trust — 7 marks**

**Defense in Depth Definition (1 mark):** A layered security strategy in which multiple independent controls protect an asset, so that the failure of one control does not lead to compromise.

**Five Layers (4 marks — 0.8 each):**
1. **Perimeter Layer:** Firewall, DDoS mitigation (e.g., Cloudflare), Web Application Firewall (WAF)
2. **Network Layer:** VLAN segmentation, Intrusion Detection/Prevention Systems (IDS/IPS), mTLS service mesh
3. **Host Layer:** OS hardening (CIS Benchmarks), Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR), patching
4. **Application Layer:** Input validation, parameterized queries, MFA, role-based access control (RBAC)
5. **Data Layer:** Encryption at rest (AES-256), tokenization, hashed passwords (bcrypt/argon2), backup integrity

**Zero Trust Principle (1 mark):** *"Never trust, always verify."* Every request — internal or external — is authenticated, authorized, and logged, regardless of network location.

**Synergy with Defense in Depth (1 mark):** Defense in Depth provides the **layered architecture**; Zero Trust provides the **per-request verification policy** running inside each layer. Together they ensure that even if a perimeter firewall is breached, the attacker cannot move laterally without re-authenticating at every layer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Bulletins (must memorize):**

- ✅ **Threat Modeling** = proactive identification of threats at design phase; answers "What are we building? What can go wrong? What will we do? Did we do well?"
- ✅ **STRIDE** = **S**poofing, **T**ampering, **R**epudiation, **I**nformation Disclosure, **D**enial of Service, **E**levation of Privilege.
- ✅ **STRIDE ↔ CIA Mapping:** Spoofing → Authentication; Tampering → Integrity; Repudiation → Non-repudiation; Info Disclosure → Confidentiality; DoS → Availability; Elevation → Authorization.
- ✅ **DREAD Formula:** $Risk = \dfrac{D + R + E + A + D}{5}$; range 1–10; ≥ 7 = Critical.
- ✅ **Saltzer-Schroeder 8 Principles:** Least Privilege, Fail-Safe Defaults, Economy of Mechanism, Complete Mediation, Open Design, Separation of Privilege, Least Common Mechanism, Psychological Acceptability.
- ✅ **Modern Additions:** Defense in Depth (layered security), Secure by Default, Zero Trust Architecture.
- ✅ **DFD Elements:** External Entity (rectangle), Process (circle), Data Store (parallel lines), Data Flow (arrow); Trust Boundaries are dashed lines.
- ✅ **Threat Modeling Tools:** Microsoft Threat Modeling Tool, OWASP Threat Dragon, IriusRisk.
- ✅ **Risk Equation (NIST):** $Risk = Probability \times Impact$.
- ✅ **Annual Loss Expectancy:** $ALE = SLE \times ARO$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Testing: Static Application Security Testing (SAST), Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST), and Penetration Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT742 - SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT WITH AGILE AND DEVOPS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Security Testing: Static Application Security Testing (SAST), Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST), and Penetration Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔐 Module 3 — Security Testing: SAST, DAST, and Penetration Testing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Security Testing — The Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Testing** is a non-functional testing methodology used to determine whether an information system protects data and maintains its intended functionality as defined by the CIA triad — **Confidentiality**, **Integrity**, and **Availability**. Within the KTU 2024 Agile–DevOps framework, security testing is embedded directly into the CI/CD pipeline to ensure *“secure-by-design”* delivery, satisfying the course outcome **CO3** of Module 3.

The CIA triad (also called the **AIC triad**) forms the cornerstone of every security testing strategy:

- **Confidentiality** — Restricting data access to authorized users only.
- **Integrity** — Guaranteeing that data is not altered or tampered with during transit or storage.
- **Availability** — Ensuring systems and data are accessible to authorized users when needed.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module 3 expects students to clearly differentiate **SAST (white-box)**, **DAST (black-box)**, and **Penetration Testing (grey-box/red-team)** — these three form the *security testing triangle* of modern DevSecOps pipelines.

### 1.2 Why Security Testing in Agile & DevOps?

In classical waterfall, security was a *final-stage* activity. In Agile/DevOps, it is **shifted left** (performed earlier) and **continuous**. This brings three practical benefits:

1. **Early Defect Detection** — Vulnerabilities caught at the *coding* phase cost **30× less** than those caught in production.
2. **Compliance Assurance** — Aligns with standards like **OWASP Top 10**, **NIST SSDF**, **PCI-DSS**, and **ISO/IEC 27001**.
3. **Pipeline Automation** — Fail-fast feedback within CI/CD (Jenkins, GitLab CI, Azure DevOps).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Inspector" Model

Imagine you have just finished building a house (your software application):

| Security Test Type | Real-World Analogy | What it Inspects |
|---|---|---|
| **SAST** | An architect reviewing the *blueprint drawings* before construction | Source code, bytecode, dependencies — **internal structure** |
| **DAST** | A city inspector visiting the *finished house* with a flashlight at night | The running application, exposed endpoints, runtime behavior |
| **Penetration Testing** | Hiring ethical burglars to *actually try to break in* | Exploitable real-world attack vectors |

> [!TIP]
> If you only do one type, you miss the others. SAST may miss a runtime misconfiguration. DAST may miss a SQL injection buried deep in unexecuted code paths. Penetration testing validates *real* exploitability. **All three together = defense in depth.**

### 1.4 The OWASP Top 10 — The Universal Threat Reference

The **Open Web Application Security Project (OWASP) Top 10** is the industry-standard awareness document representing the most critical web application security risks. The current standard is **OWASP Top 10:2021**:

1. **A01** — Broken Access Control
2. **A02** — Cryptographic Failures
3. **A03** — Injection (SQLi, XSS, LDAP)
4. **A04** — Insecure Design
5. **A05** — Security Misconfiguration
6. **A06** — Vulnerable & Outdated Components
7. **A07** — Identification & Authentication Failures
8. **A08** — Software & Data Integrity Failures
9. **A09** — Security Logging & Monitoring Failures
10. **A10** — Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
