This list acts as the **risk catalog** against which SAST, DAST, and Pen-Testing tools score their findings.

### 1.5 SAST — Static Application Security Testing (White-Box)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):** SAST is a *white-box* security testing technique that analyzes an application's **source code, bytecode, or binary code** *without executing* the program, to identify security vulnerabilities such as SQL injection, XSS, buffer overflows, and insecure cryptographic implementations.

**Key Characteristics:**
- Operates on **non-running** code (static analysis).
- Requires **source code access** — hence "white-box".
- Also called **Source Code Analysis**.
- Uses techniques like **data flow analysis**, **control flow analysis**, **taint analysis**, and **pattern matching**.

### 1.6 DAST — Dynamic Application Security Testing (Black-Box)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):** DAST is a *black-box* security testing technique that simulates external attacks against a **running application** by sending crafted HTTP requests, fuzzing inputs, and analyzing responses to discover runtime vulnerabilities without access to the source code.

**Key Characteristics:**
- Operates on **executing** code (dynamic analysis).
- Requires **no source code access** — hence "black-box".
- Detects runtime issues like **CSRF, session management flaws, misconfigurations, and authentication bypass**.
- Tools send malicious payloads to live endpoints.

### 1.7 Penetration Testing (Grey-Box / Red-Team)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):** Penetration Testing (or *Ethical Hacking*) is a structured, authorized, simulated cyberattack performed against a system to **exploit** vulnerabilities and evaluate the real-world security posture, often mimicking tactics from the **MITRE ATT&CK** framework.

**Three Variants:**

| Type | Knowledge Level | Goal | Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Black-Box** | Zero internal knowledge | Simulate external hacker | Longest |
| **Grey-Box** | Partial knowledge (e.g., credentials) | Simulate insider threat / logged-in user | Medium |
| **White-Box** | Full source + architecture | Comprehensive audit (a.k.a. *Crystal-Box*) | Shortest |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SAST — Internal Mechanics

SAST works by building an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** of the code and traversing it using formal analysis algorithms.

### 2.1.1 The SAST Operational Pipeline

The static analysis pipeline executes in **five sequential phases**:

1. **Lexical Analysis** — Tokenizes source code into keywords, identifiers, operators.
2. **Parsing** — Builds the **AST** representing program structure.
3. **Semantic Analysis** — Resolves types, scopes, symbol tables.
4. **Security Rule Engine** — Applies security rule sets (e.g., CWE mappings).
5. **Reporting** — Outputs findings with severity, line numbers, and remediation.

### 2.1.2 Core Static Analysis Techniques

- **Pattern Matching (Signature-based):** Searches for known-bad patterns (e.g., `eval($input)`).
- **Data Flow Analysis (DFA):** Tracks how *untrusted data* propagates from sources (e.g., `request.getParameter`) to sinks (e.g., `executeQuery`).
- **Control Flow Analysis (CFA):** Models all possible execution paths to detect dead code, unreachable sinks.
- **Taint Analysis:** Marks untrusted inputs as "tainted" and flags when they reach sensitive sinks without sanitization.
- **Symbolic Execution:** Solves path constraints to discover inputs that trigger vulnerabilities.

### 2.1.3 Strengths & Limitations of SAST

> [!TIP]
> Strengths: 100% code coverage at unit level, early detection, identifies root cause (line number).
> Limitations: High **false-positive rate (15–50%)**, cannot detect runtime issues, language-dependent, struggles with dynamically-typed code.

### 2.2 DAST — Internal Mechanics

DAST works by treating the application as an *attacker would* — interacting only with exposed HTTP/HTTPS endpoints.

### 2.2.1 The DAST Operational Pipeline

1. **Spidering / Crawling** — Discovers all reachable URLs, forms, and API endpoints.
2. **Authentication & Session Handling** — Logs in to test authenticated areas.
3. **Attack Vector Injection** — Sends malicious payloads (XSS, SQLi, XXE payloads).
4. **Response Analysis** — Compares responses against expected safe behavior and known vulnerability signatures.
5. **Reporting** — Catalogs confirmed vulnerabilities with HTTP request/response evidence.

### 2.2.2 Core DAST Attack Techniques

- **Fuzzing** — Sends semi-random or mutation-based inputs to crash/error out the application.
- **Injection Payload Testing** — SQL: `' OR '1'='1' --`, XSS: `<script>alert(1)</script>`.
- **Authentication & Session Tests** — Cookie tampering, session fixation, brute-force.
- **Configuration Auditing** — Checks headers (HSTS, CSP, X-Frame-Options).
- **Crawler-Based Discovery** — Crawls JavaScript-rendered SPAs (modern DAST like StackHawk supports this).

### 2.2.3 Strengths & Limitations of DAST

> [!TIP]
> Strengths: Finds *runtime* and *configuration* flaws, language-agnostic, low false positives.
> Limitations: Requires running application, only covers **executed paths** (low code coverage ~ 20%), slower scans, no line-level source pinpointing.

### 2.3 Penetration Testing — The 5-Phase Methodology

The **PTES (Penetration Testing Execution Standard)** defines a 5-phase methodology:

1. **Phase 1 — Reconnaissance (Information Gathering):** Passive (OSINT, Shodan) + Active (port scanning, DNS enumeration).
2. **Phase 2 — Scanning:** Use **Nmap**, **Nessus**, **Burp Suite** to identify live hosts, ports, services, and vulnerabilities.
3. **Phase 3 — Gaining Access (Exploitation):** Use exploits (Metasploit, manual SQLi) to break in.
4. **Phase 4 — Maintaining Access:** Establish persistent footholds (reverse shells, backdoors) — *only with explicit authorization*.
5. **Phase 5 — Covering Tracks & Reporting:** Document findings, remediation steps, business impact (CVSS scoring).

### 2.4 The CVSS — Common Vulnerability Scoring System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CVSS** is the industry-standard vulnerability severity scoring system. It produces a score from **0.0 to 10.0** and is mandatory to learn for KTU exams.

| Severity | CVSS Score Range | Action Priority |
|---|---|---|
| **None** | 0.0 | None |
| **Low** | 0.1 – 3.9 | Monitor |
| **Medium** | 4.0 – 6.9 | Patch in next cycle |
| **High** | 7.0 – 8.9 | Patch immediately |
| **Critical** | 9.0 – 10.0 | Emergency patch |

The score is computed from three metric groups: **Base**, **Temporal**, and **Environmental**.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Security Testing Metrics

| Metric / Formula | Mathematical Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Ratio of false alarms among all actual negatives |
| **True Positive Rate (TPR / Recall)** | $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Detection effectiveness on real bugs |
| **Precision** | $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Reliability of a tool's alarms |
| **F1 Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall |
| **Vulnerability Density** | $VD = \dfrac{N_{vuln}}{KLOC}$ | Vulnerabilities per 1000 lines of code |
| **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** | $MTTD = \dfrac{\sum (t_{detect} - t_{introduce})}{N}$ | Average latency in finding a bug |
| **Mean Time to Remediate (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum (t_{fix} - t_{detect})}{N}$ | Average time to patch after detection |
| **CVSS Base Score (simplified)** | $BaseScore = round(min(Impact + Exploitability, 10))$ | Standardized severity (0–10) |
| **Attack Surface Reduction** | $ASR = 1 - \dfrac{A_{after}}{A_{before}}$ | Fraction of surface eliminated post-mitigation |
| **Risk Score** | $Risk = Likelihood \times Impact$ | Qualitative risk rating |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important boundary condition for CVSS:** The base score is *clamped* to the range $[0, 10]$. Always wrap arithmetic in $\min(\cdot, 10)$ and $\max(\cdot, 0)$ during derivations.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Agile/DevOps Engineering

- **GitHub Advanced Security** uses SAST (CodeQL) inside pull requests.
- **OWASP ZAP** is the most popular free DAST tool integrated into Jenkins.
- **Burp Suite Pro** is the industry standard for manual and automated penetration testing.
- **DevSecOps pipeline gates** automatically fail builds when SAST/DAST find **Critical** CVSS $\geq 9.0$ issues.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — CVSS Risk Computation for a SQL Injection

A penetration test discovers a **SQL Injection** in the login endpoint with the following qualitative scores:

- **Likelihood** = 0.9 (almost certain exploitation, public exploit exists).
- **Impact** = 0.8 (full database read/write, possible RCE).
- **Exploitability subscore** = 0.6.
- **CVSS raw base** = 7.5 before environmental adjustment.

### Step 1 — Apply the Base Score Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RawImpact} &= 6.42 \times (1 - (1 - C)^2) \\
\text{where } C &= 0.8 \text{ (Confidentiality impact)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Derive F1 Score from a SAST Tool Report

A SAST tool reports on a 10,000-line module:
- **True Positives (TP)** = 30 (real bugs confirmed by developer)
- **False Positives (FP)** = 45 (alarms that turned out to be safe)
- **False Negatives (FN)** = 5 (bugs missed by the tool, found later)
- **True Negatives (TN)** = 9,920

> [!NOTE]
> TN is computed by subtraction: $TN = \text{Total} - TP - FP - FN = 10{,}000 - 30 - 45 - 5 = 9{,}920$.

Compute **Precision**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Precision &= \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} \\
&= \dfrac{30}{30 + 45} \\
&= \dfrac{30}{75} \\
&= 0.40
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute **Recall (TPR)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Recall &= \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} \\
&= \dfrac{30}{30 + 5} \\
&= \dfrac{30}{35} \\
&\approx 0.857
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute **F1 Score**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R} \\
&= 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.40 \times 0.857}{0.40 + 0.857} \\
&= 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.3428}{1.257} \\
&\approx 0.545
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Interpretation: The tool has **high recall (85.7%)** — it catches most bugs — but **low precision (40%)** — meaning **6 out of 10 alerts are false positives**, a typical SAST characteristic. This is why SAST results are *triaged* by developers before fixing.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SAST vs DAST vs Pen Testing

| Parameter | SAST | DAST | Penetration Testing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Knowledge Required** | White-box (source code) | Black-box (running app) | Grey/Black/White-box |
| **Phase in SDLC** | Coding / Build | Staging / Pre-prod | Post-build / Production-like |
| **Speed of Execution** | Fast (minutes) | Medium (hours) | Slow (days–weeks) |
| **False Positive Rate** | High (15–50%) | Low (<10%) | Very Low (human-validated) |
| **Code Coverage Achieved** | 100% of source scanned | ~20% of paths executed | Targeted, exploitable paths |
| **Detects Runtime Issues** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Detects Source-Level Bugs** | ✅ Yes (line number) | ❌ No | ⚠️ Sometimes |
| **Cost** | Low (open-source) | Medium | High (skilled pentester) |
| **Frequency in DevOps** | Every commit (CI) | Every nightly build | Quarterly / Annual |
| **Skill Required to Operate** | Developer | Tester | Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH) / OSCP |
| **Output Format** | Static report w/ line numbers | HTTP request/response log | Executive report + CVSS scoring |
| **OWASP Top 10 Coverage** | ~70% | ~60% | ~95% (with chaining) |
| **Can Exploit the Bug?** | No (detects only) | No (detects only) | **Yes — proves exploitability** |

## 3.3 Pentest Phase — Calculating Attack Surface Reduction

A company initially exposes **200 public-facing endpoints**. After penetration testing and remediation, only **50 endpoints** remain publicly accessible. The remaining endpoints are placed behind authentication or decommissioned.

Compute the **Attack Surface Reduction (ASR)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ASR &= 1 - \dfrac{A_{after}}{A_{before}} \\
&= 1 - \dfrac{50}{200} \\
&= 1 - 0.25 \\
&= 0.75
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the attack surface was **reduced by 75%** — a strong security posture improvement.

## 3.4 Vulnerability Density Calculation

A project has **50,000 lines of code (KLOC)** and a SAST + DAST scan combined identifies **125 vulnerabilities** of varying severity.

$$
\begin{aligned}
VD &= \dfrac{N_{vuln}}{KLOC} \\
&= \dfrac{125}{50} \\
&= 2.5 \text{ vulnerabilities per KLOC}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Industry benchmark for mature DevSecOps programs: **VD ≤ 0.5 / KLOC**. A score of 2.5 indicates significant technical debt.

## 3.5 Analytical Flow — Choosing the Right Test

```
+----------------------------+
|  Is source code available? |
+-------------+--------------+
              |
       YES    |    NO
       v            v
+----------+   +-----------+
|  SAST    |   |  Use DAST |
|  + DAST  |   |  + Pentest|
+----------+   +-----------+
       |
       v
+----------------------+
| Need exploit proof?  |
+----------+-----------+
           |
    YES    |   NO
    v           v
+----------+  +-----------+
| Pentest  |  |  Report   |
+----------+  +-----------+
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DevSecOps Pipeline — Where Security Tests Live

The following diagram illustrates the **canonical CI/CD pipeline** and the precise insertion points of SAST, DAST, and Penetration Testing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Developer Push Code] --> B[SCM: Git Repository]
    B --> C[CI: Build Compile]
    C --> D[SAST Scan\nCodeQL Semgrep]
    D -->|Pass| E[Unit Integration Tests]
    D -->|Fail CVSS ge 7| X1[Block Merge PR]
    E --> F[Build Artifact Docker Image]
    F --> G[SCA Dependency Scan\nOWASP Dependency Check]
    G --> H[Deploy to Staging Env]
    H --> I[DAST Scan\nOWASP ZAP Burp]
    I -->|Pass| J[UAT Staging Smoke Tests]
    I -->|Fail| X2[Block Promote to Prod]
    J --> K[Deploy to Production]
    K --> L[Continuous Pen Testing\nQuarterly Red Team]
    L --> M[SIEM Monitoring Logging]
    M --> N[Feedback to Dev Backlog]

    style D fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#ef476f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    style X1 fill:#e63946,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    style X2 fill:#e63946,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!TIP]
> Notice the **fail-fast gates** in red — the pipeline automatically halts deployment when security thresholds (e.g., CVSS $\geq 7$) are breached. This embodies the KTU DevOps principle of **continuous assurance**.

## 4.2 The Security Testing Triangle — Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WT[WHITE BOX]
        SA[SAST Tool\nReads Source Code]
        CW[Code Window\nInternals Visible]
    end
    subgraph BT[BLACK BOX]
        DA[DAST Tool\nSends HTTP Attacks]
        AW[Application Window\nExternally Visible]
    end
    subgraph PT[GREY / RED BOX]
        PE[Pen Tester\nHuman Exploiter]
        AW2[Attack Window\nReal Exploit Chain]
    end

    SA -->|Analyzes| R1[Reports: Source Line Vulnerability]
    DA -->|Probes| R2[Reports: HTTP Request Response]
    PE -->|Exploits| R3[Reports: CVSS Impact PoC]

    style SA fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style DA fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style PE fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Penetration Testing — PTES 5-Phase Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Phase 1 Reconnaissance\nOSINT Passive + Active Scanning] --> P2[Phase 2 Scanning\nNmap Nessus Port Enum]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Gaining Access\nExploiting Vulnerabilities]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4 Maintaining Access\nPost Exploitation Persistence]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5 Reporting Covering Tracks\nCVSS Remediation Steps]

    style P1 fill:#264653,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style P2 fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style P3 fill:#e9c46a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P4 fill:#f4a261,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P5 fill:#e76f51,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Layered Defense — SAST + DAST + Pentest Integration Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Source Code Repository] -->|Layer 1| SAST[SAST Engine\nWhite Box Static]
    BIN[Compiled Binary Container] -->|Layer 2| DAST[DAST Engine\nBlack Box Dynamic]
    DEPLOY[Running Production Stack] -->|Layer 3| PENTEST[Penetration Test\nGrey Box Manual]

    SAST --> AGG[Aggregator Findings DB]
    DAST --> AGG
    PENTEST --> AGG

    AGG --> DASH[Security Dashboard\nCVSS Trends]
    AGG --> TICK[Jira Tickets Auto Created]

    style SAST fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style DAST fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style PENTEST fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style AGG fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.5 Tools Landscape Matrix

| Layer | Open-Source Tool | Commercial Tool | Language Coverage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SAST** | Semgrep, SonarQube Community, Bandit (Python), Brakeman (Rails) | Checkmarx, Veracode, Fortify, GitHub CodeQL | Java, Python, JS, C#, Go |
| **DAST** | OWASP ZAP, Nikto, W3AF | Burp Suite Pro, Invicti (Netsparker), Acunetix | All web stacks |
| **Pen-Test** | Kali Linux, Metasploit Community, Nmap | Cobalt Strike, Core Impact, Burp Suite Pro | All |
| **SCA (Dependency)** | OWASP Dependency-Check, Snyk Free | Snyk, JFrog Xray, Black Duck | All package managers |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**Differentiate between SAST and DAST with two points each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Execution State:** SAST analyzes *non-executing* source code (static), whereas DAST analyzes a *running* application (dynamic) by sending HTTP requests.
2. **Knowledge Access:** SAST requires *source code* access (white-box); DAST requires *no* source code (black-box) and treats the app as an external attacker would.
3. *(Bonus third point)* SAST pinpoints vulnerabilities at the *line number*; DAST pinpoints at the *HTTP request* level and cannot show source code locations.

**Marks Distribution:** 2 marks (1 each for the two points) + 1 mark for presentation/terminology.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]
**List the five phases of penetration testing as per the PTES standard.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Reconnaissance** — Information gathering (OSINT, DNS enumeration).
2. **Scanning** — Port scanning, vulnerability detection (Nmap, Nessus).
3. **Gaining Access** — Exploitation of identified vulnerabilities.
4. **Maintaining Access** — Establishing persistent footholds.
5. **Covering Tracks & Reporting** — Documentation, CVSS scoring, remediation guidance.

**Marks Distribution:** 1 mark per phase × 3 phases = 3 marks (board expects at least three with brief explanation; listing all five is recommended for full marks).

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose 2–3 marks by (a) confusing *SAST* with *DAST* operational context, (b) failing to mention **OWASP Top 10** mapping, (c) omitting **CI/CD integration steps**, and (d) writing only tool names without explaining *how* the test detects vulnerabilities. Always pair each tool with its **detection mechanism**.

---

### ❓ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain **SAST** in detail with its working pipeline. List any **four popular SAST tools** and mention how SAST integrates into a **Jenkins CI/CD pipeline**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **DAST** with its working methodology. Compare DAST and SAST across **six parameters** in a tabular format. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Definition:** SAST is a white-box testing methodology that analyzes application source code, bytecode, or binaries for security vulnerabilities *without executing* the program.

**SAST Pipeline (5 stages):**
1. **Lexical Analysis** — Source code → tokens.
2. **Parsing** — Tokens → Abstract Syntax Tree (AST).
3. **Semantic Analysis** — Type checking, scope resolution.
4. **Security Rule Engine** — Pattern matching (CWE catalog), data flow, taint analysis.
5. **Reporting** — Vulnerability list with severity, line numbers, remediation links.

**Four SAST Tools:**
1. **SonarQube** — Multi-language, integrates with Jenkins via plugin.
2. **Checkmarx** — Enterprise-grade, supports 25+ languages.
3. **Semgrep** — Lightweight, developer-friendly pattern rules.
4. **GitHub CodeQL** — Native to GitHub Actions.

**Jenkins Pipeline Integration:**
- Install plugin: *SonarQube Scanner for Jenkins*.
- Add stage in Jenkinsfile:
  ```
  stage('SAST Scan') {
      steps {
          sh 'sonar-scanner -Dsonar.projectKey=myapp'
      }
  }
  ```
- Set **Quality Gate** to fail build if Critical issues > 0.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**DAST Definition:** DAST is black-box testing that simulates external attacks on a running web application by sending malicious HTTP requests and analyzing responses.

**DAST Methodology (5 steps):**
1. **Crawling** — Discover URLs, forms, REST endpoints.
2. **Authentication** — Configure session tokens.
3. **Payload Injection** — Send attack vectors (XSS, SQLi, XXE).
4. **Response Analysis** — Detect anomalous behaviors.
5. **Reporting** — Vulnerabilities with HTTP evidence.

**Tabular Comparison (6 parameters):**

| Parameter | SAST | DAST |
|---|---|---|
| Code Access | Required | Not required |
| Execution | Static (no run) | Dynamic (running app) |
| False Positives | High | Low |
| Coverage | All source | Executed paths only |
| Speed | Fast | Slow |
| Detects Runtime Bugs | No | Yes |

---

### ❓ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Define **Penetration Testing**. Explain its **five phases** as per PTES standard with one tool used in each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **OWASP Top 10** (any **six** categories) and explain how **SAST, DAST, and Penetration Testing** together provide **defense-in-depth** in a DevSecOps pipeline. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Definition:** Penetration Testing is an authorized simulated cyberattack against a system, performed to evaluate security by safely exploiting vulnerabilities.

**PTES 5 Phases + Tools:**

| Phase | Activity | Tool |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Reconnaissance | OSINT, DNS lookup | Maltego, Recon-ng |
| 2. Scanning | Port & vuln scan | Nmap, Nessus |
| 3. Gaining Access | Exploitation | Metasploit, SQLmap |
| 4. Maintaining Access | Persistence | Netcat, Empire |
| 5. Reporting | Documentation | Dradis, Serpico |

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**OWASP Top 10 (Six Key Categories):**
1. **A01 — Broken Access Control:** Bypassing authorization (SAST detects missing checks; DAST probes IDOR).
2. **A02 — Cryptographic Failures:** Weak hashing/MD5 (SAST detects `MessageDigest.getInstance("MD5")`).
3. **A03 — Injection:** SQLi, XSS (SAST flags tainted sinks; DAST sends `' OR 1=1--`).
4. **A05 — Security Misconfiguration:** Default passwords, open S3 buckets (DAST scans headers).
5. **A06 — Vulnerable Components:** Old Log4j (SCA tools like Snyk detect CVE-2021-44228).
6. **A07 — Authentication Failures:** Weak passwords, missing MFA (Pen-testing brute-forces logins).

**Defense-in-Depth in DevSecOps:**
- **SAST** = *preventive* (catches bugs at code-commit time).
- **DAST** = *detective* (catches runtime issues at staging).
- **Pen-Test** = *validative* (proves real exploitability at release).
- All three feed into a **central Security Dashboard** (Grafana + Prometheus exporters) and **Jira** for remediation tracking — a true **shift-left + continuous** security model.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert (2-mark loss):** Many students write *“SAST is better than DAST”* or *“Penetration testing replaces SAST.”* This is factually wrong. Examiners specifically test for the **complementary nature** of the three — your answer must explicitly state *"SAST and DAST are complementary, not substitutes."*

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **SAST = White-box, Static, Source-code-level**, runs in *build* stage, high false positives, pinpoints **line numbers**.
- 🔹 **DAST = Black-box, Dynamic, Runtime-level**, runs in *staging*, low false positives, covers **executed paths**.
- 🔹 **Pen Testing = Grey/Red-box, Manual + Automated Exploitation**, runs *quarterly*, validates **real exploitability**, produces **CVSS-scored reports**.
- 🔹 All three map against **OWASP Top 10:2021** — your exam answer should always reference it.
- 🔹 **CVSS** scoring: 0.1–3.9 (Low), 4.0–6.9 (Medium), 7.0–8.9 (High), 9.0–10.0 (Critical).
- 🔹 **F1 Score formula** $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{PR}{P+R}$ is the standard SAST tool performance metric.
- 🔹 **Vulnerability Density** $VD = N_{vuln}/KLOC$ — industry benchmark $\leq 0.5/\text{KLOC}$.
- 🔹 **PTES 5 Phases:** Reconnaissance → Scanning → Gaining Access → Maintaining Access → Reporting.
- 🔹 **DevSecOps pipeline gate:** CVSS $\geq 7.0$ findings should automatically **block** promotion to production.
- 🔹 Tools to remember: **SonarQube / CodeQL / Semgrep** (SAST); **OWASP ZAP / Burp Suite** (DAST); **Metasploit / Kali Linux / Nmap** (Pen-Test).
- 🔹 **Defense in Depth** = SAST (prevent) + DAST (detect) + Pen-Test (validate) — they are **complementary, never substitutes**.

> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to compare, always present a **table with at least 6 parameters**. When asked "explain," use the **5-stage pipeline** structure. KTU examiners reward **structured answers with headings and bullets** — avoid writing long unbroken paragraphs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security in CI/CD Pipeline: Integrating Security into DevOps (DevSecOps) and Automated Security Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT742 - SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT WITH AGILE AND DEVOPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Security in CI/CD Pipeline: Integrating Security into DevOps (DevSecOps) and Automated Security Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**DevSecOps** is the cultural, architectural, and engineering practice of integrating information security controls, vulnerability assessment, threat modeling, and compliance verification as automated, first-class citizens into the Continuous Integration / Continuous Delivery (CI/CD) pipeline. It transforms security from a post-development gate (the traditional "Sec at the end" model) into a **shared, continuous responsibility** of development, security, and operations teams across the entire Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** *DevSecOps is the philosophy of integrating security practices within the DevOps process. It involves creating a 'Security as Code' culture with ongoing, flexible collaboration between release engineers and security teams.*

### 1.2 The "Three Pillars" of DevSecOps

| Pillar | Description |
|---|---|
| **Culture** | Breaking silos between Dev, Sec, and Ops; shared ownership of security |
| **Automation** | Embedding security scanning and policy enforcement into CI/CD stages |
| **Measurement** | Continuous feedback on vulnerabilities, Mean Time to Detect (MTTD), and Mean Time to Remediate (MTTR) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: "The Airport Security Model"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Think of DevSecOps like modern airport security.**

In the **old model** (Waterfall/Security-at-the-End), you could walk straight to the runway, board the plane, and *only after takeoff* did someone check if you had a boarding pass. If you didn't, the plane had to turn back — catastrophic and expensive.

In the **DevSecOps model**, security checkpoints are placed at **every gate**:
- **Ticket booking (Plan stage):** Threat modeling and security requirements are defined upfront.
- **Check-in counter (Code stage):** Your bag is screened for prohibited items (SAST scans).
- **Security lane (Build stage):** Your luggage goes through X-ray (SCA dependency checks).
- **Boarding gate (Test stage):** You walk through a metal detector (DAST/IAST).
- **Takeoff (Deploy stage):** Final perimeter check (Container/IaC scanning).
- **In-flight (Monitor stage):** Air marshals continuously monitor (Runtime protection).

This means **threats are caught early**, when they are **cheap to fix**, rather than after the application is in production.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Cheat-Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Terminology for KTU Exams**

- **Shift-Left Security:** The practice of moving security testing earlier ("to the left") in the SDLC, closer to the developer.
- **Security as Code (SaC):** Defining, configuring, and enforcing security policies using machine-readable definition files (YAML/JSON/Rego).
- **Compliance as Code (CaC):** Encoding regulatory frameworks (e.g., PCI-DSS, HIPAA) into automated validation scripts.
- **Pipeline Security Gates:** Automated checkpoints in CI/CD that fail the build if a security threshold is breached.
- **SBOM (Software Bill of Materials):** A nested inventory of all components, libraries, and dependencies in a software artifact.
- **Secret Management:** Securely storing, rotating, and injecting credentials (API keys, tokens) using vaults.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind DevSecOps: Limitations of Traditional Security

Traditional security operated under the **"Perimeter + Final Review"** model. This created a **vulnerability bottleneck**:

1. Security team becomes a **gate** at the end of the cycle.
2. Backlog of vulnerabilities grows while code waits for review.
3. The cost of fixing a bug in production is **estimated to be 100x higher** than fixing it at the design phase (IBM Systems Sciences Institute, widely cited industry standard).
4. DevOps' velocity (deploying multiple times per day) makes manual security review **mathematically impossible** at scale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DevSecOps is the *consequence* of DevOps velocity.** When you deploy 50 times a day, you cannot rely on 5 human security analysts manually reviewing every change. Security *must* be automated to keep pace with delivery.

## 2.2 The Security Integration Framework in CI/CD

DevSecOps injects security **at every stage** of the pipeline. The following table maps each pipeline stage to its corresponding security activity:

| Pipeline Stage | Security Activity | Tool Category | Example Tools |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plan** | Threat modeling, security user stories, compliance requirements | Documentation / Risk Analysis | OWASP Threat Dragon, Microsoft Threat Modeling Tool |
| **Code** | Secret scanning, peer review, pre-commit hooks | Pre-commit / IDE Plugins | git-secrets, Talisman, TruffleHog |
| **Build** | Static Application Security Testing (SAST), Software Composition Analysis (SCA) | Static Analyzers | SonarQube, Checkmarx, Snyk, OWASP Dependency-Check |
| **Test** | Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST), Interactive Application Security Testing (IAST) | Runtime Analyzers | OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite, Contrast Security |
| **Release** | Artifact signing, SBOM generation, policy enforcement | Supply Chain Security | Sigstore, Cosign, in-toto, SPDX tools |
| **Deploy** | Infrastructure as Code (IaC) scanning, container image scanning | Configuration Scanners | Checkov, Terrascan, Trivy, Clair, Anchore |
| **Operate / Monitor** | Runtime threat detection, log analysis, anomaly detection | RASP / SIEM / CWPP | Falco, Aqua Security, Splunk, Datadog |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet: Security Engineering Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> These metrics are **frequently asked in KTU 14-mark questions** as analytical/short-note components.

| Metric | Formula / Definition | Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Vulnerability Density (VD)** | $VD = \dfrac{V}{KLOC}$ where $V$ = vulnerabilities, $KLOC$ = thousand lines of code | Vulns / KLOC | Code quality benchmark |
| **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** | $MTTD = \dfrac{\sum (t_{detected} - t_{introduced})}{N}$ | Hours / Days | Speed of detection |
| **Mean Time to Remediate (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum (t_{fixed} - t_{detected})}{N}$ | Hours / Days | Speed of response |
| **Patch Coverage (PC)** | $PC = \dfrac{V_{patched}}{V_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | Remediation completeness |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | Scanner accuracy |
| **CVSS Base Score** | Industry-standard 0.0–10.0 scale from FIRST.org | Score | Severity classification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Notice:** Per the engine protocol, the absolute value notation $\vert x \vert$ is used (not the keyboard pipe $\vert$) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax in your rendered notes.

## 2.4 The Shift-Left Cost Curve

The economic rationale for DevSecOps is captured by the **Shift-Left Cost Curve**:

$$C_{fix}(phase) = C_{design} \times k^{phase\_index}$$

Where:
- $C_{design}$ = cost of fixing at the design phase (baseline, typically 1x).
- $k$ = cost multiplier per phase (industry empirical average $\approx 5$ to $10$).
- $phase\_index$ = 0 (Design), 1 (Code), 2 (Build), 3 (Test), 4 (Production).

**Implication:** A flaw that costs 1 unit to fix at the design stage may cost $k^4$ units in production. This is the **financial justification** for shifting security left.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In production engineering, DevSecOps underpins:
- **Regulated industries** (Banking, Healthcare) where automated Compliance-as-Code is mandated.
- **Open-source supply chains** (e.g., the 2020 SolarWinds attack, the 2021 Log4j vulnerability) where SBOM-based dependency auditing became a national security requirement.
- **Cloud-native architectures** (Kubernetes, Serverless) where misconfigurations account for the majority of breaches (Gartner estimate: **99% of cloud failures will be the customer's fault**, mostly misconfigurations).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Vulnerability Risk Score Model

A commonly tested KTU analytical problem is computing a **prioritized risk score** for a vulnerability across the pipeline. Let us derive the **Composite Vulnerability Risk Index (CVRI)** used in many security dashboards.

**Step 1 — Define the four input factors:**
- $S$ = CVSS Severity Score (0–10).
- $E$ = Exploitability (0 = theoretical, 1 = proof-of-concept, 2 = weaponized in the wild).
- $C$ = Criticality of the affected module (1 = peripheral, 2 = business logic, 3 = authentication/payment).
- $A$ = Asset exposure (0 = internal only, 1 = internet-facing).

**Step 2 — Build the weighted linear composite:**

$$
CVRI = w_1 \cdot \hat{S} + w_2 \cdot \hat{E} + w_3 \cdot \hat{C} + w_4 \cdot \hat{A}
$$

where $\hat{x}$ denotes the **normalized** value in $[0, 1]$.

**Step 3 — Apply normalization:**

$$
\hat{S} = \dfrac{S}{10}, \quad \hat{E} = \dfrac{E}{2}, \quad \hat{C} = \dfrac{C}{3}, \quad \hat{A} = A
$$

**Step 4 — Choose weights (typical industry default):**

$$
w_1 = 0.40, \quad w_2 = 0.25, \quad w_3 = 0.20, \quad w_4 = 0.15
$$

(Severity gets the highest weight, exposure the lowest, since exposure is mitigated by WAFs/segmentation.)

**Step 5 — Example numerical evaluation:**

Suppose we have a vulnerability with $S = 8.5$, $E = 2$, $C = 3$, $A = 1$.

$$
\hat{S} = \dfrac{8.5}{10} = 0.85
$$

$$
\hat{E} = \dfrac{2}{2} = 1.00
$$

$$
\hat{C} = \dfrac{3}{3} = 1.00
$$

$$
\hat{A} = 1.00
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the final score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CVRI &= (0.40 \times 0.85) + (0.25 \times 1.00) + (0.20 \times 1.00) + (0.15 \times 1.00) \\
&= 0.340 + 0.250 + 0.200 + 0.150 \\
&= 0.940
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation thresholds:**

| CVRI Range | Classification | Pipeline Action |
|---|---|---|
| $0.00 - 0.30$ | Low | Log only, do not block |
| $0.31 - 0.60$ | Medium | Warn developer, allow merge |
| $0.61 - 0.85$ | High | Block merge, require fix |
| $0.86 - 1.00$ | Critical | Block build, page on-call |

Our result $CVRI = 0.94$ → **Critical** → the build would be **hard-blocked**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: SAST vs. DAST vs. IAST vs. SCA

This comparison matrix is a **high-probability 14-mark question** ("Compare and contrast static, dynamic, interactive, and software composition analysis").

| Feature | **SAST** | **DAST** | **IAST** | **SCA** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **When it runs** | Build / pre-commit | Test (staging env) | Test (instrumented env) | Build / pre-deploy |
| **What it scans** | Source code / bytecode | Running app from outside | Running app from inside (agent) | Third-party dependencies |
| **Code access needed** | Yes | No (black-box) | Yes (instrumented) | Yes (manifest files) |
| **Speed** | Fast | Slow | Medium | Fast |
| **False positive rate** | Medium-High | Low | Very Low | Very Low |
| **Detects** | SQL injection, XSS in code, hard-coded secrets | Runtime vulns, auth issues, server misconfig | Combined SAST+DAST view | Known CVEs in libraries, license issues |
| **Misses** | Runtime / environment issues | Source-level logic flaws | Cannot run without app | Custom code, novel vulns |
| **KTU Example Tool** | SonarQube | OWASP ZAP | Contrast Security | Snyk, OWASP Dependency-Check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A mature DevSecOps pipeline uses **all four** in a layered "defense-in-depth" model. SAST catches what DAST cannot see (unreachable code), and SCA catches what neither can analyze (transitive dependencies).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: DevSecOps CI/CD Pipeline with Integrated Security Gates

This block-level functional architecture shows where each security control is injected into the pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plan: Threat Modeling and Security Requirements] --> B[Code: Secret Scanning via git-secrets]
    B --> C[Build: SAST and SCA via SonarQube and Snyk]
    C --> D{Security Gate 1: CVSS and CVRI Threshold}
    D -- Fail --> X1[Block Build: Notify Developer]
    D -- Pass --> E[Test: DAST via OWASP ZAP]
    E --> F{Security Gate 2: Runtime Vulnerabilities}
    F -- Fail --> X2[Quarantine Artifact: Open Ticket]
    F -- Pass --> G[Release: Artifact Signing and SBOM]
    G --> H[Deploy: IaC and Container Scan via Trivy and Checkov]
    H --> I{Security Gate 3: Policy Compliance}
    I -- Fail --> X3[Reject Deploy: Rollback]
    I -- Pass --> J[Operate: Runtime Protection via Falco and SIEM]
    J --> K[Continuous Feedback: MTTD and MTTR Metrics]
    K --> A
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: The DevSecOps "Security as Code" Feedback Loop

This diagram illustrates the closed-loop nature of DevSecOps, where every stage feeds metrics back into planning.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Stage1["PLAN PHASE"]
        P1[Define Security User Stories]
        P2[Threat Model with STRIDE]
        P3[Policy as Code in OPA Rego]
    end

    subgraph Stage2["CODE PHASE"]
        C1[Pre-commit Secret Scans]
        C2[IDE Plugin SAST Hints]
    end

    subgraph Stage3["BUILD PHASE"]
        B1[SAST Engine Execution]
        B2[SCA Dependency Audit]
        B3[Container Image Build]
    end

    subgraph Stage4["TEST PHASE"]
        T1[DAST Runtime Scanning]
        T2[IAST with Code Agent]
    end

    subgraph Stage5["RELEASE PHASE"]
        R1[SBOM Generation SPDX]
        R2[Artifact Signing Cosign]
    end

    subgraph Stage6["DEPLOY PHASE"]
        D1[IaC Scan with Checkov]
        D2[Admission Controller OPA Gatekeeper]
    end

    subgraph Stage7["OPERATE PHASE"]
        O1[Runtime Defense Falco]
        O2[Anomaly Detection ML Models]
    end

    P1 --> C1 --> B1 --> T1 --> R1 --> D1 --> O1
    O1 -->|Telemetry| Metrics[Security Metrics Dashboard]
    O2 -->|Telemetry| Metrics
    Metrics -->|Vulnerability Trends| P2
    Metrics -->|Policy Violations| P3
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Tool Layer Cake for Security-as-Code

This architecture diagram maps the tool categories to the application stack layers they protect.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Application Source Code] --> L2[Compiled Bytecode]
    L2 --> L3[Container Image]
    L3 --> L4[Kubernetes Manifest IaC]
    L4 --> L5[Cloud Infrastructure AWS Azure GCP]
    L5 --> L6[Runtime Workload]

    T1[SAST SonarQube Checkmarx] -.protects.-> L1
    T2[SCA Snyk OWASP Dependency Check] -.protects.-> L2
    T3[Container Scan Trivy Clair Anchore] -.protects.-> L3
    T4[IaC Scan Checkov Terrascan] -.protects.-> L4
    T5[CSPM Prisma Cloud Wiz] -.protects.-> L5
    T6[RASP Falco Aqua CWPP] -.protects.-> L6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three diagrams?** KTU 14-mark questions on DevSecOps frequently include a "Draw and explain" component. These three diagrams cover the most commonly asked: (1) Pipeline integration, (2) Feedback loop, and (3) Tool-to-layer mapping.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]
**Define DevSecOps. How does it differ from the traditional approach to security in software development?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** DevSecOps is the practice of integrating security as a shared, automated responsibility across the DevOps pipeline, rather than treating it as a final-stage gate. It stands for **Development, Security, and Operations** working in tandem.

**Traditional vs. DevSecOps (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Traditional Security | DevSecOps |
|---|---|---|
| **Timing** | At the end (pre-release review) | Continuous, from plan to operate |
| **Ownership** | Security team only | Shared across Dev, Sec, Ops |
| **Method** | Manual review, periodic audits | Automated scanning, policy-as-code |
| **Feedback** | Delayed (weeks/months) | Real-time, pipeline-integrated |
| **Goal** | Block release if vulnerable | Enable safe, fast release |

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample]
**What is "Shift-Left Security" and why is it important in a DevOps context?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Shift-Left Security is the practice of moving security testing and controls *earlier* in the SDLC, closer to the design and coding phases.

**Importance (2 marks):**
1. **Cost reduction:** The cost to fix a defect grows exponentially with phase. A bug caught at design costs ~1x; at production, ~100x.
2. **Velocity alignment:** DevOps deploys multiple times per day. Manual post-development security review is mathematically infeasible at that velocity. Shift-left automation is the only way to maintain security without slowing delivery.
3. **Developer empowerment:** Tools like IDE-integrated SAST give developers instant feedback, reducing context switching.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **six stages of the DevSecOps pipeline** and the security activities integrated at each stage. Include appropriate tool examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **SAST, DAST, IAST, and SCA** in terms of their working principle, advantages, limitations, and suitable use cases. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — DevSecOps Pipeline Stages (7 marks)

**Stage 1 — PLAN (1 mark):**
- Activities: Define security requirements, perform **threat modeling** (STRIDE, PASTA frameworks), identify compliance obligations (GDPR, PCI-DSS).
- Tools: OWASP Threat Dragon, Microsoft Threat Modeling Tool.
- Output: Security user stories, abuse cases, compliance checklist.

**Stage 2 — CODE (1 mark):**
- Activities: **Secret scanning** (detect hardcoded API keys/passwords), secure code review, pre-commit hooks.
- Tools: `git-secrets`, `TruffleHog`, `Talisman`, IDE plugins (Snyk Code, SonarLint).
- Output: Clean code, no leaked credentials in version control.

**Stage 3 — BUILD (1.5 marks):**
- Activities: **SAST** (static analysis of source/bytecode), **SCA** (scan third-party dependencies for known CVEs), compile with security flags.
- Tools: SonarQube (SAST), Snyk, OWASP Dependency-Check (SCA), BlackDuck.
- Output: A list of static vulnerabilities + dependency CVEs. Pipeline **fails** if critical issues found.

**Stage 4 — TEST (1.5 marks):**
- Activities: **DAST** (black-box scan of running app), **IAST** (instrumented runtime analysis), penetration testing in staging.
- Tools: OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite, Contrast Security.
- Output: Runtime vulnerabilities (XSS, SQLi, auth bypasses).

**Stage 5 — RELEASE (1 mark):**
- Activities: Generate **SBOM** (SPDX or CycloneDX format), sign artifacts cryptographically, store in trusted registry.
- Tools: Sigstore/Cosign, in-toto, Anchore.
- Output: Verifiable, tamper-proof software supply chain artifacts.

**Stage 6 — DEPLOY & OPERATE (1 mark):**
- Activities: **IaC scanning** (Terraform/CloudFormation), **container image scanning**, admission controllers, **runtime threat detection** (e.g., unexpected syscalls, network anomalies).
- Tools: Checkov, Terrascan, Trivy, Falco, OPA Gatekeeper.
- Output: Continuous compliance, anomaly alerts, automated rollback.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — SAST vs. DAST vs. IAST vs. SCA (7 marks)

| Criterion | **SAST** | **DAST** | **IAST** | **SCA** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Working principle (1.5 marks)** | Analyzes source code / bytecode without executing. Pattern-matches against known insecure constructs (taint analysis, data flow). | Simulates external attacks against a running app via HTTP. Crawls endpoints and fuzzes inputs. | Inserts an agent into the running app; observes data flow at runtime; correlates with code context. | Parses dependency manifests (package.json, pom.xml, requirements.txt) and cross-references against CVE databases. |
| **Advantages (1.5 marks)** | Finds bugs early; covers all code paths; no need to run the app. | Detects real-world exploitable issues; language-agnostic. | Very low false positive rate; full visibility. | Catches transitive (nested) dependencies; covers license compliance. |
| **Limitations (1.5 marks)** | High false positives; cannot find runtime/environment issues. | Blind to source-level issues; slow; needs a deployed app. | Requires instrumented runtime; language-specific agent needed. | Misses custom code; only catches *known* CVEs. |
| **Best use case (1.5 marks)** | Pre-commit and build-time checks. | Pre-release staging tests. | Comprehensive test environment. | Build-time dependency audit. |
| **Example tool (1 mark)** | SonarQube | OWASP ZAP | Contrast Security | Snyk Open Source |

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** What is **Software Bill of Materials (SBOM)**? Explain its role in securing the software supply chain, with reference to recent high-profile vulnerabilities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **automated security gates** in a CI/CD pipeline. Describe any **three common security gate policies** with examples. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — SBOM and Software Supply Chain (7 marks)

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A **Software Bill of Materials (SBOM)** is a formal, machine-readable inventory of all components, libraries, modules, and dependencies used to build a software artifact. Standard formats include **SPDX** (Linux Foundation) and **CycloneDX** (OWASP).

**Role in Supply Chain Security (4 marks):**
1. **Transparency:** When a new CVE is announced (e.g., Log4Shell — CVE-2021-44228 in Log4j), organizations can query their SBOMs in seconds to identify all affected systems, rather than manual grep across thousands of artifacts.
2. **Provenance verification:** Combined with cryptographic signing (Sigstore), SBOMs prove an artifact was built from a specific, unmodified dependency tree — defending against supply-chain attacks.
3. **Regulatory compliance:** U.S. Executive Order 14028 (May 2021) mandates SBOMs for federal software procurement. EU Cyber Resilience Act extends this to all commercial software.
4. **License compliance:** SBOMs enumerate open-source licenses, preventing inadvertent GPL contamination in proprietary products.

**Real-world case study (1.5 marks):** The **SolarWinds attack (2020)** demonstrated the cost of supply-chain compromise — attackers injected malicious code into Orion builds, affecting 18,000+ organizations including the U.S. Treasury. The post-incident response universally recommended **SBOM + artifact signing** as a mitigation. Similarly, **Log4j (CVE-2021-44228)** had a CVSS score of **10.0** and remained a top vulnerability for *years* in unscanned systems, simply because teams did not know they had Log4j in their dependency tree.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Automated Security Gates (7 marks)

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A **security gate** is an automated checkpoint in a CI/CD pipeline that evaluates an artifact against a policy. If the policy is violated, the pipeline is **halted** (hard gate) or a **warning is raised** (soft gate). Gates are implemented as code (Policy-as-Code) and are evaluated by tools like **Open Policy Agent (OPA)** or **Conftest**.

**Three Common Gate Policies (5.5 marks, ~1.8 each):**

**Gate 1 — Vulnerability Severity Threshold Policy**
- **Rule:** "Block merge if any SCA finding has CVSS ≥ 7.0."
- **Example (YAML in OPA Rego):**
  - Input: SBOM JSON output from Snyk.
  - Logic: `if any(dep.cvss >= 7.0) { fail }`
- **Rationale:** Prevents high-severity known CVEs from reaching production.

**Gate 2 — Container Image Compliance Policy**
- **Rule:** "Reject container images that run as root, use `:latest` tag, or have critical CVEs in base image."
- **Example tools:** Trivy, Anchore, OPA Gatekeeper for Kubernetes admission.
- **Rationale:** Reduces attack surface, enforces immutable tagging for traceability.

**Gate 3 — Secret & Configuration Leak Policy**
- **Rule:** "Block build if the diff contains patterns matching AWS keys, GitHub tokens, or private keys."
- **Example tools:** `gitleaks`, `trufflehog`, GitHub secret scanning.
- **Rationale:** Credentials in source code are the **#1 initial-access vector** in cloud breaches (per Verizon DBIR 2023).

**Bonus — Compliance-as-Code Gate:**
- Encode frameworks (PCI-DSS, HIPAA) as Rego rules and validate deployment manifests before release.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse SAST and DAST.** SAST = *static* (no execution, sees code). DAST = *dynamic* (running app, black-box). Mixing these up costs full marks.
> 2. **SBOM ≠ SCA.** SBOM is the *output inventory*; SCA is the *scanning process* that populates it. Students often use the terms interchangeably — examiners penalize this.
> 3. **Always mention automation.** A DevSecOps answer that talks about "manual security review" has misunderstood the question. The keyword is **automation, automation, automation**.
> 4. **Pipeline stages must be in order.** Plan → Code → Build → Test → Release → Deploy → Operate. Reversing Build and Test, or omitting Release, indicates surface-level preparation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 DevSecOps**

- **DevSecOps** = Security integrated as a **shared, automated, continuous** responsibility across the DevOps pipeline.
- The transition from **"Sec at the End"** to **"Sec from the Start"** is the **single most important conceptual shift** in the topic.
- **Shift-Left Security** = Move security earlier in SDLC; reduces cost and aligns with DevOps velocity.
- **Six pipeline stages** to remember verbatim: **Plan → Code → Build → Test → Release → Deploy/Operate.**
- **Tool taxonomy (must know cold):**
  - **SAST** → Source code, no execution → *SonarQube, Checkmarx*
  - **DAST** → Black-box, running app → *OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite*
  - **IAST** → Instrumented runtime agent → *Contrast Security*
  - **SCA** → Third-party dependencies → *Snyk, OWASP Dependency-Check*
  - **Container Scan** → Images → *Trivy, Clair, Anchore*
  - **IaC Scan** → Terraform/CloudFormation → *Checkov, Terrascan*
  - **Runtime Defense** → RASP/CWPP → *Falco, Aqua Security*
- **SBOM** = Software Bill of Materials; SPDX/CycloneDX formats; critical for supply-chain security (Log4j, SolarWinds).
- **Security as Code (SaC)** = Security policies written in machine-readable code (YAML/Rego).
- **Compliance as Code (CaC)** = Regulatory frameworks encoded as automated tests.
- **Key metrics:** MTTD, MTTR, Vulnerability Density, False Positive Rate, CVSS Score.
- **Composite Vulnerability Risk Index (CVRI)** formula: weighted sum of normalized Severity, Exploitability, Criticality, and Asset exposure.
- **Pipeline security gates** = automated checkpoints that hard-block or soft-warn based on policy violations.
- **Secret management** is *not optional*; use vaults (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) — never commit secrets to Git.
- **The "Airport Security" analogy** is a great exam-introduction hook to demonstrate conceptual understanding.
- **Shift-Left cost curve** ($C \propto k^n$) is the financial justification for DevSecOps.
- **Pipeline order is sacred** in KTU answers: Plan → Code → Build → Test → Release → Deploy → Operate. Memorize and reproduce in the same sequence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Custom Pipelines, Blue-Green Deployments, and Canary Releases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT742 - SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT WITH AGILE AND DEVOPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Custom Pipelines, Blue-Green Deployments, and Canary Releases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **DevOps engineering** and **Continuous Delivery (CD)**, a **Custom Pipeline** is a user-defined, parameterized sequence of automated build, test, and deployment stages that is engineered to satisfy the specific build, compliance, and quality-gate requirements of a particular software project or organization. Unlike the generic default pipelines offered by CI/CD platforms, custom pipelines allow fine-grained control over stage sequencing, parallel execution, conditional branching, artifact promotion, and integration with proprietary toolchains.

A **Blue-Green Deployment** is a zero-downtime release strategy in which two identical, isolated production-grade environments — conventionally labeled **Blue** (the current/live version) and **Green** (the new version) — are maintained in parallel. A **router** or **load balancer** is then atomically switched from the Blue environment to the Green environment, enabling instantaneous rollback if the new version exhibits failures.

A **Canary Release** (or **Canary Deployment**) is a progressive, risk-mitigated release strategy in which the new version of the software is incrementally exposed to a small, statistically representative subset of end-users (the "canary cohort"). Operational, performance, and business Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) are observed on this cohort. If the metrics remain within acceptable Service Level Objectives (SLOs), traffic is gradually shifted — typically in **10%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%** stages — until full rollout is achieved.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The course explicitly clusters these three concepts under *Advanced Release Engineering*. Examiners frequently frame 14-mark Part B questions around the comparative analysis of Blue-Green vs. Canary and the design of a custom pipeline integrating both.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Restaurant Renovation Model**
> Imagine a fully operational restaurant (the **Blue** environment) serving hundreds of diners every evening. The owner wants to introduce a new menu and a renovated kitchen (the **Green** environment).
>
> - **Custom Pipeline** = The owner's personalized *Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)*: a documented, repeatable recipe specifying exactly *how* ingredients must be sourced, prepped, cooked, plated, quality-checked, and delivered — custom-built for this restaurant's particular layout and suppliers.
> - **Blue-Green Deployment** = Building an *entirely duplicate, fully-equipped second kitchen* next door. The new menu is tested in the new kitchen for an entire evening with VIP invitees. At a single moment, the head waiter (load balancer) is told, "Tonight, send all customers to the new kitchen." If the new kitchen falters, the head waiter simply reverses the instruction in milliseconds — no customer ever knows.
> - **Canary Release** = A *soft launch*: the new menu is offered first to only **5% of tables** (the canary cohort, perhaps the regulars whose feedback is trusted). If the new dishes are received well and the kitchen does not catch fire, the proportion of tables receiving the new menu is steadily increased — **10% → 25% → 50% → 100%**. If a dish fails, only the small canary group is impacted, and the rollout is paused or rolled back.

### 1.3 Core Terminology & Quantitative Markers

The following standard engineering metrics govern the design and evaluation of these strategies:

- **SLO (Service Level Objective):** The internal target for a system metric, e.g., **99.9%** availability over a 30-day window.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Recovery):** Average time to restore service after an incident. **Blue-Green** excels here, often reducing MTTR to **< 60 seconds**.
- **Canary Cohort Size:** Typically **1% – 10%** of total user traffic for the initial stage.
- **Promotion Velocity:** The rate at which traffic percentage is increased between canary stages (e.g., **+15% per hour**).
- **Bake Time / Soak Time:** The duration (commonly **15 minutes – 4 hours**) the new version is observed at each traffic percentage before further promotion.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of a Custom Pipeline

A custom pipeline is modeled as a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** of stages, where each node is a discrete automation unit and each edge represents an artifact or signal flow. The architectural pillars are:

1. **Source Stage (Trigger)**
   - Listens for events: Git `push`, Pull Request `opened`, tag-based releases, scheduled cron triggers, or webhook callbacks.
   - Performs workspace checkout and dependency resolution.

2. **Build Stage**
   - Compiles source code, resolves transitive dependencies, and produces a versioned, immutable **artifact** (e.g., Docker image tagged with Git SHA, e.g., `myapp:7f3a2b1`).

3. **Test Stage(s) — Quality Gates**
   - **Static Analysis:** Linting, code-style enforcement, security scanning (e.g., SAST — Static Application Security Testing).
   - **Unit Tests:** Isolated, fast, deterministic (typical target coverage ≥ **80%**).
   - **Integration Tests:** Validates inter-service contracts.
   - **Performance / Load Tests:** Validates throughput, latency (p95, p99).
   - **Security Gates:** Container image vulnerability scanning.

4. **Artifact Promotion Stage**
   - The validated artifact is pushed to a central registry (e.g., Docker Hub, AWS ECR, JFrog Artifactory) with semantic versioning.

5. **Deployment Stage (Blue-Green or Canary)**
   - Executes the chosen release strategy against target environments (Dev → Staging → Production).

6. **Post-Deployment Verification**
   - Synthetic transactions, smoke tests, SLO dashboards, log anomaly detection.

7. **Notification & Feedback Stage**
   - Posts status back to collaboration tools (Slack, MS Teams) and to the Git commit status API.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a DAG?** Cycles in a pipeline would imply re-running indefinitely without convergence. The DAG constraint guarantees **terminology** and **deterministic replayability**, both of which are board-examination favorites.

### 2.2 Theoretical Basis of Blue-Green Deployment

The strategy is rooted in **Atomic Switch Theory**: a binary state transition (Blue ↔ Green) at the routing layer guarantees that at any instant in time, only one environment receives 100% of user traffic. This yields:

- **Zero-Downtime Releases:** DNS or load-balancer updates propagate in **< 1 second** with modern anycast networks.
- **Instantaneous Rollback:** Re-pointing the router to the prior environment is a metadata operation, not a redeployment.
- **Environment Parity:** The Green stack is an exact clone of Blue, eliminating the *it works on staging* paradox.

**Cost-Downside:** Doubled infrastructure footprint during deployment, plus database schema migration complications, since both environments typically share a single persistent data store.

### 2.3 Theoretical Basis of Canary Release

Canary is a **statistical hypothesis test applied to live traffic**. Let:

- $p_b$ = error rate / defect rate observed in the **baseline (Blue)** population.
- $p_c$ = error rate / defect rate observed in the **canary (Green)** cohort.

The canary is promoted only if a sequential statistical test (e.g., **Wald's Sequential Probability Ratio Test — SPRT**) determines with high confidence that:

$$p_c \le p_b + \delta_{tol}$$

where $\delta_{tol}$ is the **tolerable regression threshold** (commonly **0.1% – 0.5%**).

If the observed metric breaches this bound, the rollout is **aborted** and traffic is reverted to Blue.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Symbol / Formula** | **Boundary / Standard Value** | **Engineering Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Canary Traffic Fraction | $f_c = \dfrac{N_{canary}}{N_{total}}$ | $0.01 \le f_c \le 0.10$ (initial) | Cohort size in canary strategy |
| Error Rate Differential | $\Delta p = p_c - p_b$ | $\vert \Delta p \vert \le \delta_{tol}$ | Promotion gate condition |
| Availability SLO | $A = \dfrac{Uptime}{Uptime + Downtime}$ | $A \ge 0.999$ (3-nines) | Production readiness gate |
| MTTR (Blue-Green) | $MTTR_{BG} \approx t_{switch}$ | $\le 60\text{ s}$ | Rollback latency bound |
| Pipeline Stage Cardinality | $S = \vert\text{Stages}\vert$ | $5 \le S \le 12$ (typical) | Custom pipeline complexity |
| Promotion Velocity | $v = \dfrac{\Delta f_c}{\Delta t}$ | e.g., $+0.15/\text{hour}$ | Canary rollout pacing |
| Bake / Soak Time | $t_{soak}$ | $15 \text{ min} \le t_{soak} \le 4 \text{ h}$ | Per-stage observation window |
| Infrastructure Cost Multiplier (Blue-Green) | $C_{BG} \approx 2 \times C_{single}$ | $C_{BG} = 2 \cdot C_{base}$ | OpEx estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Note:** All absolute-value delimiters are rendered using `\vert` and `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity. Do not use the vertical pipe character `|` inside table cells.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These patterns are foundational in **microservice architectures**, **cloud-native SaaS platforms**, and **mobile app delivery pipelines**. Tech giants such as **Netflix (Spinnaker / Atlas)**, **Google (Borg / Spinnaker)**, **Amazon (AWS CodeDeploy)**, and **Facebook (Gatekeeper)** all implement variants of these strategies to deploy thousands of times per day with **< 0.01%** error budgets.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Canary Promotion Decision Boundary

**Problem Statement:** Given observed defect rates from a baseline population and a canary cohort, derive the formal promotion gate condition used by a sequential canary analysis engine.

**Given:**
- Sample size of canary cohort: $n_c$
- Sample size of baseline: $n_b$
- Observed defects in canary: $x_c$
- Observed defects in baseline: $x_b$
- Tolerable regression threshold: $\delta_{tol}$
- Statistical confidence requirement: $1 - \alpha$ (commonly $\alpha = 0.05$)

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

We begin with the maximum-likelihood estimator for the defect rate in each population:

$$
\hat{p}_b = \dfrac{x_b}{n_b}
$$

This is the standard unbiased estimator of a Bernoulli proportion, derived from the method of moments by equating the empirical mean to the parameter.

$$
\hat{p}_c = \dfrac{x_c}{n_c}
$$

Similarly, the canary defect rate is estimated as the empirical proportion of defects in the canary sample.

We now state the **Null Hypothesis** $H_0$ (safe to promote) and the **Alternative** $H_1$ (regress detected):

$$
H_0: \hat{p}_c - \hat{p}_b \le \delta_{tol}
$$

$$
H_1: \hat{p}_c - \hat{p}_b > \delta_{tol}
$$

The promotion engine **promotes** the canary only when $H_0$ cannot be rejected at significance level $\alpha$.

Under $H_0$, the difference $D = \hat{p}_c - \hat{p}_b$ is approximately normally distributed (Central Limit Theorem, valid when $n_b, n_c \ge 30$):

$$
D \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\delta_{tol},\ \sigma_D^{\,2}\right)
$$

The pooled variance of the difference of two independent proportions is computed as:

$$
\sigma_D^{\,2} = \dfrac{\hat{p}_b (1 - \hat{p}_b)}{n_b} + \dfrac{\hat{p}_c (1 - \hat{p}_c)}{n_c}
$$

This follows from the variance-addition property for independent random variables, applying the standard Bernoulli variance $\sigma^2 = p(1-p)/n$ to each estimator.

The Z-score quantifying the deviation of the observed difference from the null hypothesis boundary is:

$$
Z = \dfrac{(\hat{p}_c - \hat{p}_b) - \delta_{tol}}{\sigma_D}
$$

The promotion gate fires (rejects $H_0$, aborts rollout) when:

$$
Z > Z_{1-\alpha}
$$

For $\alpha = 0.05$ (one-tailed), $Z_{0.95} = 1.645$. Therefore, the **decision rule** becomes:

$$
\dfrac{(\hat{p}_c - \hat{p}_b) - \delta_{tol}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}_b (1 - \hat{p}_b)}{n_b} + \dfrac{\hat{p}_c (1 - \hat{p}_c)}{n_c}}} \le 1.645 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{PROMOTE}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the algebraic foundation of automated canary analysis (ACA) engines embedded in platforms like Spinnaker, Argo Rollouts, and LaunchDarkly. KTU examiners value the explicit statement of $H_0$ and $H_1$ for full marks.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Trade-offs

| **Dimension** | **Blue-Green Deployment** | **Canary Release** |
|---|---|---|
| Rollback Speed (MTTR) | Sub-60 seconds, near-instant | Slower (reverse traffic shift, ~minutes) |
| Infrastructure Cost | $2\times$ baseline (two full envs) | Marginal (small canary slice) |
| Risk Exposure | All-or-nothing switch | Gradually expanding cohort |
| Realistic Production Load Test | Full production traffic mirrored only after switch | Continuous production traffic on canary |
| Database Compatibility | Tricky (shared schema) | Easier (canary reads from shared DB) |
| Statistical Confidence | Low (binary event) | High (sequential hypothesis testing) |
| Suitable for Stateful Services | Difficult | Highly suitable |
| Rollback Granularity | Whole-version | Partial (cohort only) |
| Typical Use Case | Critical, low-tolerance releases | Frequent, incremental updates |
| Tooling Examples | AWS CodeDeploy, Azure Slots | Spinnaker, Argo Rollouts, Flagger |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A canary engine observes $x_c = 12$ defects in $n_c = 2000$ canary requests, while the baseline shows $x_b = 50$ defects in $n_b = 10000$ requests. The tolerable threshold is $\delta_{tol} = 0.005$ and $\alpha = 0.05$. Should the rollout be promoted?

**Solution:**

$$
\hat{p}_c = \dfrac{12}{2000} = 0.006
$$

$$
\hat{p}_b = \dfrac{50}{10000} = 0.005
$$

$$
\sigma_D^{\,2} = \dfrac{0.005 \cdot 0.995}{10000} + \dfrac{0.006 \cdot 0.994}{2000}
$$

$$
\sigma_D^{\,2} = 4.975 \times 10^{-7} + 2.982 \times 10^{-6} = 3.4795 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\sigma_D = \sqrt{3.4795 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 0.001865
$$

$$
Z = \dfrac{(0.006 - 0.005) - 0.005}{0.001865} = \dfrac{-0.004}{0.001865} \approx -2.144
$$

Since $Z = -2.144 \le 1.645$, the canary is **safe to promote**. The negative Z-score indicates the canary is statistically *better* than the regression threshold would tolerate, hence the rollout continues.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Custom Pipeline DAG

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[stepA: Source Trigger] --> B[stepB: Build Artifact]
    B --> C[stepC: Unit Test Gate]
    C --> D[stepD: SAST Security Scan]
    C --> E[stepE: Integration Test]
    D --> F[stepF: Container Image Build]
    E --> F
    F --> G[stepG: Push to Registry]
    G --> H[stepH: Deploy to Staging]
    H --> I[stepI: Smoke Verification]
    I --> J[stepJ: Promote to Production]
    J --> K[stepK: Canary Stage 5pct]
    K --> L[stepL: Canary Stage 25pct]
    L --> M[stepM: Canary Stage 50pct]
    M --> N[stepN: Canary Stage 100pct]
    N --> O[stepO: Post Deploy Monitoring]
    O --> P[stepP: Notification Slack]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Blue-Green Deployment Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    User[stepUser: End User] --> LB[stepLB: Load Balancer Router]
    LB -->|Active 100 percent| Blue[stepBlue: Blue Environment Current Version]
    LB -->|Idle Standby| Green[stepGreen: Green Environment New Version]
    Blue -.->|Deploy new version here| Green
    Green -->|Validate smoke test| Verify[stepVerify: Health Check Probe]
    Verify -->|Switch router atomically| Switch[stepSwitch: Traffic Flip Event]
    Switch --> LB
    Switch -->|Rollback trigger| Rollback[stepRollback: Revert to Blue]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Canary Release Progressive Traffic Shift

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[stepStart: Deploy New Build] --> Stage1[stepStage1: Canary 1 percent Traffic]
    Stage1 -->|SLO green soak 30 min| Stage2[stepStage2: Canary 10 percent Traffic]
    Stage2 -->|SLO green soak 30 min| Stage3[stepStage3: Canary 25 percent Traffic]
    Stage3 -->|SLO green soak 30 min| Stage4[stepStage4: Canary 50 percent Traffic]
    Stage4 -->|SLO green soak 30 min| Stage5[stepStage5: Canary 100 percent Rollout]
    Stage1 -->|SLO red abort| Abort[stepAbort: Automatic Rollback to Baseline]
    Stage2 -->|SLO red abort| Abort
    Stage3 -->|SLO red abort| Abort
    Stage4 -->|SLO red abort| Abort
    Abort --> RCA[stepRCA: Post Incident Analysis]
    Stage5 --> Monitor[stepMonitor: Continuous SLO Tracking]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hybrid Blue-Green + Canary

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GIT[Source Control Layer]
        Repo[stepRepo: Git Repository]
    end
    subgraph CI[Custom CI Pipeline Layer]
        Builder[stepBuilder: Build Runner]
        Tester[stepTester: Test Executor]
        Scanner[stepScanner: Security Scanner]
    end
    subgraph REG[Artifact Registry Layer]
        ImageStore[stepImageStore: Versioned Container Images]
    end
    subgraph CD[Advanced CD Strategy Layer]
        BlueEnv[stepBlueEnv: Blue Production Stack]
        GreenEnv[stepGreenEnv: Green New Version Stack]
        CanaryRouter[stepCanaryRouter: Progressive Traffic Shifter]
    end
    subgraph OBS[Observability Layer]
        Metrics[stepMetrics: Prometheus Telemetry]
        Logs[stepLogs: Centralized Log Aggregator]
        Alerts[stepAlerts: SLO Alertmanager]
    end
    Repo --> Builder
    Builder --> Tester
    Tester --> Scanner
    Scanner --> ImageStore
    ImageStore --> GreenEnv
    BlueEnv --> CanaryRouter
    GreenEnv --> CanaryRouter
    CanaryRouter --> Metrics
    Metrics --> Alerts
    CanaryRouter --> Logs
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node identifiers are alphanumeric and prefixed (e.g., `stepA`, `stepBlueEnv`), avoiding reserved keywords. All labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text, free of markdown emphasis tags.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
*Define a custom CI/CD pipeline. List any four stages that are commonly part of such a pipeline.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **custom CI/CD pipeline** is a project-specific, automated, end-to-end workflow that orchestrates the build, test, and deployment of software, customized to satisfy the unique build, compliance, and quality requirements of a given application or organization.

Four essential stages are:
1. **Source Stage** — triggers the pipeline on Git events such as `push` or `pull_request`.
2. **Build Stage** — compiles source code and produces an immutable, versioned artifact.
3. **Test Stage** — executes unit, integration, and security tests as quality gates.
4. **Deployment Stage** — promotes the validated artifact to the target environment (Dev / Staging / Production).

*(Full 3 marks awarded for definition + any four stages with one-line descriptions.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
*Differentiate between Blue-Green Deployment and Canary Release in terms of traffic switching and risk exposure.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Dimension** | **Blue-Green** | **Canary** |
|---|---|---|
| **Traffic Switching** | Atomic 100% flip via load balancer | Gradual progressive shift in stages |
| **Risk Exposure** | All-or-nothing; if the new version is buggy, all users are affected post-switch | Low; only a small cohort is exposed initially |

*(3 marks: 1 for each dimension explained + 1 for the conclusion/synthesis line.)*

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Blue-Green Deployment** strategy. Discuss its **advantages** and **limitations** in a cloud-native microservices environment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **custom CI/CD pipeline** for a containerized microservices application. Identify and justify **at least five stages**, and explain how automated quality gates are enforced between stages. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Blue-Green Deployment — 7 marks**

**Definition (1 mark):** Blue-Green Deployment is a release strategy that maintains two identical, parallel production environments — *Blue* (current live version) and *Green* (new version). A router or load balancer directs all production traffic to one environment at a time.

**Working Mechanism (3 marks):**
1. The current production version runs in the **Blue** environment, serving 100% of user traffic.
2. The new version is deployed to the **Green** environment, which is **idle** with respect to user traffic.
3. A **smoke test** and health-check probe is executed against Green.
4. Upon successful validation, the **load balancer** is reconfigured to route 100% of traffic from Blue to Green in an **atomic** operation.
5. The Blue environment is retained in a warm-standby state for **instantaneous rollback**.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.2 Blue-Green Deployment Flow.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Zero-downtime deployments.
- Sub-60-second rollback latency.
- Eliminates staging-vs-production drift by ensuring environment parity.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Doubled infrastructure cost during deployment.
- Database schema migration challenges (both environments share the same datastore).

---

**(b) Custom Pipeline Design — 7 marks**

A **five-stage custom pipeline** for containerized microservices:

1. **Source Stage (1 mark):** Triggered by Git `push` events; checks out code and resolves dependencies.
2. **Build Stage (1 mark):** Compiles code and produces a Docker image tagged with the Git commit SHA (e.g., `myapp:7f3a2b1`).
3. **Test Stage (2 marks):** Executes **unit tests** (≥ 80% coverage gate), **integration tests** (contract validation), and **SAST** (security gate). A failure in any test halts the pipeline.
4. **Artifact Promotion Stage (1 mark):** The validated image is pushed to a central registry (AWS ECR / Docker Hub) with semantic versioning.
5. **Deployment Stage (2 marks):** Executes a **Canary Release** — rolling out to 5% → 25% → 50% → 100% with SLO-based auto-promotion and auto-abort. Post-deployment synthetic monitoring verifies SLO compliance.

**Quality-Gate Enforcement:** Each stage is gated by a **pass/fail rule**. Failure halts downstream progression and triggers a Slack/email alert, ensuring that no unverified artifact ever reaches production.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **Canary Release** strategy with a suitable diagram. Derive the **statistical promotion-gate condition** using the hypothesis-testing framework. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Blue-Green Deployment** and **Canary Release** across at least **six parameters**, and recommend the optimal strategy for the following scenarios: **(i)** a banking transaction service requiring **zero-downtime** and **instant rollback**, **(ii)** a social media feed requiring **frequent updates** with **statistical risk mitigation**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Canary Release & Statistical Derivation — 7 marks**

**Definition (1 mark):** Canary Release is a progressive rollout strategy where the new version is exposed to a small, statistically representative subset of users (typically **1%–10%**) and gradually expanded to 100% if SLOs hold.

**Working (2 marks):** Stages: 1% → 10% → 25% → 50% → 100%, with a **soak time** (15 min – 4 h) at each stage for observation. Metrics include error rate, latency (p95, p99), CPU/memory utilization, and business KPIs.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.3 Canary Release Progressive Traffic Shift.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
Let $\hat{p}_c = x_c / n_c$ (canary defect rate) and $\hat{p}_b = x_b / n_b$ (baseline defect rate). The hypotheses are:

$$
H_0: \hat{p}_c - \hat{p}_b \le \delta_{tol} \quad \text{(promote)}
$$

$$
H_1: \hat{p}_c - \hat{p}_b > \delta_{tol} \quad \text{(abort)}
$$

The Z-test statistic is:

$$
Z = \dfrac{(\hat{p}_c - \hat{p}_b) - \delta_{tol}}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}_b (1 - \hat{p}_b)}{n_b} + \dfrac{\hat{p}_c (1 - \hat{p}_c)}{n_c}}}
$$

The canary is **promoted** iff $Z \le Z_{1-\alpha}$ (where $Z_{0.95} = 1.645$ for $\alpha = 0.05$); otherwise, the rollout is **aborted**.

---

**(b) Comparative Analysis & Recommendations — 7 marks**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Blue-Green** | **Canary** |
|---|---|---|
| Rollback Speed | < 60 s | Minutes (reverse traffic) |
| Cost | $2 \times$ infrastructure | Marginal slice |
| Risk Exposure | All-or-nothing | Gradual, low |
| Statistical Validation | None | SPRT / hypothesis testing |
| Database Compatibility | Tricky (shared schema) | Easier |
| Use Case Frequency | Rare, high-stakes releases | Frequent, incremental |

**Recommendations (4 marks):**
- **(i) Banking Transaction Service → Blue-Green (2 marks):** Banking systems demand zero-downtime, sub-second rollback, and full environment parity. The **MTTR advantage** of Blue-Green (< 60 s) is mission-critical. Cost is a non-issue compared to regulatory penalties.
- **(ii) Social Media Feed → Canary (2 marks):** Frequent feature updates benefit from canary's low-risk incremental exposure, real-user SLO validation, and statistical confidence. The marginal infrastructure cost is well-suited to high-velocity deployment cadence.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
